= number of clients | $L_s$ must be $\leq$ Server Capacity $C_s$, else congestion | | Server Capacity Limit (Hard Bound) | $N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_s}{\bar{R}} \right\rfloor$ | Maximum number of clients supported given average request rate $\bar{R}$ | $N_{max}$ is the hard saturation point | | P2P Aggregate Throughput | $T_{p2p} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i \cdot B_i$ | $u_i$ = upload bandwidth of peer $i$, $B_i$ = bottleneck bandwidth factor | Theoretical upper bound: $T_{p2p} \approx N \cdot B_{avg}$ | | BitTorrent Piece Count | $P = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_f}{S_p} \right\rceil$ | $P$ = number of pieces, $S_f$ = file size (bytes), $S_p$ = piece size (bytes) | Last piece may be smaller than $S_p$ | | DHT Lookup Complexity | $T_{lookup} = O(\log_2 N)$ | Time to find a peer in a Chord/Kademlia DHT with $N$ nodes | $N$ = total peers in the DHT ring | | Gnutella Flooding (Pure P2P) | $M_{TT L=k} = (D-1) \cdot D^{k-1}$ | Number of messages at hop $k$ with avg. degree $D$ (TTL = Time-to-Live) | $M$ grows exponentially; TTL typically $\leq 7$ | | Mean Hop Count (Random Graph) | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{\log N}{\log \bar{D}}$ | Average hops in a random P2P overlay, $\bar{D}$ = mean peer degree | Valid for $N \gg 1$ and uniform random topology | | Client-Server Hop Count (Worst Case) | $h_{cs} = 2$ | Client → Server → Client (exactly 2 application-layer hops) | Independent of $N$ | | Amdahl's P2P Scalability Bound | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1 - f_s}{N}}$ | $f_s$ = fraction of work that must remain centralized (e.g., tracker) | As $N \to \infty$, $S \to \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | | TCP Round-Trip Time | $RTT = 2 \cdot t_p + t_{prop} + t_{proc}$ | $t_p$ = transmission delay, $t_{prop}$ = propagation delay, $t_{proc}$ = processing delay | Negligible for LAN, dominant for GEO satellite links | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Heuristic Tip:** Questions asking "Compare scalability of C/S vs P2P" almost always want you to: > (a) Plot or describe the **load curve** of the server as $N$ increases (linear saturation in C/S). > (b) Show that P2P aggregate throughput scales roughly **linearly with $N$**. > (c) Reference the **Amdahl's Law** bound for hybrid P2P. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Server Saturation Point in Client-Server Architecture We derive the **maximum number of clients** a server can sustain before request queueing latency becomes unacceptable. **Step 1 — Define the variables.** Let: - $C_s$ = total CPU/network capacity of the server in **requests per second (rps)**. - $\bar{R}$ = average request rate per client in **rps** (assume uniform across clients). - $N$ = total number of active clients. - $\rho$ = server utilization ratio, $0 \leq \rho < 1$ (use $\rho = 0.8$ for safe planning). **Step 2 — Write the load equation.** The total demand placed on the server is the sum of all client request rates: $$ L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i $$ For uniform clients, $R_i = \bar{R}$, so: $$ L_{total} = N \cdot \bar{R} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the capacity constraint.** The server remains stable only if its utilization $\rho = \dfrac{L_{total}}{C_s} < 1$: $$ N \cdot \bar{R} < C_s $$ Solving for $N$: $$ N < \dfrac{C_s}{\bar{R}} $$ **Step 4 — Introduce the practical safety margin.** KTU board examinations typically expect the **practical saturation point** at $\rho = 0.8$: $$ N_{max} = \left\lfloor 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{\bar{R}} \right\rfloor $$ **Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style).** A web server has capacity $C_s = 10{,}000$ **rps**. Each client issues on average $\bar{R} = 0.5$ **rps**. $$ N_{max} = 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.5} = 0.8 \cdot 20{,}000 = 16{,}000 \text{ clients} $$ **Conclusion:** The server can handle a hard ceiling of $\mathbf{16{,}000}$ concurrent clients beyond which request latency grows unbounded (queueing theory M/M/1 result). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aggregate Throughput Scaling in P2P (BitTorrent Model) We derive the aggregate download throughput of a P2P swarm as a function of peer count $N$. **Step 1 — Define the per-peer capacity.** For each peer $i$: - $u_i$ = upload bandwidth (bits/sec). - $d_i$ = download bandwidth (bits/sec). - The bottleneck is $\min(u_i, d_i)$. **Step 2 — Express the aggregate upload capacity.** The total upload bandwidth available in the swarm is: $$ U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i $$ **Step 3 — Express the aggregate download capacity (ideal case).** Assuming every peer wants to download the same file (a "flash crowd" scenario), the total demand is $N$ copies of the file, but the limiting factor is **upload supply**, not demand. $$ T_{aggregate} = U_{total} = N \cdot \bar{u} $$ where $\bar{u}$ is the average upload bandwidth per peer. **Step 4 — Apply the **Tit-for-Tat** constraint.** In BitTorrent, peers preferentially upload to peers who reciprocate. Empirically, a **leeching-to-seeding ratio** $\alpha$ applies. The effective throughput is: $$ T_{effective} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot \bar{u}, \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1 $$ Empirically, BitTorrent studies report $\alpha \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ for healthy swarms. **Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example.** A swarm of $N = 1000$ peers, each with $\bar{u} = 256$ **kbps** upload, $\alpha = 0.7$: $$ T_{effective} = 0.7 \cdot 1000 \cdot 256 = 179{,}200 \text{ kbps} \approx 175 \text{ Mbps} $$ If the same file were served by a single C/S system with $C_s = 175$ **Mbps**, it would saturate at $N_{max} = 175 \text{ Mbps} / 0.5 \text{ Mbps per client} = 350$ clients. The P2P swarm thus supports **~2.85× more concurrent clients** at the same per-peer quality. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DHT Lookup Hop Count in Structured P2P (Chord) **Step 1 — Chord's structure.** Chord arranges $N$ peers in a **ring** modulo $2^m$ (where $m = 160$ for SHA-1 identifiers). Each peer maintains a **finger table** of $m$ entries pointing to peers at distances $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^{m-1}$ ahead in the ring. **Step 2 — Expected hop count.** Each lookup roughly halves the distance to the target (in identifier space), so: $$ h_{Chord} = O(\log_2 N) $$ **Step 3 — Exact expected value.** The expected number of hops in Chord is given by: $$ \mathbb{E}[h] \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \log_2 N $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example.** For $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ peers (e.g., a global DHT): $$ \mathbb{E}[h] = \dfrac{1}{2} \log_2(10^6) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 19.93 \approx 10 \text{ hops} $$ Even with **1 million peers**, a lookup takes only **~10 hops** — comparable to the **2 hops** of C/S but without the central bottleneck. This is the key insight behind IPFS and Ethereum's discovery layer. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — C/S vs P2P > [!NOTE] > The following matrix is the **single most-tested comparison** in KTU ESE Module 1. Memorize all 8 rows. | **Parameter** | **Client-Server** | **Peer-to-Peer** | |---|---|---| | **Topology** | Centralized (star) | Distributed (mesh / ring / tree) | | **Roles** | Asymmetric (server vs client) | Symmetric (every peer is both) | | **Scalability** | Limited by server capacity $C_s$ | Scales with peer count $N$ | | **Single Point of Failure** | **Yes** (server) | **No** (graceful degradation) | | **Resource Discovery** | DNS / service registry | DHT lookup / tracker / flooding | | **Security Model** | Centralized (PKI, ACLs) | Trustless (cryptographic proofs, reputation) | | **Lookup Latency** | $O(1)$ to $O(2)$ hops | $O(\log_2 N)$ hops | | **Administrative Cost** | High (server maintenance) | Low (organic) | | **Examples** | Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), DNS | BitTorrent, Bitcoin, IPFS | | **Best Use Case** | Centralized data, regulated workflows | Content distribution, censorship resistance | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Client-Server Architecture (Star Topology) ```mermaid graph TD C1[Client 1
Browser App]:::client C2[Client 2
Mobile App]:::client C3[Client 3
Desktop App]:::client C4[Client 4
IoT Device]:::client C5[Client 5
CLI Tool]:::client S1[Central Server
Application Logic]:::server DB1[(Database
Persistent Storage)]:::db FS1[(File System
Static Assets)]:::db LB1[Load Balancer
HAProxy / Nginx]:::lb FW1[Firewall
Security Layer]:::fw C1 -->|HTTP Request :80| FW1 C2 -->|HTTPS Request :443| FW1 C3 -->|REST API Call| FW1 C4 -->|MQTT :8883| FW1 C5 -->|SQL :3306| FW1 FW1 --> LB1 LB1 --> S1 S1 --> DB1 S1 --> FS1 classDef client fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef server fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef db fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lb fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fw fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** Five heterogeneous clients (browser, mobile, desktop, IoT, CLI) communicate with a **central server** protected by a **firewall** and fronted by a **load balancer**. The server reads/writes to a **database** and serves **static files**. This is the canonical 3-tier enterprise architecture. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Pure Peer-to-Peer Mesh Topology ```mermaid graph TD P1[Peer A
Has pieces 1,2,3]:::peer P2[Peer B
Has pieces 2,4,5]:::peer P3[Peer C
Has pieces 1,3,6]:::peer P4[Peer D
Has pieces 4,5,6]:::peer P5[Peer E
Has pieces 1,2,5]:::peer P6[Peer F
Has pieces 3,4,6]:::peer P1 ---|TCP| P2 P1 ---|TCP| P3 P2 ---|TCP| P4 P2 ---|TCP| P5 P3 ---|TCP| P5 P3 ---|TCP| P6 P4 ---|TCP| P6 P5 ---|TCP| P6 P1 ---|TCP| P6 P2 ---|TCP| P3 classDef peer fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c63a4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** Six peers arranged in a **mesh** with no central coordinator. Each peer holds a **subset of the file's pieces** and exchanges them with neighbors. Notice that **Peer A** is directly connected to Peer C and Peer F, providing multiple disjoint paths — illustrating P2P's **redundancy** and **fault tolerance**. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hybrid P2P with Central Tracker ```mermaid graph LR T1[Central Tracker
Peer Registry]:::tracker P1[Peer 1
Leech + Seed]:::peer P2[Peer 2
Leech + Seed]:::peer P3[Peer 3
Leech]:::peer P4[Peer 4
Seed Only]:::peer P5[Peer 5
Leech]:::peer P1 -->|1. Announce / Scrape| T1 P2 -->|1. Announce / Scrape| T1 P3 -->|1. Announce / Scrape| T1 P4 -->|1. Announce / Scrape| T1 P5 -->|1. Announce / Scrape| T1 T1 -->|2. Returns Peer List| P1 T1 -->|2. Returns Peer List| P2 T1 -->|2. Returns Peer List| P3 P1 <-->|3. P2P Piece Transfer| P2 P1 <-->|3. P2P Piece Transfer| P4 P2 <-->|3. P2P Piece Transfer| P3 P2 <-->|3. P2P Piece Transfer| P5 P3 <-->|3. P2P Piece Transfer| P4 P4 <-->|3. P2P Piece Transfer| P5 classDef peer fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c63a4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tracker fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** A **central tracker** maintains a registry of active peers. Peers query the tracker (step 1–2) to discover each other, then exchange file pieces **directly peer-to-peer** (step 3). This is the **BitTorrent hybrid P2P model** — combines the discovery simplicity of C/S with the bandwidth efficiency of P2P. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Comparison) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CS_BLOCK[CLIENT SERVER ARCHITECTURE FLOW] CS1[Client Initiates Request] --> CS2[DNS Resolves Server IP] CS2 --> CS3[TCP Handshake with Server] CS3 --> CS4[Server Processes Request] CS4 --> CS5[Server Queries Database] CS5 --> CS6[Server Constructs Response] CS6 --> CS7[Response Sent to Client] end subgraph P2P_BLOCK[PEER TO PEER ARCHITECTURE FLOW] P1[Peer Joins Network] --> P2[Discover Peers via Tracker or DHT] P2 --> P3[Establish Direct Peer Connections] P3 --> P4[Negotiate Available Pieces] P4 --> P5[Exchange Pieces with Multiple Peers] P5 --> P6[Verify Integrity via Hash] P6 --> P7[Assemble Complete File] end classDef csStep fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef p2pStep fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c63a4,color:#000 class CS1,CS2,CS3,CS4,CS5,CS6,CS7 csStep class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 p2pStep ``` **Description:** Side-by-side functional flow comparison. The C/S path has a **linear, server-centric** sequence (7 steps all mediated by the server). The P2P path is **distributed and parallel** — multiple peers exchange pieces simultaneously in step P5, dramatically improving aggregate throughput. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Differentiate between Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer architectures with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Basis** | **Client-Server** | **Peer-to-Peer** | |---|---|---| | **Role Definition** | Dedicated server serves multiple clients | Each node is both client and server | | **Examples** | Web (HTTP), Email, DNS | BitTorrent, Bitcoin, Gnutella | | **Central Server** | Mandatory | Optional (pure vs hybrid) | Client-Server uses a **centralized server** to provide services (e.g., a web server hosting a website), while in P2P, **every peer** is autonomous and shares resources directly (e.g., BitTorrent file sharing). *(1.5 marks for each architecture + 0.5 for examples & key distinction.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **List any THREE advantages and TWO disadvantages of Peer-to-Peer architecture.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages (any 3, 0.5 mark each):** 1. **High scalability** — capacity grows with peer count. 2. **No single point of failure** — robust against individual peer crashes. 3. **Cost-effective** — no expensive central infrastructure. 4. **Resource aggregation** — leverages idle bandwidth/storage of millions of peers. **Disadvantages (any 2, 0.75 mark each):** 1. **Security challenges** — no central authority to enforce policy; vulnerable to **malware propagation** and **sybil attacks**. 2. **Quality of Service (QoS) variability** — peer availability and bandwidth are unpredictable. 3. **Complex peer discovery** — requires DHTs, trackers, or flooding (which wastes bandwidth). --- --- ## 📗 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > For 14-mark questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by: > 1. Writing a "definition-only" answer without **architecture diagrams** (KTU requires at least **one labeled diagram** for full marks). > 2. Confusing **TCP/IP layers** (e.g., writing "P2P uses UDP" without qualifying with specific use cases like BitTorrent using both). > 3. **Forgetting to give real-world examples** — examiners explicitly look for "Name 2 real-world systems." > 4. Writing comparisons as **paragraphs** instead of **tables** — tables score better in KTU valuation. > 5. Not mentioning **scalability equations** when asked about "performance comparison" (always reference $O(\log N)$ vs $O(1)$ with caveat about server bottleneck). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **(a)** Explain the **Client-Server architecture** in detail. Discuss its **components, working principle, and key characteristics**. Provide suitable **real-world examples** and a **labeled diagram**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss **three types of P2P architectures** (Pure, Hybrid, Structured) with examples. Explain how a **BitTorrent-based P2P file sharing system** works, including the role of the **tracker**, **torrent file**, and **piece verification mechanism**. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Client-Server Architecture (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Client-Server architecture is a distributed computing model where a centralized **server** provides resources, services, or data to multiple **client** machines over a network, using a **request-response** communication pattern. **Key Components (2 marks):** 1. **Client** — End-user device/application that initiates requests (e.g., web browser, mobile app). 2. **Server** — Powerful machine that hosts services, applications, and data (e.g., Apache HTTP Server, MySQL DBMS). 3. **Network** — The communication medium (LAN, WAN, Internet) using protocols like **TCP/IP, HTTP/HTTPS**. 4. **Middleware** (optional) — Software that mediates between client requests and server resources. 5. **Database / Storage** — Persistent data storage backend. **Working Principle (2 marks):** 1. The server **binds** to a well-known **port** (e.g., 80, 443) and **listens** for incoming connections. 2. The client opens a **TCP connection** via a 3-way handshake (**SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK**). 3. The client sends an **HTTP request** (e.g., `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`). 4. The server processes the request, accesses the database/filesystem, and constructs an **HTTP response** with status code (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error). 5. The response is sent back to the client; the connection is closed or kept alive (HTTP/1.1). **Real-World Examples (1 mark):** - **Web**: Browser ↔ Apache/Nginx server - **Email**: Outlook ↔ Gmail SMTP/POP3 servers - **DNS**: Resolver ↔ Root/TLD/Authoritative DNS servers - **Database**: Application ↔ MySQL/PostgreSQL server **Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — P2P Architectures and BitTorrent (7 marks)** **Three Types of P2P (3 marks):** 1. **Pure P2P** — No central server. Peers use **flooding** (limited TTL) or **DHTs** for discovery. *Example*: Early Gnutella (2000). 2. **Hybrid P2P** — Lightweight **central tracker** for peer discovery; data transfer is P2P. *Example*: BitTorrent (with `.torrent` + tracker), Skype (early versions). 3. **Structured P2P** — Peers organized in a **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** for $O(\log N)$ lookups. *Examples*: Chord, Kademlia, IPFS, Ethereum's discovery layer. **BitTorrent Workflow (3 marks):** 1. **Torrent File Acquisition** — User downloads a `.torrent` file or uses a **magnet link** (contains SHA-1 infohash). 2. **Tracker Contact** — The BitTorrent client contacts the **tracker** (HTTP/HTTPS UDP-tracker) to get a list of peers currently sharing the file. 3. **Peer Handshake** — Client performs the **BitTorrent peer protocol handshake** (infohash exchange, peer ID). 4. **Piece Exchange** — File is split into **pieces** (typically 256 KB). Client downloads from multiple peers in parallel using a **rarest-first** strategy. 5. **Tit-for-Tat Choking** — Peers preferentially upload to those who reciprocate, preventing free-riders. 6. **Hash Verification** — Each piece is verified against its **SHA-1 hash** stored in the torrent's info dictionary. 7. **Endgame Mode** — Final pieces are requested from all peers simultaneously. **Role of Tracker (0.5 mark):** The tracker does **not** host the file; it only maintains a **registry of active peers** and their IP/port/availability. It enables peers to find each other but is not a bottleneck for data transfer. **Piece Verification (0.5 mark):** After each piece is downloaded, the client computes the **SHA-1 hash** of the piece and compares it with the hash in the **metainfo** section of the torrent. Mismatches cause the piece to be **discarded and redownloaded**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer architectures** across at least **six parameters**. Use a **comparison table** and justify which is more suitable for **(i) a banking application** and **(ii) a software distribution platform**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **scalability challenges** in Client-Server architecture. Derive the **maximum number of clients** a server with capacity $C_s = 5000$ requests/second can support, given an average request rate of $\bar{R} = 2$ rps per client, using a utilization factor of $\rho = 0.8$. Also explain **two techniques** to overcome scalability bottlenecks. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparison and Suitability (7 marks)** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Client-Server** | **Peer-to-Peer** | |---|---|---| | Topology | Centralized (Star) | Distributed (Mesh/Ring) | | Role Asymmetry | Server ≠ Client | All peers equal | | Scalability | Limited (server-bound) | High (organic growth) | | Single Point of Failure | Yes | No | | Security | Centralized (PKI, ACL) | Decentralized (cryptographic) | | Lookup Performance | $O(1)$ via direct IP | $O(\log N)$ via DHT | | Cost | High (server infrastructure) | Low (shared resources) | | Examples | Web, Email, Banking | BitTorrent, Bitcoin | **Suitability Analysis (4 marks):** **(i) Banking Application → Client-Server** *(2 marks)* A banking system demands: - **Centralized control** for regulatory compliance (RBI, KYC norms). - **Strong authentication** (OAuth 2.0, mTLS, hardware tokens). - **Atomic transactions** (ACID guarantees from RDBMS). - **Audit logging** and forensic traceability. - **No tolerance for data inconsistency** — P2P eventual consistency is unacceptable. Hence, a **client-server** architecture (often with a **3-tier** web + **mainframe** backend) is mandatory. **(ii) Software Distribution Platform → P2P (Hybrid)** *(2 marks)* Distributing a 50 GB software ISO to millions of users would crush any single server. **BitTorrent-based P2P**: - **Aggregates bandwidth** from millions of seeders. - **Reduces CDN costs** dramatically (Steam, Ubuntu, Debian, Arch Linux all use BitTorrent for ISO distribution). - **Resilient** to seeders leaving (other peers can continue). Hence, a **hybrid P2P** (with a tracker for .torrent discovery, but P2P for actual data transfer) is the ideal choice. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Scalability and Numerical Derivation (7 marks)** **Scalability Challenges in C/S (2 marks):** 1. **Server Saturation** — As $N$ increases, the server's CPU, memory, and network bandwidth are exhausted. Beyond the **knee point**, response latency grows exponentially (M/M/1 queueing). 2. **Single Point of Failure** — Server crash = total service outage. 3. **Bandwidth Bottleneck** — The server's uplink (e.g., 1 Gbps) becomes a hard ceiling. 4. **Database Contention** — Concurrent reads/writes cause lock contention and slow queries. 5. **State Management** — Maintaining session state for millions of clients strains memory. **Mathematical Derivation (3 marks):** **Given:** - Server capacity: $C_s = 5000$ **rps** (requests per second) - Average request rate per client: $\bar{R} = 2$ **rps** - Utilization factor: $\rho = 0.8$ **Step 1:** Write the load equation. $$ L_{total} = N \cdot \bar{R} $$ **Step 2:** Apply capacity constraint with utilization. $$ \rho = \dfrac{L_{total}}{C_s} = \dfrac{N \cdot \bar{R}}{C_s} \leq 0.8 $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $N$. $$ N \leq \dfrac{0.8 \cdot C_s}{\bar{R}} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute numerical values. $$ N \leq \dfrac{0.8 \cdot 5000}{2} = \dfrac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ clients} $$ **Conclusion:** The server can sustain a maximum of $\mathbf{N_{max} = 2000}$ concurrent clients under the given load profile. **Techniques to Overcome Scalability Bottlenecks (2 marks):** 1. **Horizontal Scaling (Load Balancing)** — Distribute incoming requests across multiple server replicas using a **load balancer** (e.g., HAProxy, NGINX, AWS ELB). Effective capacity scales linearly: $C_{total} = k \cdot C_s$ for $k$ replicas. 2. **Caching (Redis, Memcached, CDN)** — Cache frequently-accessed resources (e.g., static HTML, images) at edge servers (CDN) or in-memory (Redis). Cache hit ratio $\eta$ reduces server load by factor $(1 - \eta)$: $$ L_{effective} = (1 - \eta) \cdot L_{total} $$ For $\eta = 0.9$, the effective load drops to **10%** of original, multiplying capacity by ~10×. 3. **Database Sharding / Replication** — Split a monolithic database into **shards** (horizontal partitioning) or use **read replicas** to offload read traffic. 4. **Microservices Architecture** — Decompose the monolith into independently scalable services, each with its own database. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 1** - ✅ **Client-Server** = asymmetric roles, centralized, request-response, single point of failure. - ✅ **P2P** = symmetric roles, distributed, scalable, no central authority. - ✅ **Three P2P flavors**: Pure (no server), Hybrid (tracker), Structured (DHT, $O(\log N)$ lookups). - ✅ **Standard ports to memorize**: HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**, FTP = **21**, SSH = **22**, SMTP = **25**, DNS = **53**. - ✅ **BitTorrent piece size** = typically **256 KB** to **1 MB**; verified by **SHA-1** hash from the torrent's `info` dictionary. - ✅ **BitTorrent rarest-first** strategy ensures **piece diversity** in the swarm. - ✅ **Tit-for-Tat** choking prevents free-riding; peers reciprocate uploads. - ✅ **Chord DHT** lookup complexity = $O(\log_2 N)$ hops, with expected value $\approx \dfrac{1}{2} \log_2 N$. - ✅ **Gnutella flooding** uses **TTL-limited broadcast**; messages grow as $(D-1) \cdot D^{k-1}$ at hop $k$. - ✅ **Server saturation formula**: $N_{max} = \left\lfloor \rho \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{\bar{R}} \right\rfloor$, with $\rho = 0.8$ as the safe margin. - ✅ **P2P aggregate throughput** scales as $T_{aggregate} \approx \alpha \cdot N \cdot \bar{u}$, where $\alpha$ is the leech-to-seed efficiency. - ✅ **Amdahl's Law** for hybrid P2P: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1 - f_s}{N}}$, bound $\dfrac{1}{f_s}$ as $N \to \infty$. - ✅ **Banking/government** → Client-Server (regulatory, ACID, audit). - ✅ **Software ISO distribution** → Hybrid P2P (BitTorrent, used by Ubuntu, Debian, Arch). - ✅ **Censorship-resistant publishing** → Pure P2P / DHT (IPFS, Freenet). - ✅ **Examples to ALWAYS cite**: BitTorrent (P2P file sharing), Bitcoin (P2P currency), Web (C/S), Email (C/S), IPFS (Structured P2P), DNS (C/S hierarchical). - ✅ **C/S lookup** = $O(1)$ to $O(2)$ hops; **P2P DHT lookup** = $O(\log_2 N)$ hops — but C/S has the bottleneck issue. - ✅ **Always include a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers (worth 1 mark minimum). - ✅ **Always use a comparison table** when asked to "compare and contrast" — tables score higher than prose in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Design Issues for the Layers: Interface, Service, and Service Primitives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Issues for the Layers: Interface, Service, and Service Primitives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Layered Architecture Paradigm

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme module on **Computer Networks Fundamentals**, computer networks are inherently complex systems involving heterogeneous hardware, diverse transmission media, and millions of communicating endpoints. To manage this complexity, network architects employ a **Layered Architecture** — a hierarchical design philosophy in which the network communication process is decomposed into a stack of discrete, manageable sub-problems, each handled by a separate functional layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Layered Architecture):**
> A *layered architecture* is a design methodology in which a complex communication system is partitioned into a vertical stack of layers, where each layer **n** provides services to the layer above it (layer **n+1**) while requesting services from the layer below it (layer **n-1**). The ISO-OSI model and the TCP/IP model are the canonical implementations of this concept.

The **3 Pillars of Layered Design** — *Service*, *Interface*, and *Protocol* — form the conceptual backbone of this topic and are recurring high-yield questions in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.2 Service — The "What"

**Service** refers to *what a layer provides* to the layer immediately above it. It defines the set of operations and capabilities that an **n-layer** offers to an **(n+1)-layer** entity, abstracting away the internal mechanics of *how* those operations are implemented.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Tanenbaum — KTU Standard Reference):**
> A *service* is the set of capabilities that one layer offers to the layer above it, defined in terms of the **primitive operations** the upper layer can invoke, together with the **semantics** of those operations. Service is defined at a **Service Access Point (SAP)** and exists only at the **logical** level between adjacent layers.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a customer in a fine-dining restaurant:
- The **customer** (upper layer) wants food but is unaware of how it's prepared.
- The **kitchen** (lower layer) prepares the food using internal recipes, stoves, and ingredients.
- The **menu** (interface) lists what the customer can order.
- The **served dish** (service) is what the customer ultimately receives.

The customer neither knows *how* the dish is cooked, nor has direct access to the kitchen's internal operations. The *menu* abstracts the *service* — this is precisely how **interface** and **service** are decoupled from **protocol** in networking.

---

## 1.3 Interface — The "How to Request"

**Interface** defines *how the upper layer accesses the service*. It is the precise set of rules, parameters, and invocation conventions that the upper layer must obey to obtain a particular service from the layer below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> An *interface* is the boundary specification between two adjacent layers that defines which **service primitives** the upper layer can call, what **parameters** must accompany each call, and what **results** are returned. The interface is typically implemented as a set of function calls or API endpoints.

### Key Property of a Well-Designed Interface

- The interface should be **clean and minimal** (Information Hiding Principle).
- The internal implementation of layer **n** (the *protocol*) should be **completely hidden** from layer **n+1**.
- Changes to the internal protocol must **NOT** affect the interface contract.

---

## 1.4 Service Primitives — The "Verbs of Networking"

**Service Primitives** are the **atomic, indivisible operations** (essentially function calls) that an upper-layer entity can invoke on a lower-layer entity to request a specific service action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Service Primitive):**
> A *service primitive* is the formal, standardized operation exposed at the service access point (SAP) between two adjacent layers. The complete set of primitives at a SAP constitutes the **service interface**. Primitives are categorized into four types: **Request, Indication, Response, and Confirmation.**

### The Four Primitive Categories

| Category | Direction | Meaning |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Request** | Upper → Lower | An entity requests a service to be performed. |
| **Indication** | Lower → Upper | The peer layer is informed that an event has occurred. |
| **Response** | Upper → Lower | An entity responds to an indication. |
| **Confirmation** | Lower → Upper | The upper layer is informed that its request has been completed. |

### Real-World Analogy: Telephone Call Setup

- You **dial** a number → `CONNECT.request`
- Your phone **rings** on the other end → `CONNECT.indication`
- The callee **lifts the receiver** → `CONNECT.response`
- Your phone **stops ringing** with dial tone → `CONNECT.confirmation`

This is the exact mapping of service primitives to a real-world signalling protocol — the **client-server handshake** in OS kernels (Berkeley Sockets API) follows this same pattern.

---

## 1.5 Protocol — The "How It Actually Works"

> [!NOTE]
> **Protocol** is the set of internal rules, formats, and procedures governing communication between *peer* entities in the **same layer** on different machines. It is entirely **hidden from the upper layer** and may change without affecting the interface contract.

The classic KTU mnemonic for these three concepts:

> **S**ervice = *What* | **I**nterface = *How to ask* | **P**rotocol = *How it's done*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Relationship Between Service, Interface, and Protocol

A frequently tested KTU conceptual question concerns the **distinction** between these three abstractions. The following decomposition is high-yield for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

### 2.1.1 Service Types — A Detailed Classification

Network services are classified along two orthogonal axes:

#### Axis 1: Connection Mode
1. **Connection-Oriented Service** — A connection (analogous to a telephone call) is established before data transfer, maintained during transfer, and explicitly torn down afterward.
2. **Connectionless Service** — Each message (datagram) is routed independently, like postal letters. No prior setup is required.

#### Axis 2: Reliability
1. **Reliable Service** — Guarantees delivery, sequence, and absence of duplicates.
2. **Unreliable Service** — No such guarantees; the application handles errors.

### The 4 Canonical Service Combinations (Tanenbaum Table)

| Service Type | Example | Characteristics |
|--------------|---------|-----------------|
| **Reliable Connection-Oriented** | TCP, ATM Virtual Circuits | Connection established, no loss, in-order delivery |
| **Unreliable Connection-Oriented** | (Rare) | Connection state but no reliability guarantee |
| **Datagram (Connectionless, Unreliable)** | UDP, IP | Each packet independent, no guarantee |
| **Reliable Datagram (Acknowledged Connectionless)** | (Rare, e.g., some RPC systems) | Independent packets but with acknowledgments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The TCP/IP model uses a **reliable connection-oriented** service (TCP) and an **unreliable connectionless** service (UDP) at the transport layer. Be prepared to compare them in a 14-mark question.

---

## 2.2 The Six Standard Service Primitives (Tanenbaum Model)

For a **reliable connection-oriented service**, six primitives are defined (KTU expects you to know all six):

| # | Primitive | Meaning | Type |
|---|-----------|---------|------|
| 1 | `LISTEN` | Server waits passively for an incoming connection. | Blocked on server |
| 2 | `CONNECT` | Client initiates a connection to a peer. | Blocked on client |
| 3 | `ACCEPT` | Server accepts the incoming connection. | Blocked on server |
| 4 | `RECEIVE` | Blocks until a data TPDU arrives. | Blocking call |
| 5 | `SEND` | Sends a data TPDU to the peer. | Non-blocking possible |
| 6 | `DISCONNECT` | Terminates the connection. | May block |

For a **connectionless service**, only **two** primitives are needed:

| # | Primitive | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | `SEND` (or `UNIDATA`) | Sends a single, independent datagram. |
| 2 | `RECEIVE` | Blocks until a datagram arrives. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Examiners:** When drawing primitive timelines, the timing of `CONNECT.indication` arriving at the server **MUST** account for network round-trip time. The confirmation does **not** arrive instantaneously.

---

## 2.3 Service Access Points (SAPs)

A **Service Access Point (SAP)** is the logical address or "port" at which a layer **n** delivers service to layer **n+1**.

- In the **OSI model**, the SAP between the **Transport layer (L4)** and **Session layer (L5)** is the **Transport Service Access Point (TSAP)**.
- In **TCP/IP**, the analogue is the **port number** (e.g., HTTP uses port 80, HTTPS uses 443).

### SAP Hierarchy

| SAP Type | Layer Pair | Real-World Instance |
|----------|------------|---------------------|
| **MSAP** | Physical ↔ Data Link | MAC Address |
| **LSAP** | Data Link ↔ Network | Frame relay DLCI |
| **NSAP** | Network ↔ Transport | IP Address |
| **TSAP** | Transport ↔ Session | Port Number (e.g., 80) |
| **SSAP** | Session ↔ Presentation | Session ID |
| **PSAP** | Presentation ↔ Application | (Conceptual) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Reference Matrix — Comparison of Primitives in Connection Modes

| Property | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Number of Primitives** | 6 (LISTEN, CONNECT, ACCEPT, RECEIVE, SEND, DISCONNECT) | 2 (SEND, RECEIVE) |
| **State Required** | Yes (connection state table) | No (stateless) |
| **Setup Phase** | 3-way handshake analog | None |
| **Reliability** | Often reliable (if specified) | Typically unreliable |
| **Use Cases** | File transfer, Web (HTTP) | DNS, VoIP, video streaming |
| **Example** | TCP | UDP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 6-primitive model for connection-oriented services is implemented in real-world socket APIs (e.g., **Berkeley Sockets**). The `socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()`, `connect()`, `send()`, `recv()`, `close()` system calls are a direct mapping of Tanenbaum's abstract primitives to the **C/POSIX** interface.

---

## 2.5 Why Three Abstractions? The Separation Principle

The separation of **service**, **interface**, and **protocol** is not arbitrary — it is a deliberate engineering choice to maximize **modularity** and **evolvability**.

1. **Modularity** — Each layer can be developed, tested, and replaced independently.
2. **Reusability** — A single protocol (e.g., Ethernet) can serve many upper-layer services.
3. **Innovation** — New protocols can be deployed without disrupting upper-layer applications.
4. **Standardization** — Multiple vendors can interoperate as long as they conform to the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Pitfall:** A frequent mistake is to confuse *service* with *protocol*. **Service = what is offered; Protocol = how it is implemented.** Two different protocols (e.g., TCP and SCTP) can offer the **same** transport service to the application layer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Primitive Timelines & Analytical Walkthrough

Since this topic is **conceptual-architectural** (rather than numerically computational), the "derivation" in this section focuses on the **time-ordered sequence of service primitive invocations** in two canonical scenarios. KTU frequently tests this via 14-mark timeline diagrams and time-analysis sub-questions.

---

## 3.1 Primitive Timeline: Connection-Oriented Service (Reliable)

We will trace every primitive invocation between a **Client** and a **Server** across two adjacent layers, layer **(n+1)** and layer **(n)**, with explicit timing.

### Step 1: Server Initialization

The server's layer (n+1) issues a blocking call:

$$\text{Listen.request} \rightarrow \text{Layer (n)} \rightarrow \text{Server enters passive state}$$

The server-side **layer n** entity (e.g., TCP) is now idle, listening for a `CONNECT` TPDU from any client.

### Step 2: Client Initiates Connection

The client's layer (n+1) calls:

$$\text{Connect.request}(\text{dest\_addr}, \text{dest\_SAP})$$

Layer **n** on the client side constructs a **CONNECT TPDU** and transmits it. This TPDU travels over the physical medium and incurs **propagation delay** $t_p$.

### Step 3: Server Notification

Upon arrival, the server's layer **n** notifies layer (n+1) via:

$$\text{Connect.indication} \rightarrow \text{Server's layer (n+1)}$$

The server application's `accept()` is then invoked, which the kernel satisfies by issuing:

$$\text{Accept.response} \rightarrow \text{Layer (n)} \rightarrow \text{CONNECT ACCEPTANCE TPDU sent}$$

### Step 4: Client Confirmation

The acceptance TPDU reaches the client after delay $t_p$. The client's layer **n** issues:

$$\text{Connect.confirm} \rightarrow \text{Client's layer (n+1)} \rightarrow \text{connect() returns}$$

**Total connection-establishment latency**:

$$T_{\text{estab}} = 2 \cdot t_p + T_{\text{proc,client}} + T_{\text{proc,server}}$$

where $t_p$ is the one-way propagation delay and $T_{\text{proc}}$ denotes per-layer processing time.

---

## 3.2 Symbolic Timeline Representation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } t_0 &: \text{ Server issues Listen.request} \\
\text{Time } t_1 &: \text{ Client issues Connect.request} \\
\text{Time } t_1 + t_p &: \text{ Server receives CONNECT TPDU} \\
\text{Time } t_1 + t_p &: \text{ Server issues Connect.indication} \\
\text{Time } t_2 &: \text{ Server issues Accept.response} \\
\text{Time } t_2 + t_p &: \text{ Client receives ACCEPTANCE TPDU} \\
\text{Time } t_2 + t_p &: \text{ Client issues Connect.confirm}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **data transfer** phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client:} \quad & \text{Send.request} \rightarrow \text{Data TPDU} \rightarrow \text{Server} \\
\text{Server:} \quad & \text{Receive.indication} \rightarrow \text{Server's layer (n+1)} \\
\text{Server:} \quad & \text{Receive (ACK TPDU)} \rightarrow \text{Client} \\
\text{Client:} \quad & \text{Receive.confirm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **acknowledgment round-trip time (RTT)** for one data unit is:

$$T_{\text{RTT}} = 2 \cdot t_p + 2 \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{proc}}$$

where $T_{\text{trans}}$ is the transmission delay $= \frac{L}{R}$, with $L$ = packet length (bits) and $R$ = link rate (bps).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: TCP Connection Latency (Analytical)

> **Problem:** A client and server are connected by a link of length $D = 2000$ km with a propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The link bandwidth is $R = 1$ Mbps, and the processing delay at each layer is $T_{\text{proc}} = 2$ ms. The connection-request TPDU is **40 bytes** and the acceptance TPDU is also **40 bytes**. Compute the total time from `Connect.request` to `Connect.confirm`.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute one-way propagation delay.**

$$t_p = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute transmission delay for the 40-byte TPDU.**

$$T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{40 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = \frac{320}{10^6} \text{ s} = 0.32 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Total time for CONNECT TPDU to reach the server.**

$$t_{\text{forward}} = t_p + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{proc}} = 10 + 0.32 + 2 = 12.32 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Total time for ACCEPT TPDU to return to the client.**

$$t_{\text{return}} = t_p + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{proc}} = 12.32 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Total connection-establishment time.**

$$T_{\text{estab}} = t_{\text{forward}} + t_{\text{return}} = 2 \times 12.32 = 24.64 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Examiners:** The `Connect.confirm` arrives at the client at $t_1 + 24.64$ ms. Until then, the client's `connect()` call **blocks**. Any process that is multi-threaded is unaffected, but a single-threaded client will appear "frozen" for this duration. This is a classic interview-style follow-up that KTU often asks in Part B.

---

## 3.4 Primitive Timeline: Connectionless Service (Datagram)

For a **connectionless service**, the sequence collapses to just two primitives. The client **SEND**s a datagram; the server **RECEIVE**s it. No `LISTEN`, `CONNECT`, `ACCEPT`, or `DISCONNECT` is required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client layer (n+1):} \quad & \text{Send.request}(\text{data}, \text{dest\_addr}) \\
\text{Client layer (n):} \quad & \text{Encapsulates as Data TPDU} \rightarrow \text{transmits} \\
\text{Server layer (n):} \quad & \text{Receives Data TPDU} \\
\text{Server layer (n+1):} \quad & \text{Receive.indication} \rightarrow \text{app reads buffer}
\end{aligned}
$$

There is **no confirmation**. The sender has no guarantee the datagram arrived. The "latency" is a simple one-way delay:

$$T_{\text{datagram}} = t_p + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{proc}}$$

For the worked example above, this would be $10 + 0.32 + 2 = 12.32$ ms — exactly **half** the connection-oriented latency, since no return TPDU is required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Compare the two latencies:
> - Connection-Oriented: $24.64$ ms (full handshake) + data phase.
> - Connectionless: $12.32$ ms per datagram (no handshake).
> For a single short message, the connectionless service is **faster**, but at the cost of **no reliability guarantee**. This trade-off is the foundation of the **TCP vs UDP** design decision.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `appN1`, `layerN`, `peerN`), contain no reserved keywords, and use clean uppercase labels inside double-quoted node definitions. No markdown formatting or HTML tags are embedded inside node labels.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Layer Interaction Showing Service, Interface, and Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST_A["HOST A"]
        direction TB
        appN1["LAYER N PLUS 1 APPLICATION"]
        layerN["LAYER N DATA COMMUNICATION MODULE"]
        layerN1["LAYER N MINUS 1 PHYSICAL MEDIUM DRIVER"]
    end

    subgraph HOST_B["HOST B"]
        direction TB
        appN1B["LAYER N PLUS 1 PEER APPLICATION"]
        layerNB["LAYER N PEER DATA COMMUNICATION MODULE"]
        layerN1B["LAYER N MINUS 1 PEER PHYSICAL DRIVER"]
    end

    appN1 -- SERVICE PRIMITIVES VIA SAP --> layerN
    layerN -- SERVICE PRIMITIVES VIA SAP --> layerN1

    appN1B -- PEER SERVICE PRIMITIVES VIA SAP --> layerNB
    layerNB -- PEER SERVICE PRIMITIVES VIA SAP --> layerN1B

    layerN == "PEER PROTOCOL EXCHANGE HORIZONTAL" ==> layerNB

    layerN1 == "PHYSICAL BIT STREAM TRANSMISSION" ==> layerN1B
```

> **Interpretation:** The *vertical* arrows represent the **interface** (service primitives) between adjacent layers within the same host. The *horizontal* arrows represent the **protocol** (peer-to-peer exchange) between the same layer on different hosts. The **service** is what the vertical arrow exposes to the upper layer.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Primitive Timeline for Connection-Oriented Service (Client-Server)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CAPP as CLIENT LAYER N PLUS 1
    participant CN as CLIENT LAYER N
    participant SN as SERVER LAYER N
    participant SAPP as SERVER LAYER N PLUS 1

    CAPP->>CN: CONNECT REQUEST
    Note over CN: BLOCKS UNTIL CONFIRM
    CN->>SN: CONNECT TPDU OVER NETWORK
    SN->>SAPP: CONNECT INDICATION
    SAPP->>SN: ACCEPT RESPONSE
    SN->>CN: ACCEPTANCE TPDU OVER NETWORK
    CN->>CAPP: CONNECT CONFIRM
    Note over CAPP: CONNECT RETURNS

    CAPP->>CN: SEND REQUEST WITH DATA
    CN->>SN: DATA TPDU
    SN->>SAPP: RECEIVE INDICATION
    SAPP->>SN: RECEIVE COMPLETION

    CAPP->>CN: DISCONNECT REQUEST
    CN->>SN: DISCONNECT TPDU
    SN->>SAPP: DISCONNECT INDICATION
    SAPP->>SN: DISCONNECT RESPONSE
    SN->>CN: DISCONNECT ACK TPDU
    CN->>CAPP: DISCONNECT CONFIRM
```

> **Interpretation:** Each numbered step corresponds to a primitive invocation. Notice the **blocking semantics** on `CONNECT` and `DISCONNECT`. The total wall-clock time is the sum of all propagation and processing delays, as computed in Section 3.3.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Primitive Timeline for Connectionless Service

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CAPP as CLIENT LAYER N PLUS 1
    participant CN as CLIENT LAYER N
    participant SN as SERVER LAYER N
    participant SAPP as SERVER LAYER N PLUS 1

    CAPP->>CN: SEND REQUEST WITH DATA AND DEST
    Note over CN: NO HANDSHAKE NO ACK
    CN->>SN: DATAGRAM TPDU
    SN->>SAPP: RECEIVE INDICATION

    Note over CAPP,SAPP: NO CONFIRMATION IS GENERATED
    Note over CN,SN: UNRELIABLE FIRE AND FORGET
```

> **Interpretation:** Notice the **absence** of `CONNECT`, `ACCEPT`, and `DISCONNECT` steps. The connectionless service offers only the two primitives `SEND` and `RECEIVE`, and there is no confirmation of delivery.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: SAP Architecture Across All OSI Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    L4 -- NSAP --> L3["LAYER 3 NETWORK"]
    L3 -- LSAP --> L2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK"]
    L2 -- MSAP --> L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL"]

    L4 -.- TSAPEX["EXAMPLE: TCP PORT 80 HTTP"]
    L3 -.- NSAPEX["EXAMPLE: IP ADDRESS 192.168.1.10"]

```

> **Interpretation:** Each adjacent-layer boundary in the OSI model has a named **Service Access Point**. These are the concrete realization of the abstract "interface" concept.
