# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing *Service* with *Protocol*:** The most frequent 3-mark deduction. Service is *what* is provided; protocol is *how* it is provided.
> 2. **Mis-drawing the primitive timeline:** Students often forget to include `Indication` and `Confirmation` arrows, or omit the network propagation delay between the two hosts.
> 3. **Forgetting the four primitive types:** Request, Indication, Response, and Confirmation. KTU expects all four in a complete primitive-timeline answer.
> 4. **Mixing up connection-oriented and connectionless primitive counts:** 6 vs 2. Drawing a 6-primitive timeline for a UDP question (or vice versa) loses 3-4 marks immediately.
> 5. **Ignoring SAPs:** A 14-mark answer that does not mention **Service Access Points** is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Service vs. Protocol
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

> **Differentiate between *Service* and *Protocol* in the context of a layered network architecture. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Service** is the set of operations that a layer offers to the layer immediately above it. It describes *what* the upper layer can request, not *how* those requests are fulfilled. The service is implemented at the **Service Access Point (SAP)** using **service primitives**.

**Protocol**, on the other hand, is the set of rules governing communication between *peer* entities on different machines. It defines *how* the data is formatted, addressed, and exchanged between two entities in the same layer. A protocol is entirely internal to a layer and is **hidden from the upper layer**.

| Aspect | Service | Protocol |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Definition | What is provided | How it is provided |
| Scope | Vertical (between adjacent layers) | Horizontal (between peer layers) |
| Visibility | Visible to upper layer | Hidden from upper layer |
| Example | HTTP file transfer service | TCP (implements that service) |

> **Example:** A user browsing a webpage receives an HTTP *service* (a file-fetch API). This service is provided by the **TCP protocol** running beneath it. The user sees only the *service*; the *protocol* is invisible.

---

### Question 2: Service Primitives — Four Categories
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **What are the four categories of service primitives? Illustrate the categories using a telephone-call analogy.**

**Model Answer:**

The four primitive categories are:

1. **Request** — Issued by the upper layer to request a service.  
   *Telephone analogy: The caller dials a number.*

2. **Indication** — Issued by the lower layer to inform the upper layer that an event has occurred.  
   *Telephone analogy: The callee's phone rings.*

3. **Response** — Issued by the upper layer in response to an indication.  
   *Telephone analogy: The callee lifts the receiver.*

4. **Confirmation** — Issued by the lower layer to inform the upper layer that its request has been completed.  
   *Telephone analogy: The caller's phone stops ringing; the dial tone is heard.*

These four categories together enable **synchronous, confirmed service interactions** between adjacent layers, mirroring real-world signalling protocols.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with sub-parts requiring a diagram, derivation, or comparison.

---

### Question A: Full Primitive Timeline Analysis
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

> **(a)** With neat timeline diagrams, describe the **six service primitives** used in a reliable connection-oriented service. Clearly label every `Request`, `Indication`, `Response`, and `Confirmation`.  
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A client and a server are separated by a satellite link of one-way propagation delay **270 ms** and link bandwidth **1 Gbps**. Each processing delay is **0.5 ms**, and the CONNECT TPDU and ACCEPT TPDU are each **100 bytes**. Compute the total time from `Connect.request` to `Connect.confirm` on the client. If 5 data TPDUs of 1500 bytes each are then sent (with immediate acknowledgments), what is the total wall-clock time until all 5 ACKs have been received?  
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part A — Model Solution

The **six service primitives** for a reliable connection-oriented service are:

1. `LISTEN` — server waits for connection.
2. `CONNECT` — client initiates.
3. `ACCEPT` — server accepts.
4. `SEND` — client sends data.
5. `RECEIVE` — peer receives data.
6. `DISCONNECT` — both ends terminate.

**Timeline Diagram (model student should reproduce this):**

```
         CLIENT LAYER N+1   CLIENT LAYER N         SERVER LAYER N     SERVER LAYER N+1
              |                  |                       |                    |
   CONNECT_req|----------------->|                       |                    |
              |   CONN TPDU      | -- network delay --> |                    |
              |                  |                       | CONN_indic  -----> |
              |                  |                       |                    |
              |                  |                       | <----  ACCEPT_resp|
              |                  |  <-- ACCEPT TPDU ---  |                    |
   CONN_conf<-|------------------|                       |                    |
              |   DATA TPDU      | -- network delay --> |                    |
              |                  |                       | DATA_indic  -----> |
              |   ACK TPDU       | <-- network delay -- |                    |
              |                  |                       |                    |
  DISCON_req  |----------------->|                       |                    |
              |  DISCON TPDU     | -- network delay --> |                    |
              |                  |                       | DISCON_indic  ---->|
              |                  | <-- DISCON ACK TPDU -|                    |
  DISCON_conf<|------------------|                       |                    |
```

**Marking Scheme Allocation (7 marks):**
- Naming all 6 primitives: **1.5 marks**
- Correct timeline diagram with all 4 primitive categories: **3.5 marks**
- Brief explanation of each primitive: **2 marks**

---

#### Part B — Model Solution

**Step 1: Compute one-way propagation delay.**

$$t_p = 270 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute transmission delay for a 100-byte TPDU.**

$$T_{\text{trans,conn}} = \frac{100 \times 8}{10^9} = \frac{800}{10^9} = 0.0008 \text{ ms} \approx 0.8 \, \mu s$$

Since the transmission delay is **negligible** compared to the 270 ms propagation delay, we may approximate:

$$t_{\text{forward}} = t_p + T_{\text{proc}} = 270 + 0.5 = 270.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Total connection-establishment time (round trip).**

$$T_{\text{estab}} = 2 \times 270.5 = 541 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Transmission delay for a 1500-byte data TPDU.**

$$T_{\text{trans,data}} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{10^9} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.012 \text{ ms}$$

This is again negligible.

**Step 5: Time for 5 data TPDUs and their ACKs.**

Each data TPDU + ACK constitutes a **round trip**:

$$T_{\text{1 data+ACK}} = 2 \times t_p + 2 \times T_{\text{proc}} = 2 \times 270.5 = 541 \text{ ms}$$

For **5 sequential** data TPDUs (the client waits for each ACK before sending the next, in a stop-and-wait protocol):

$$T_{\text{5 data+ACKs}} = 5 \times 541 = 2705 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6: Total wall-clock time.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{estab}} + T_{\text{5 data+ACKs}} = 541 + 2705 = 3246 \text{ ms} = 3.246 \text{ s}$$

**Marking Scheme Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct computation of $t_p$ and $T_{\text{trans}}$: **1.5 marks**
- Correct connection establishment time: **1.5 marks**
- Correct per-data TPDU latency: **1.5 marks**
- Correct total wall-clock time with units: **2.5 marks**

---

### Question B: Service Primitives and Connectionless Service Comparison
**[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** Compare and contrast **connection-oriented** and **connectionless** services in terms of: (i) number of primitives, (ii) state, (iii) reliability, (iv) latency, and (v) example protocols.  
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Draw the complete primitive timeline for a **datagram (connectionless)** service between two peers. Explain why no `CONNECT` or `DISCONNECT` primitive is required. If 3 datagrams are sent back-to-back, what is the total time given $t_p = 50$ ms and processing delay $T_{\text{proc}} = 1$ ms per datagram (ignoring transmission delay)?  
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part A — Model Solution

| Criterion | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **(i) Primitives** | 6 (LISTEN, CONNECT, ACCEPT, RECEIVE, SEND, DISCONNECT) | 2 (SEND, RECEIVE) |
| **(ii) State** | Connection state table maintained at both ends | Stateless |
| **(iii) Reliability** | Often reliable (if specified) | Typically unreliable |
| **(iv) Latency** | High (handshake overhead: $2 \times t_p + \text{proc}$) | Low (one-way only: $t_p + \text{proc}$) |
| **(v) Example** | TCP, ATM, Frame Relay | UDP, IP, ICMP, DNS |

**Additional discussion points for full marks:**
- Connection-oriented is suited for **bulk, in-order data transfer** (file transfer, email).
- Connectionless is suited for **short, latency-sensitive messages** (DNS, video, VoIP).
- The 3-way handshake of TCP is a direct implementation of the CONNECT/ACCEPT primitive exchange.

**Marking Scheme Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct entries in all 5 criteria: **3.5 marks**
- Justified examples and use-case discussion: **2.5 marks**
- Comparison summary: **1 mark**

---

#### Part B — Model Solution

**Timeline Diagram (model student should reproduce):**

```
       CLIENT LAYER N+1   CLIENT LAYER N         SERVER LAYER N     SERVER LAYER N+1
            |                  |                       |                    |
  SEND_req#1|----------------->|                       |                    |
            |  DATA TPDU #1    | -- t_p delay --->     |                    |
            |                  |                       | RECV_indic#1 ----> |
            |                  |                       |                    |
  SEND_req#2|----------------->|                       |                    |
            |  DATA TPDU #2    | -- t_p delay --->     |                    |
            |                  |                       | RECV_indic#2 ----> |
            |                  |                       |                    |
  SEND_req#3|----------------->|                       |                    |
            |  DATA TPDU #3    | -- t_p delay --->     |                    |
            |                  |                       | RECV_indic#3 ----> |
```

**Why no CONNECT/DISCONNECT?**  
In a connectionless service, there is **no logical session** between the two peers. Each datagram carries its full destination address, and the receiver has no obligation to acknowledge. The service is **stateless and best-effort**, so a setup or teardown phase would be redundant and would actually waste bandwidth. Furthermore, a connection state table would consume memory and add overhead for every sender-receiver pair — an unnecessary cost for short, one-shot messages.

**Step-by-step latency calculation:**

**Step 1: Time for one datagram.**

$$T_{\text{1 datagram}} = t_p + T_{\text{proc}} = 50 + 1 = 51 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: For 3 back-to-back datagrams (no ACKs in between).**

$$T_{\text{3 datagrams}} = 3 \times 51 = 153 \text{ ms}$$

**Marking Scheme Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct timeline diagram with SEND/RECEIVE primitives: **3 marks**
- Valid justification for absence of CONNECT/DISCONNECT: **2 marks**
- Correct latency calculation: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this just before entering the exam hall):**

- [ ] **Service** = *what* a layer offers to the upper layer (vertical, SAP-bound).
- [ ] **Interface** = *how* the upper layer invokes the service (set of primitives).
- [ ] **Protocol** = *how* the service is implemented (horizontal, peer-to-peer).
- [ ] The triad **Service — Interface — Protocol** is the cornerstone of layered design.
- [ ] **4 Primitive Types:** Request, Indication, Response, Confirmation.
- [ ] **6 Primitives** for reliable connection-oriented: LISTEN, CONNECT, ACCEPT, RECEIVE, SEND, DISCONNECT.
- [ ] **2 Primitives** for connectionless: SEND, RECEIVE.
- [ ] **SAP (Service Access Point)** = the logical "port" through which services are delivered. Examples: MAC address, IP address, port number.
- [ ] Connection establishment latency = $2 \cdot t_p + 2 \cdot T_{\text{proc}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{trans}}$.
- [ ] Datagram latency = $t_p + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{trans}}$ (no return path).
- [ ] **Connection-oriented = high setup latency but reliable** (e.g., TCP).
- [ ] **Connectionless = low latency but unreliable** (e.g., UDP).
- [ ] A change in **protocol** must **not** change the **service** (Information Hiding Principle).
- [ ] Real-world API mapping: Berkeley Sockets (`socket`, `bind`, `listen`, `accept`, `connect`, `send`, `recv`, `close`) directly corresponds to Tanenbaum's abstract primitives.
- [ ] Always draw the **propagation-delay segment** between hosts in timeline diagrams — examiners look for it explicitly.
- [ ] In a stop-and-wait protocol, **N** data units + ACKs take $N \times 2t_p$ of pure propagation time.
- [ ] Connection-oriented services need **state**; connectionless services are **stateless**.
- [ ] KTU 2024 frequently pairs this topic with **OSI/TCP-IP layer comparisons** and **TCP vs UDP trade-offs**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reference Models: OSI and TCP/IP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reference Models: OSI and TCP/IP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Network Reference Model?

A **Network Reference Model** is a logical, layered blueprint that standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstracted hierarchical layers. Each layer performs a well-defined set of networking functions and communicates only with its immediate **adjacent layers** through well-defined **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *reference model* is a conceptual framework that describes how data moves through a network. It does not implement anything — it only *describes*. The two dominant reference models are the **OSI Model (7 layers)** and the **TCP/IP Model (4 layers)**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to be able to:
- Identify each layer of both models.
- Describe the **functions**, **services**, and **protocols** of every layer.
- Compare the two models critically.
- Trace a packet through the layers (encapsulation/decapsulation).

---

## 1.2 The OSI Reference Model (Open Systems Interconnection)

The **OSI Model** was developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** as part of the **ISO/IEC 7498** standard, formally adopted in **1984**. It was created to be a vendor-neutral, universal reference model for **Open System Interconnection** — meaning systems built by *any* manufacturer following the model could interoperate.

### The 7 Layers of the OSI Model (Top-Down Mnemonic — "**A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing"):

| # | Layer | Mnemonic Position | Primary Role |
|---|-------|------------------|--------------|
| 7 | **Application** | All | Network services to end-user applications |
| 6 | **Presentation** | People | Data translation, encryption, compression |
| 5 | **Session** | Seem | Dialog control and synchronization |
| 4 | **Transport** | To | Reliable end-to-end delivery, segmentation |
| 3 | **Network** | Need | Logical addressing and routing |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Data | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection |
| 1 | **Physical** | Processing | Bit transmission over physical medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU questions, you will often be asked to list layers in *bottom-up* order as well. Memorize both directions — top-down and bottom-up.

---

## 1.3 The TCP/IP Reference Model (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)

The **TCP/IP Model** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) was developed by the **U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)** through **DARPA** (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency) in the **1970s** and is the *de facto* model powering the modern Internet. It is defined primarily in the **RFC series** (Request for Comments) published by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)**.

### The 4 Layers of the TCP/IP Model:

| # | Layer | Maps to OSI Layers |
|---|-------|--------------------|
| 4 | **Application** | OSI Layers 5, 6, 7 |
| 3 | **Transport** | OSI Layer 4 |
| 2 | **Internet** | OSI Layer 3 |
| 1 | **Network Access (Link/Physical)** | OSI Layers 1, 2 |

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The International Postal System"

Imagine you want to send a **gift** from your home in **Kerala** to a friend in **Germany**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Analogy:**

1. **You (Application Layer):** You decide *what* to send and *why* — a letter, a photo, or a file. (Just like the Application layer chooses the network service — email, web, FTP.)

2. **Translation Bureau (Presentation Layer):** If your friend only reads German, the letter must be **translated**; if you want privacy, you put it inside a **sealed envelope** (encryption). If the file is too large, you **compress** it.

3. **Post Office Counter (Session Layer):** The clerk opens a "session" — gives you a **receipt number**, decides if the conversation will be **half-duplex** (alternating letters) or **full-duplex** (phone call), and when the session ends, the receipt is torn.

4. **Sorting Department (Transport Layer):** The long letter is split into **smaller envelopes** (segments), each labeled with a **sequence number** so the friend can reassemble correctly. A **parcel tracking ID** (port number) is added.

5. **Route Planner (Network Layer):** The postal network uses the friend's **country, city, and street address** (logical IP address) to determine the **best global route** — maybe via Mumbai → Dubai → Frankfurt.

6. **Truck Loading Bay (Data Link Layer):** Each envelope is placed in a **truck crate** (frame), labeled with a **barcode** (MAC address) for the *next* truck, with a **checksum** to detect damage in transit.

7. **The Truck Itself (Physical Layer):** The crate is loaded onto a **physical truck** that drives on **real roads** — the *bits* travel as electrical signals in copper wires, pulses of light in fiber, or radio waves in Wi-Fi.

**At the receiving end**, every step runs in **reverse** — the friend's "layers" unwrap one by one until the gift is delivered. This exact *top-down then bottom-up* flow is what happens in a real network!

---

## 1.5 Why We Need Layered Models (Advantages)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board loves asking "Why layering?" — here is the full marks answer:**

- **Modularity:** Each layer can be designed/replaced independently without disturbing others (e.g., HTTP can run over TCP today, QUIC tomorrow — Application layer unchanged).
- **Abstraction:** Upper layers don't need to know how lower layers physically work; they just use the *services* offered below.
- **Interoperability:** Vendors worldwide follow the same layer definitions → products interoperate.
- **Easier Troubleshooting:** Network engineers can isolate problems to a specific layer (e.g., "IP conflict" → Layer 3; "cable unplugged" → Layer 1).
- **Standardization of Protocols:** Promotes a common language and protocol interface (e.g., SAPs).
- **Encourages Specialization:** Hardware vendors focus on Layer 1–2; OS developers on Layer 3–4; application developers on Layer 7.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Function Breakdown of Each OSI Layer

### 🔹 Layer 1 — Physical Layer
- Transmits **raw bits** (`0`s and `1`s) over a communication channel.
- Defines **mechanical** (connector type — RJ45, LC fiber), **electrical** (voltage levels, impedance), **functional** (bit synchronization), and **procedural** (handshaking) specifications.
- Examples: **Ethernet (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX), USB, Bluetooth radio, SONET/SDH, IEEE 802.11 PHY**.
- **Data Unit:** Bit.
- Typical devices: **Hub, Repeater, Network Interface Card (PHY chip), Cables, Connectors**.

### 🔹 Layer 2 — Data Link Layer
- Provides **node-to-node** transfer on the *same* physical segment.
- Divided into two sublayers (IEEE 802 split):
  - **LLC (Logical Link Control) — IEEE 802.2:** Multiplexing, error reporting, flow control to upper layer.
  - **MAC (Media Access Control) — IEEE 802.3/802.11:** Physical addressing, channel access control (CSMA/CD for Ethernet, CSMA/CA for Wi-Fi).
- **Functions:** Framing, physical (MAC) addressing, error *detection* (not correction at this layer in classic OSI), flow control between adjacent nodes.
- **Data Unit:** **Frame**.
- Examples: **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay, ARP**.
- Typical devices: **Switch, Bridge, NIC (MAC layer)**.

### 🔹 Layer 3 — Network Layer
- Handles **end-to-end packet delivery** across *multiple* networks (inter-networking).
- Provides **logical addressing** (IP addresses — IPv4 32-bit, IPv6 128-bit).
- Performs **routing** — determining the best path through routers using protocols like **OSPF, BGP, RIP**.
- Handles **packet fragmentation & reassembly** when traversing links of different **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)**.
- **Data Unit:** **Packet / Datagram**.
- Examples: **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, IPsec, OSPF, BGP**.
- Typical devices: **Router, Layer 3 Switch**.

### 🔹 Layer 4 — Transport Layer
- Provides **end-to-end** (process-to-process) delivery between **hosts**.
- **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** using **port numbers** (16-bit, range 0–65535).
- Two principal protocol classes:
  - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream, three-way handshake, retransmission, flow & congestion control.
  - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** — Connectionless, unreliable (best-effort), low-overhead, used for DNS, VoIP, video streaming.
- **Data Unit:** **Segment (TCP)** or **Datagram (UDP)**.
- Provides **end-to-end error recovery** and **flow control** (TCP only).
- Examples: **TCP, UDP, SCTP, DCCP**.

### 🔹 Layer 5 — Session Layer
- Establishes, **manages, and terminates** communication sessions between two hosts.
- Handles **dialog control** (half-duplex vs. full-duplex), **synchronization** (checkpoints for long transfers — e.g., resuming an interrupted file download).
- Examples: **NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP**.
- **Reality check:** The Session layer's functionality is often absorbed into the Application layer in TCP/IP.

### 🔹 Layer 6 — Presentation Layer
- **Data representation, encoding, and syntax.**
- Functions: **Encryption/Decryption (SSL/TLS handshake is initiated here, though encryption happens at multiple layers)**, **compression (gzip)**, **character encoding translation (ASCII ↔ EBCDIC ↔ Unicode)**.
- Examples: **SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, MIME, XDR**.
- **Data Unit:** Often referred to as "formatted data" or simply bytes.

### 🔹 Layer 7 — Application Layer
- Closest to the **end user**; provides **network services** to user applications.
- Does **NOT** include the application itself, but the *protocols* the application uses.
- Examples: **HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, DHCP, Telnet, SSH, SNMP**.
- **Data Unit:** **Message** or **Data**.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Function Breakdown of TCP/IP Layers

| TCP/IP Layer | Core Function | Key Protocols | OSI Equivalent |
|--------------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Application** | High-level APIs, network services to end-user | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, DHCP, SSH, SNMP | 5, 6, 7 |
| **Transport** | Host-to-host communication, multiplexing | TCP, UDP, DCCP | 4 |
| **Internet** | Logical addressing & routing across networks | IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, IGMP, ARP* | 3 |
| **Network Access (Link)** | Framing, MAC addressing, physical bit transmission | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay, SLIP | 1, 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> *ARP technically operates at Layer 2/3 boundary — it resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024 — Save this for the exam hall!**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **MTU (Max Transmission Unit)** | Largest single PDU a layer can pass down | Bytes (typical Ethernet MTU $= 1500$ B) |
| 2 | **Throughput (Goodput)** | $G = \dfrac{\text{Useful data bytes}}{\text{Total time}}$ | bits per second (bps) |
| 3 | **Efficiency of Stop-and-Wait ARQ** | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Dimensionless ($\vert \eta \vert \le 1$) |
| 4 | **Total Time for Frame Transmission** | $T_{total} = T_t + T_p$ | seconds |
| 5 | **Propagation Time** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$, where $v$ is signal propagation speed | seconds; $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber |
| 6 | **Transmission Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$, where $L$ is frame size, $R$ is link rate | seconds |
| 7 | **IP Header Length (IPv4)** | $\text{IHL} \times 4$ | Bytes (min 20, max 60) |
| 8 | **IPv4 Address** | 32 bits $= 4 \times 8$ bits $= 4$ octets | Decimal-dotted; $2^{32}$ addresses |
| 9 | **IPv6 Address** | 128 bits $= 8 \times 16$ bits $= 8$ hextets | Hex-colon; $2^{128}$ addresses |
| 10 | **TCP/UDP Port Range** | $0 - 65535$ ($\vert 0 \vert - \vert 65535 \vert$) | Well-known: $0-1023$; Registered: $1024-49151$; Dynamic: $49152-65535$ |
| 11 | **OSI Layers Count** | 7 layers | Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical |
| 12 | **TCP/IP Layers Count** | 4 layers | Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access |
| 13 | **Data Unit Naming per Layer** | Message $\to$ Segment $\to$ Packet $\to$ Frame $\to$ Bits | Top to bottom (OSI) |
| 14 | **OSI Layer Where Encryption Occurs** | Layer 6 (Presentation) — primarily | TLS actually spans Layers 4–7 |

---

## 2.4 Key Services Offered by Each Layer (KTU Favorite Question)

Each layer uses the services of the layer *below* and provides services to the layer *above*. The boundary is called a **SAP (Service Access Point)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example — Reading your Gmail:**
> - **HTTP (L7)** requests the page → uses **TCP's reliable stream (L4)** → uses **IP's global delivery (L3)** → uses **Ethernet's frame delivery (L2)** → uses **electrical/optical signals (L1)**.

**Service Types at the Transport Layer (KTU Frequently Asked):**
- **Connection-oriented service** (TCP) — like a phone call; setup, use, teardown.
- **Connectionless service** (UDP) — like posting a letter; no setup, no guarantee.

**Quality of Service (QoS) parameters that layers may guarantee:**
- **Reliability** (no lost data)
- **Throughput / Bandwidth** (bits per second)
- **Delay / Latency** (seconds)
- **Jitter** (variation in delay)
- **Error rate** (probability of bit error — BER)
- **Security** (confidentiality, integrity, authentication)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does an engineer care about these models?**

- **Network Engineers** use the OSI model for **troubleshooting bottom-up** (ping the physical layer first, then work up).
- **Cybersecurity Analysts** identify attack vectors per layer: **Layer 1** — physical tapping; **Layer 2** — ARP spoofing, MAC flooding; **Layer 3** — IP spoofing, smurf attacks; **Layer 4** — SYN flood, port scanning; **Layer 7** — SQL injection, XSS, phishing.
- **Software Developers** choose the right layer to develop at — e.g., a chat app developer works at Layer 7, while a router OS developer works at Layer 3.
- **Telecom Engineers** designing 5G NR follow the layered model — radio physical layer (L1), MAC/RLC (L2), RRC/PDCP (L3).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Encapsulation Flow & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Encapsulation / Decapsulation Process (Most Important Diagram!)

When data travels **down** the sending host's stack, every layer **adds its own header** (and sometimes trailer). This process is called **Encapsulation**. At the receiving end, the reverse — **Decapsulation** — occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 12-Mark Question Pattern:** *"With a neat diagram, explain data encapsulation in the OSI model. Name the data unit at each layer."*

### Step-by-Step Encapsulation Walkthrough

Let's assume an **HTTP GET request** is sent from Host A to Host B. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step transformation:

---

**Step 1 — Application Layer (L7)**
The browser creates an **HTTP request message** (e.g., `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`).
No header added yet. **Data Unit: Message (or Data).**

$$\text{Data}_7 = \text{"GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: ktu.ac.in\r\n..."}$$

---

**Step 2 — Presentation Layer (L6)**
The message may be **compressed (gzip)**, **encrypted (TLS)**, or **encoded (ASCII → UTF-8)**.
A Presentation header $H_6$ is prepended. **Data Unit: Formatted Data.**

$$\text{Data}_6 = H_6 \;\vert\; \text{Data}_7$$

---

**Step 3 — Session Layer (L5)**
A **session token / synchronization point** is added in $H_5$.
**Data Unit: SPDU (Session Protocol Data Unit).**

$$\text{Data}_5 = H_5 \;\vert\; H_6 \;\vert\; \text{Data}_7$$

---

**Step 4 — Transport Layer (L4) — TCP Segment**
A **TCP header** $H_4$ is prepended. The TCP header contains:
- **Source & Destination Port Numbers** (16 bits each) — used for multiplexing.
- **Sequence Number** (32 bits) — for ordering.
- **Acknowledgment Number** (32 bits).
- **Flags** (SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG) — control bits.
- **Checksum**, **Window Size**, **Urgent Pointer**.
**Data Unit: Segment.**

$$\text{Segment} = H_{4,TCP} \;\vert\; \text{Data}_5$$

---

**Step 5 — Network Layer (L3) — IP Packet**
An **IP header** $H_3$ is prepended. The IPv4 header (20–60 bytes) contains:
- **Version (4 bits)** $= 4$, **IHL**, **Type of Service**, **Total Length**.
- **Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset** (for fragmentation).
- **TTL (Time To Live)**, **Protocol** (6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 1 = ICMP).
- **Header Checksum**.
- **Source IP Address (32 bits)** and **Destination IP Address (32 bits)**.
**Data Unit: Packet / Datagram.**

$$\text{Packet} = H_{3,IP} \;\vert\; \text{Segment}$$

---

**Step 6 — Data Link Layer (L2) — Frame**
A **Frame Header** ($H_2$, with source & destination **MAC addresses** of the *next hop*) is prepended, and a **Frame Trailer** ($T_2$, with **FCS — Frame Check Sequence** for CRC error detection) is appended.
**Data Unit: Frame.**

$$\text{Frame} = H_{2,MAC} \;\vert\; \text{Packet} \;\vert\; T_{2,FCS}$$

---

**Step 7 — Physical Layer (L1) — Bits**
The frame is converted into a **bitstream** of `0`s and `1`s, encoded as **electrical voltages**, **light pulses**, or **radio waves**, and transmitted one bit at a time on the physical medium.
**Data Unit: Bits.**

$$\text{Bitstream} = b_0, b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_{N-1} \in \{0, 1\}$$

---

### 3.1.1 Formal Generalized Encapsulation Equation

For a generic layer $k$, the encapsulation operation can be expressed as:

$$\text{PDU}_k = H_k \;\vert\; \text{SDU}_{k+1}$$

where:
- $\text{PDU}_k$ = Protocol Data Unit of layer $k$ (the data unit as seen at layer $k$),
- $H_k$ = Header (and sometimes trailer) added by layer $k$,
- $\text{SDU}_{k+1}$ = Service Data Unit handed down by the layer above (layer $k+1$).

The mapping between layers:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PDU}_7 &= \text{Message} \\
\text{PDU}_6 &= \text{Formatted Data} = H_6 \vert \text{PDU}_7 \\
\text{PDU}_5 &= \text{SPDU} = H_5 \vert \text{PDU}_6 \\
\text{PDU}_4 &= \text{Segment} = H_4 \vert \text{PDU}_5 \\
\text{PDU}_3 &= \text{Packet} = H_3 \vert \text{PDU}_4 \\
\text{PDU}_2 &= \text{Frame} = H_2 \vert \text{PDU}_3 \vert T_2 \\
\text{PDU}_1 &= \text{Bitstream}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Total Frame Size on the Wire

If a single HTTP message of $L$ bytes is sent, the total on-wire frame size is:

$$L_{\text{on-wire}} = \underbrace{L_{H2}}_{\text{L2 header}} + \underbrace{L_{H3}}_{\text{L3 header (20-60 B)}} + \underbrace{L_{H4}}_{\text{L4 header (TCP: 20-60 B)}} + L + \underbrace{L_{T2}}_{\text{L2 trailer (4 B FCS)}}$$

**Example calculation** (Ethernet + IPv4 + TCP, no options):
- L2 header (Ethernet): $L_{H2} = 14$ B
- L2 trailer (FCS): $L_{T2} = 4$ B
- L3 header (IPv4): $L_{H3} = 20$ B
- L4 header (TCP): $L_{H4} = 20$ B
- HTTP message: $L = 500$ B

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{on-wire}} &= 14 + 20 + 20 + 500 + 4 \\
&= 558 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Overhead Ratio:**
$$\text{Overhead} = \frac{L_{\text{on-wire}} - L}{L_{\text{on-wire}}} = \frac{58}{558} \approx 0.104 = 10.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Students often forget the **trailer** at L2 (the FCS). Examiners explicitly test for this. Always include both header **and** trailer at Layer 2.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — OSI vs TCP/IP

| Attribute | **OSI Model** | **TCP/IP Model** |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Full Name** | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| **Year of Adoption** | 1984 (ISO/IEC 7498) | 1970s (DoD ARPANET); IETF RFCs |
| **Number of Layers** | 7 | 4 |
| **Type** | Reference model (descriptive) | Both reference and *implementation* model |
| **Origin** | ISO (international standards body) | DARPA / DoD / IETF (USA) |
| **Adoption** | Conceptual teaching, legacy systems | The Internet — practical, ubiquitous |
| **Session & Presentation Layers** | Separate (L5, L6) | Combined into Application layer |
| **Physical & Data Link Layers** | Separate (L1, L2) | Combined into Network Access layer |
| **Protocol Dependency** | Protocol-independent (generic) | Built around IP, TCP, UDP |
| **Granularity** | High — clear separation of concerns | Pragmatic — focuses on what works |
| **Security Built-in** | Security layer was *proposed* (L6) but rarely implemented | IPSec integrated at Internet (L3) layer |
| **Usage in Real Networks** | Mostly a teaching reference | Used by all Internet-connected devices |
| **Flexibility** | More flexible theoretically | More flexible in practice |
| **Examples of Protocols** | Generic services described | HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet, Wi-Fi |
| **Rigor in Design** | Designed *before* protocols were written | Designed *alongside* protocols (TCP, IP) |

---

## 3.3 Data Flow Direction — Host-to-Host Transmission

When Host A sends to Host B through one intermediate **Router R**:

> **Host A** (Layers 7 → 1, encapsulating) → **Physical medium** → **Router R** (decapsulates up to L3, decides route, re-encapsulates L2 for next hop) → **Physical medium** → **Host B** (decapsulates Layers 1 → 7, delivers to application).

**Key Insight (KTU Trick Question):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At a router, the data is decapsulated only up to Layer 3.** The router does NOT look at Layers 4–7. It re-encapsulates a *new* L2 header for the *next* hop's MAC addresses.

If $n$ routers are in the path, the data is encapsulated/decapsulated **$2n + 2$ times** at Layer 2 (each hop changes the L2 header, but the L3 source/destination IP **remain unchanged** unless NAT is involved).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 10-MB file is to be transmitted over a 1-Gbps link that is 2000 km long. The signal propagates at $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Using the **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** protocol, compute:
1. Transmission time $T_t$
2. Propagation time $T_p$
3. Parameter $a = T_p / T_t$
4. Protocol efficiency $\eta$

**Given:**
- File size $L = 10$ MB $= 10 \times 10^6 \times 8$ bits $= 80 \times 10^6$ bits
- Link rate $R = 1$ Gbps $= 10^9$ bits/s
- Distance $d = 2000$ km $= 2 \times 10^6$ m
- Propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 — Transmission time:**

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{10^9} = 0.08 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation time:**

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Parameter $a$:**

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{0.01}{0.08} = 0.125$$

**Step 4 — Stop-and-Wait Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.125)} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Interpretation:** Even with a very high-speed link and a moderately long distance, the simple Stop-and-Wait protocol wastes **20% of bandwidth** waiting for acknowledgments. This is why **Sliding Window (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat)** protocols are used in real TCP.

> [!NOTE]
> This type of calculation is the foundation of Layer 2 / Layer 4 error and flow control — frequently asked in KTU Module 1 / 2 questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The OSI 7-Layer Reference Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1["APPLICATION LAYER 7 - HTTP FTP SMTP DNS"]
    user2["PRESENTATION LAYER 6 - TLS JPEG MPEG ASCII"]
    user3["SESSION LAYER 5 - NetBIOS RPC SIP"]
    user4["TRANSPORT LAYER 4 - TCP UDP SCTP"]
    user5["NETWORK LAYER 3 - IPv4 IPv6 ICMP OSPF"]
    user6["DATA LINK LAYER 2 - Ethernet WiFi PPP MAC"]
    user7["PHYSICAL LAYER 1 - Cables Fiber Radio Waves"]

    user1 --> user2
    user2 --> user3
    user3 --> user4
    user4 --> user5
    user5 --> user6
    user6 --> user7

    ext1["7 Application"] -.-> user1
    ext2["6 Presentation"] -.-> user2
    ext3["5 Session"] -.-> user3
    ext4["4 Transport"] -.-> user4
    ext5["3 Network"] -.-> user5
    ext6["2 Data Link"] -.-> user6
    ext7["1 Physical"] -.-> user7
```

---

## 4.2 The TCP/IP 4-Layer Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tcp1["APPLICATION LAYER - HTTP DNS SMTP SSH FTP"]
    tcp2["TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP UDP"]
    tcp3["INTERNET LAYER - IP ICMP ARP IGMP"]
    tcp4["NETWORK ACCESS LAYER - Ethernet WiFi PPP"]

    tcp1 --> tcp2
    tcp2 --> tcp3
    tcp3 --> tcp4

    map1["OSI Layers 5 6 7"] -.-> tcp1
    map2["OSI Layer 4"] -.-> tcp2
    map3["OSI Layer 3"] -.-> tcp3
    map4["OSI Layers 1 2"] -.-> tcp4
```

---

## 4.3 Data Encapsulation — End-to-End Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SEND["SENDING HOST"]
        direction TB
        s7["L7 Message: HTTP GET"]
        s6["L6 Encrypted or Compressed"]
        s5["L5 Session Token Added"]
        s4["L4 Segment: TCP Header plus Data"]
        s3["L3 Packet: IP Header plus Segment"]
        s2["L2 Frame: MAC Header plus Packet plus FCS"]
        s1["L1 Bitstream on the Wire"]

        s7 --> s6 --> s5 --> s4 --> s3 --> s2 --> s1
    end

    NET["PHYSICAL MEDIUM - Copper Fiber Wireless"]

    s1 --> NET

    subgraph RECV["RECEIVING HOST"]
        direction TB
        r1["L1 Bits Received"]
        r2["L2 Frame Decapsulated - MAC Checked"]
        r3["L3 Packet Decapsulated - IP Routed"]
        r4["L4 Segment Decapsulated - TCP Reassembled"]
        r5["L5 Session Closed"]
        r6["L6 Decrypted or Decompressed"]
        r7["L7 HTTP Message Delivered to Browser"]

        r1 --> r2 --> r3 --> r4 --> r5 --> r6 --> r7
    end

    NET --> r1
```

---

## 4.4 OSI-to-TCP/IP Layer Mapping — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    osiApp["OSI 7 Application"] --> tcpApp["TCP IP Application"]
    osiPres["OSI 6 Presentation"] --> tcpApp
    osiSess["OSI 5 Session"] --> tcpApp
    osiTrans["OSI 4 Transport"] --> tcpTrans["TCP IP Transport"]
    osiNet["OSI 3 Network"] --> tcpInt["TCP IP Internet"]
    osiDl["OSI 2 Data Link"] --> tcpLink["TCP IP Network Access"]
    osiPhy["OSI 1 Physical"] --> tcpLink
```

---

## 4.5 Encapsulation Layer-by-Layer (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appData["User Data - HTTP Request"]
    tcpHdr["TCP Header 20 Bytes - Source Port - Dest Port - SEQ - ACK - Flags"]
    ipHdr["IP Header 20 Bytes - Source IP - Dest IP - TTL - Protocol"]
    ethHdr["Ethernet Header 14 Bytes - Source MAC - Dest MAC - EtherType"]
    fcs["FCS Trailer 4 Bytes - CRC32 Error Check"]
    bits["Bits on the Physical Medium"]

    appData --> tcpHdr
    tcpHdr --> ipHdr
    ipHdr --> ethHdr
    ethHdr --> bits
    ethHdr --> fcs
    fcs --> bits
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the KTU exam:** Always draw **rectangular blocks** for each layer (top-down), label the **interfaces** (e.g., SAP) between them, and **annotate the PDU name** beside each layer. Use arrows to show data flow direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between OSI and TCP/IP reference models. List the layers in each model. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Number of layers | 7 | 4 |
| Type | Conceptual reference model | Practical implementation model |
| Developed by | ISO (1984) | DARPA / IETF (1970s, ARPANET) |
| Session & Presentation layers | Separate layers 5 and 6 | Merged into the Application layer |
| Physical & Data Link layers | Separate layers 1 and 2 | Merged into the Network Access layer |

**OSI Layers (top-down):** Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical.

**TCP/IP Layers (top-down):** Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "encapsulation" in the OSI model? Name the data unit at each layer. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer of the OSI model **adds its own header** (and sometimes a trailer) to the data unit received from the layer above, before passing it down to the next lower layer. This builds the data unit incrementally as it travels down the sending host's stack.

**Data units at each layer:**

| Layer | Data Unit Name |
|-------|----------------|
| 7 Application | Message / Data |
| 6 Presentation | Formatted Data |
| 5 Session | SPDU (Session Protocol Data Unit) |
| 4 Transport | Segment (TCP) or Datagram (UDP) |
| 3 Network | Packet / Datagram |
| 2 Data Link | Frame |
| 1 Physical | Bits |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain the OSI reference model in detail. List the functions of each of the seven layers. (7 Marks)**
**(b) With a neat diagram, describe the TCP/IP reference model and explain the functions of its four layers. Compare TCP/IP with the OSI model. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** was developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and published as the **ISO/IEC 7498** standard in **1984**. It is a **7-layer conceptual model** that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstract layers.

**Functions of each layer:**

1. **Layer 7 — Application Layer:** Provides **network services directly to end-user applications** (e.g., email clients, browsers). Protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS. (1 Mark)

2. **Layer 6 — Presentation Layer:** Handles **data translation, encryption/decryption, and compression**. Ensures that data sent by the Application layer of one system is readable by the Application layer of another. Examples: TLS, JPEG, ASCII. (1 Mark)

3. **Layer 5 — Session Layer:** Establishes, **manages, and terminates sessions** between two communicating hosts. Handles **dialog control** (half-duplex/full-duplex) and **synchronization** (checkpoints). Example: NetBIOS, SIP. (1 Mark)

4. **Layer 4 — Transport Layer:** Provides **end-to-end (process-to-process) delivery**, segmentation, flow control, and error recovery. Uses **port numbers** for multiplexing. **TCP** is connection-oriented and reliable; **UDP** is connectionless and best-effort. (1 Mark)

5. **Layer 3 — Network Layer:** Performs **logical addressing** (IP) and **routing** of packets across multiple networks. Handles **fragmentation/reassembly** and **TTL-based** loop prevention. Protocols: IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP. (1 Mark)

6. **Layer 2 — Data Link Layer:** Provides **node-to-node frame delivery** on the same physical link. Handles **MAC addressing**, **framing**, and **error detection** (CRC). Divided into **LLC** (sublayer for upper-layer multiplexing) and **MAC** (sublayer for channel access). (1 Mark)

7. **Layer 1 — Physical Layer:** Transmits **raw bits** as electrical, optical, or radio signals. Defines **mechanical, electrical, functional, and procedural** specifications of cables, connectors, and voltage levels. (1 Mark)

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**TCP/IP Model Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.2.)*

The **TCP/IP Reference Model** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) was developed by **DARPA** in the **1970s** and is the practical model behind the global Internet. It has **4 layers**:

1. **Application Layer:** Combines OSI layers 5, 6, and 7. Provides high-level protocols such as **HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH**. (1.5 Marks)

2. **Transport Layer:** Equivalent to OSI Layer 4. Provides host-to-host communication using **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) and **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless). Port numbers multiplex multiple applications. (1.5 Marks)

3. **Internet Layer:** Equivalent to OSI Layer 3. Routes packets across networks using logical IP addresses. Protocols: **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, ARP**. (1.5 Marks)

4. **Network Access (Link) Layer:** Combines OSI Layers 1 and 2. Defines how bits are placed on the physical medium using **Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay**. Includes framing, MAC addressing, and physical signaling. (1.5 Marks)

**Comparison with OSI:** *(Refer to the comparison matrix in SECTION 3.2 for the full table.)* — OSI is a **7-layer conceptual model**; TCP/IP is a **4-layer practical model**. OSI's Session and Presentation layers are absorbed into TCP/IP's Application layer; OSI's Physical and Data Link layers are merged into TCP/IP's Network Access layer. (1 Mark)

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain data encapsulation and decapsulation in the OSI model. Show the data unit at each layer. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Explain the functions of the Transport Layer and Network Layer in detail. Differentiate between TCP and UDP. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer **adds its own control information (header/trailer)** to the data unit received from the upper layer, before passing it to the next lower layer. **Decapsulation** is the reverse process at the receiving host.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.3 — Data Encapsulation Flow.)*

**Step-by-step encapsulation (sending side — top to bottom):**

1. **L7 (Application):** Browser creates the **HTTP request message** (e.g., `GET /index.html`). (0.5 Mark)
2. **L6 (Presentation):** Data is **encrypted (TLS)** and/or **compressed (gzip)**; Presentation header $H_6$ added. (0.5 Mark)
3. **L5 (Session):** **Session token** added in $H_5$ to identify the dialog. (0.5 Mark)
4. **L4 (Transport):** **TCP segment** is formed by prepending $H_4$ (20 bytes: source/dest port, sequence, ACK, flags, checksum, window). (1 Mark)
5. **L3 (Network):** **IP packet** is formed by prepending $H_3$ (20–60 bytes: source/dest IP, TTL, protocol, checksum). (1 Mark)
6. **L2 (Data Link):** **Frame** is formed by prepending $H_2$ (14 bytes Ethernet header with source/dest MAC, EtherType) and appending $T_2$ (4-byte CRC FCS trailer). (1.5 Marks)
7. **L1 (Physical):** Frame is converted to a **bitstream** and transmitted as electrical/optical/radio signals. (0.5 Mark)

**Decapsulation (receiving side — bottom to top):**
The receiving host strips off each header in reverse order, **passing the payload up to the next layer**, until the original HTTP message is delivered to the receiving browser. (1 Mark)

**Formal summary equation:**

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{H_2}_{\text{14B}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{H_3}_{\text{20-60B}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{H_4}_{\text{20-60B}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{HTTP Data}}_{L} \;\vert\; \underbrace{T_2}_{\text{4B FCS}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Transport Layer Functions (OSI Layer 4 / TCP-IP Layer 2):**
- **End-to-end (process-to-process)** delivery between source and destination hosts. (1 Mark)
- **Segmentation and reassembly** of large messages. (0.5 Mark)
- **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** using **16-bit port numbers** ($0 - 65535$). (0.5 Mark)
- **Connection management** (TCP's 3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK). (0.5 Mark)
- **Flow control** using sliding window (TCP). (0.5 Mark)
- **Error recovery** via acknowledgments and retransmission (TCP). (0.5 Mark)

**Network Layer Functions (OSI Layer 3 / TCP-IP Internet Layer):**
- **Logical addressing** using **IP addresses** (32-bit IPv4, 128-bit IPv6). (0.5 Mark)
- **Routing** — determining the best path using protocols like **OSPF, BGP, RIP**. (0.5 Mark)
- **Packet forwarding** at each router hop. (0.5 Mark)
- **Fragmentation and reassembly** of packets when traversing links of different MTU. (0.5 Mark)
- **TTL-based** loop prevention. (0.5 Mark)
- **ICMP** for error reporting and diagnostics (e.g., `ping`, `traceroute`). (0.5 Mark)

**TCP vs UDP Comparison:**

| Feature | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** |
|---------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Connection type | **Connection-oriented** (3-way handshake) | **Connectionless** |
| Reliability | **Reliable** — ACKs, retransmission, sequencing | **Unreliable** — best-effort, no ACK |
| Ordering | Guaranteed in-order delivery | No ordering guarantee |
| Flow control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Congestion control | Yes (slow start, AIMD) | No |
| Header size | **20–60 bytes** | **8 bytes** (fixed) |
| Speed / Overhead | Slower, higher overhead | Faster, lower overhead |
| Use cases | Web (HTTP/HTTPS), Email (SMTP), File transfer (FTP) | DNS, VoIP, video streaming, online gaming, TFTP |
| Data unit name | Segment | Datagram |

(1 Mark for the table)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Layer 2 trailer (FCS / CRC).** Many students write only the L2 header and forget the 4-byte trailer. Examiners check for this — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing "Data Unit" names** — calling a packet a "frame", or a segment a "packet". Memorize the hierarchy: **Message → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bits**.
> 3. **Writing TCP/IP as a 5-layer model** (sometimes shown with a separate "Physical" layer in textbooks). KTU expects the **4-layer** version.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention port numbers** in the Transport Layer — examiners consider this a must.
> 5. **Saying "Session layer handles encryption"** — wrong. Encryption is at the **Presentation layer** (and TLS spans layers 4–7).
> 6. **Not drawing neat, labeled diagrams.** A messy, unlabeled diagram = 0 marks for the diagram portion of a 7-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!NOTE]
> **Final revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before your exam.**

### 🔁 The 7 OSI Layers (Bottom-Up)
1. **Physical** — bits, cables, voltages, radio.
2. **Data Link** — frames, MAC addresses, switches, error detection (CRC).
3. **Network** — packets, IP addresses, routers, routing.
4. **Transport** — segments (TCP) / datagrams (UDP), ports, end-to-end.
5. **Session** — dialog control, checkpoints.
6. **Presentation** — encryption (TLS), compression, encoding.
7. **Application** — HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, the user's network service.

### 🔁 The 4 TCP/IP Layers (Bottom-Up)
1. **Network Access** — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, framing.
2. **Internet** — IP, ICMP, routing.
3. **Transport** — TCP, UDP.
4. **Application** — HTTP, DNS, SMTP, FTP, SSH.

### 🔁 Data Unit Memory Trick
> **"My S**ister **P**aints **F**ences **B**eautifully" →
> **M**essage, **S**egment, **P**acket, **F**rame, **B**its (top → bottom).

### 🔁 Key Port Numbers to Memorize
- **HTTP** = 80, **HTTPS** = 443
- **FTP** = 21, **SSH** = 22, **Telnet** = 23
- **SMTP** = 25, **POP3** = 110, **IMAP** = 143
- **DNS** = 53, **DHCP** = 67/68

### 🔁 Key Standard Bodies
- **ISO** → OSI Model
- **IEEE** → LAN/MAN standards (802.3 Ethernet, 802.11 Wi-Fi)
- **IETF** → TCP/IP, RFCs
- **ITU-T** → Telecom standards (X.25, ISDN)

### 🔁 One-Liners to Remember
- **OSI = 7 layers; TCP/IP = 4 layers.**
- **TCP is reliable + connection-oriented; UDP is unreliable + connectionless.**
- **Routers work at Layer 3; Switches at Layer 2; Hubs at Layer 1.**
- **IP address = logical (L3); MAC address = physical (L2).**
- **Encapsulation = adding headers as data goes DOWN the stack.**
- **Decapsulation = removing headers as data goes UP the stack.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this module cold — Reference Models appear in nearly every other module of this course (Module 2 = Data Link, Module 3 = Network, Module 4 = Transport, Module 5 = Application).** A weak foundation here will hurt you throughout the semester.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality of Service Metrics in Packet-Switched Networks (Throughput, Delay, Packet Loss, and Jitter)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality of Service Metrics in Packet-Switched Networks (Throughput, Delay, Packet Loss, and Jitter)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Quality of Service (QoS)?

**Quality of Service (QoS)** is a set of measurable service performance attributes that characterize the overall end-to-end transmission behavior of a packet-switched network. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, QoS metrics are the quantitative benchmarks used to evaluate **how good** a network connection is from the user's perspective.

In packet-switched networks, data is broken into discrete units called **packets** and transmitted across a shared infrastructure. Unlike traditional circuit-switched telephone networks (where a dedicated physical path is reserved), packets in a packet network must compete for shared resources (links, buffers, switches), which makes performance **variable and unpredictable**. QoS metrics give us a mathematical handle on this variability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The four cardinal QoS metrics are **(1) Throughput, (2) Delay, (3) Packet Loss, and (4) Jitter**. A network engineer must be able to define, measure, and optimize each of these.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of QoS — Intuitive Analogies

To make these four abstract metrics stick in memory, consider this single unifying analogy: **"The Internet is a Highway System."**

| QoS Metric | Highway Analogy | One-Line Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Throughput** | Number of cars that pass a toll booth per hour | "How *much* data successfully gets through?" |
| **Delay** | Travel time from city A to city B | "How *long* does it take for one packet to arrive?" |
| **Packet Loss** | Cars that fall off a cliff (accidents) | "How *many* packets never arrive at all?" |
| **Jitter** | Cars arriving at irregular intervals (some together, some alone) | "How *consistent* is the arrival pattern?" |

### 1.3 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU Board-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Throughput:** Throughput is the **rate** (in bits per second) at which useful data is successfully delivered from a source to a destination across a network path. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the total number of **successfully received bits** at the destination to the total time taken (including retransmissions, if any).  
> **Unit:** **bits/second (bps)**, often expressed in **Kbps, Mbps, or Gbps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delay (Latency):** Delay is the total time elapsed between the moment the **first bit** of a packet is transmitted at the source and the moment the **last bit** of that packet is received at the destination.  
> **Unit:** **seconds (s)**, typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)** for terrestrial networks and **microseconds (µs)** for LANs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packet Loss:** Packet loss is the ratio of the number of packets that fail to reach the destination to the total number of packets transmitted by the source. Packets are dropped when intermediate router buffers (queues) overflow.  
> **Unit:** Dimensionless **ratio** or **percentage (% Pkt Loss)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jitter (Delay Variation):** Jitter is the variation in the end-to-end delay experienced by **consecutive packets** belonging to the same flow. If packet *i* arrives with delay $d_i$ and packet $i+1$ arrives with delay $d_{i+1}$, the inter-packet jitter is $\vert d_{i+1} - d_i \vert$.  
> **Unit:** **seconds (s)**, practically in **milliseconds (ms)**.

### 1.4 Why Do These Metrics Matter? — The Real-World Perspective

| Application | Most Critical Metric | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| **File Download (FTP, HTTP)** | Throughput | High delay tolerable |
| **Video Conferencing (Zoom, Meet)** | Delay & Jitter | Needs < 150 ms one-way |
| **VoIP (Voice over IP)** | Jitter & Delay | Needs < 200 ms total |
| **Online Gaming (FPS games)** | Delay & Jitter | Needs < 50 ms |
| **Email / Web Browsing** | Packet Loss | Loss tolerable (TCP recovers) |
| **Live Streaming (IPTV)** | Throughput & Jitter | Needs consistent rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Streaming applications (audio/video) are **delay-sensitive but loss-tolerant**. File transfer applications are **loss-sensitive but delay-tolerant**. This contrast is a frequent 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Throughput — The "Bandwidth of Reality"

Throughput is often confused with **bandwidth** (the theoretical maximum capacity of a link). The crucial distinction:

* **Bandwidth** = *capacity* of the pipe (e.g., 100 Mbps link).
* **Throughput** = *actual data rate* that makes it through (e.g., you get 71 Mbps on your 100 Mbps link).

**Bottleneck Principle:** The end-to-end throughput of a path is determined by the **slowest link** along the path. If a packet traverses links of rates $R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_N$, the throughput is:

$$\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_N)$$

This is because data must "pass through" each link sequentially, and the slowest one constrains the entire flow — like a chain whose strength equals its weakest link.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$\text{Throughput} \;=\; \frac{L \cdot N}{T} \;\; \text{(bits/second)}$$

Where:
* $L$ = size of each packet in bits
* $N$ = number of packets successfully received
* $T$ = total transfer time in seconds

### 2.2 Delay — The "Four Horsemen" of Latency

The end-to-end delay ($d_{\text{end-end}}$) of a single packet consists of **four distinct components** that every KTU student must memorize:

| # | Delay Type | Symbol | Cause | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nodal Processing Delay** | $d_{\text{proc}}$ | Checking bit errors, determining output link | Microseconds (µs) |
| 2 | **Queueing Delay** | $d_{\text{queue}}$ | Waiting in router buffer for transmission | Microseconds to milliseconds |
| 3 | **Transmission Delay** | $d_{\text{trans}}$ | Pushing the packet's bits onto the link | Microseconds to milliseconds |
| 4 | **Propagation Delay** | $d_{\text{prop}}$ | Bit travelling through the physical medium | Microseconds to milliseconds |

**Master Formula:**

$$d_{\text{end-end}} \;=\; N \cdot (d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$$

Where $N$ is the **number of links (hops/nodes)** in the path.

#### 2.2.1 Transmission Delay vs. Propagation Delay — The Classic Confusion

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common KTU exam trap:** Transmission and propagation delay are **NOT the same thing**.
> * **Transmission delay** = time to **push all the bits** of the packet onto the wire. Depends on **packet size (L)** and **link bandwidth (R)**. It is a function of the **transmitter**.
> * **Propagation delay** = time for **one bit** to physically travel from one end of the link to the other. Depends on **distance (d)** and **propagation speed (s)**. It is a function of the **medium**.

Formulas:

$$d_{\text{trans}} \;=\; \frac{L}{R}$$

$$d_{\text{prop}} \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{phys}}}{s_{\text{prop}}}$$

Where:
* $L$ = packet length (bits)
* $R$ = link rate (bits/second)
* $d_{\text{phys}}$ = physical length of the link (meters)
* $s_{\text{prop}}$ = propagation speed in the medium (≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber, $3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum/air)

#### 2.2.2 Queueing Delay — The Probabilistic Beast

Queueing delay is the **most variable** of the four delays. It depends on:
* **Traffic intensity** at the router.
* **Number of packets already queued** ahead of yours.
* **Queueing discipline** (FIFO, Priority, WFQ).

If $I = \frac{La}{R}$ is the **traffic intensity** (where $a$ = average packet arrival rate, $L$ = avg packet length, $R$ = link rate), then:

* If $I \approx 0$: queueing delay is **small** (low traffic).
* If $I \to 1$: queueing delay **grows rapidly** (system saturated).
* If $I > 1$: queueing delay becomes **infinite** — packets pile up faster than they can be sent. This is a fundamental result of queueing theory.

### 2.3 Packet Loss

When a packet arrives at a router whose buffer is **full**, the router has no choice but to **drop the packet**. The packet loss ratio is:

$$\text{Pkt Loss Ratio} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{lost}}}{P_{\text{sent}}} \;\times\; 100\%$$

Where:
* $P_{\text{lost}}$ = number of packets dropped
* $P_{\text{sent}}$ = number of packets transmitted

**Real-world impact:**
* In **UDP** applications: lost packets cause glitches, gaps in audio/video.
* In **TCP** applications: lost packets trigger **retransmission**, which *reduces throughput* and *increases delay*.

### 2.4 Jitter — The "Unequal Spacing" Phenomenon

If two consecutive packets are sent with a fixed inter-departure spacing $\Delta t$ at the source, but arrive with spacing $\Delta t + \epsilon$ at the destination, then $\epsilon$ is the **jitter**. The absolute jitter for packet $i$ is:

$$J_i \;=\; \big\vert (d_{i+1} - d_i) - \Delta t_{\text{source}} \big\vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Jitter is a problem for streaming media** because the receiving application expects packets at a regular cadence (e.g., one voice sample every 20 ms). If packets arrive irregularly, the playout buffer may underrun (causing a glitch) or overrun (causing delay buildup). **Playout buffers** and **de-jitter buffers** are used at the receiver to smooth out jitter by holding packets for a small amount of time before playing them.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. Memorize it. Use it for the exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission Delay | $d_{\text{trans}} = L / R$ | $L$ = packet bits, $R$ = link rate | seconds |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $d_{\text{prop}} = d_{\text{phys}} / s$ | $d_{\text{phys}}$ = distance, $s$ = speed | seconds |
| 3 | End-to-End Delay | $d_{\text{end-end}} = N \cdot (d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$ | $N$ = hops | seconds |
| 4 | Traffic Intensity | $I = La / R$ | $L$ = pkt size, $a$ = arrival rate | dimensionless |
| 5 | Throughput (single link) | $\text{TP} = L / T$ or $L \cdot N / T$ | $L$ = pkt size, $T$ = time | bps |
| 6 | Throughput (path) | $\text{TP} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ | $R_i$ = link rates | bps |
| 7 | Packet Loss Ratio | $\text{PLR} = (P_{\text{lost}} / P_{\text{sent}}) \times 100\%$ | counts | % |
| 8 | Jitter | $J_i = \vert (d_{i+1} - d_i) - \Delta t_{\text{source}} \vert$ | delays | seconds |
| 9 | Last-mile Throughput | $\text{TP} = \min(R_{\text{server}}, R_{\text{access}}, R_i, \dots)$ | link rates | bps |
| 10 | Total Bits Transmitted | $B = L \cdot N + (N-1) \cdot L_{\text{ack}}$ | pkt sizes, count | bits |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Network Planning:** ISPs use these metrics for **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** design — e.g., "99.9% of packets delivered with delay < 50 ms."
* **Capacity Engineering:** Throughput calculations determine the required **bandwidth upgrades** during traffic growth.
* **Routing Optimization:** Metrics like delay and loss drive **OSPF cost** and **BGP path selection** decisions.
* **QoS Protocols:** Modern protocols (DiffServ, MPLS, RSVP) explicitly target improvements in delay, jitter, and loss.
* **Buffer Sizing:** Router memory and playout buffer sizes are designed based on expected jitter and loss profiles.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Delay from First Principles

We derive the end-to-end delay of a single packet traversing a path of $N$ identical links.

**Step 1 — Identify the four delay contributions at each node.**

When a packet arrives at router $k$, three things happen *at that node* before the packet moves to the next link:
1. The router checks the header for bit errors → **processing delay $d_{\text{proc}}$**.
2. The packet is placed in a queue if the outgoing link is busy → **queueing delay $d_{\text{queue}}$**.
3. The packet waits for its turn to be pushed onto the wire → **transmission delay $d_{\text{trans}}$**.

**Step 2 — Identify the delay contribution along each link.**

4. Once the first bit is on the link, it must physically travel to the next node → **propagation delay $d_{\text{prop}}$**.

**Step 3 — Sum the delays at a single node and single link.**

The time spent per node-link combination is:

$$d_{\text{node,link}} \;=\; d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all $N$ nodes in the path.**

The packet visits $N-1$ intermediate routers and $N$ links (the source transmits over link 1, the last link terminates at the destination). For typical KTU problems, we treat each "hop" as contributing all four delays:

$$d_{\text{end-end}} \;=\; N \cdot (d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$$

**Step 5 — Special case: only processing, transmission, and propagation matter.**

If queueing delay is negligible (light traffic assumption, $I \approx 0$):

$$d_{\text{end-end}} \;=\; N \cdot (d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$$

This is the formula used in most KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Complete Delay Calculation

> **Problem:** A packet of size $L = 1500$ bytes is sent from Host A to Host B across a path of $N = 3$ links. Each link has a transmission rate of $R = 1$ Mbps. The physical length of each link is $d_{\text{phys}} = 2000$ km. The propagation speed in each medium is $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Each router has a processing delay of $d_{\text{proc}} = 3$ ms, and assume queueing delay is negligible. Calculate the end-to-end delay.

**Step 1 — Convert packet size to bits.**

$$L = 1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate transmission delay per link.**

$$d_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate propagation delay per link.**

$$d_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d_{\text{phys}}}{s} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Sum delays per hop.**

$$d_{\text{hop}} = d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}} = 3 + 0 + 12 + 10 = 25 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the number of hops.**

$$d_{\text{end-end}} = N \cdot d_{\text{hop}} = 3 \times 25 = 75 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $d_{\text{end-end}} = 75$ ms.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Bottleneck Throughput

> **Problem:** A file transfer travels across a path with four links of rates $R_1 = 100$ Mbps, $R_2 = 10$ Mbps, $R_3 = 50$ Mbps, and $R_4 = 1$ Gbps. The file size is $F = 50$ MB. Find the end-to-end throughput and the total transfer time.

**Step 1 — Identify the bottleneck (minimum rate) link.**

$$\text{TP} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4) = \min(100, 10, 50, 1000) \text{ Mbps} = 10 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 2 — Convert file size to bits.**

$$F = 50 \text{ MB} \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/MB} = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Compute transfer time.**

$$T = \frac{F}{\text{TP}} = \frac{4 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 40 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:** Throughput = **10 Mbps**, Transfer time = **40 s**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Jitter Calculation

> **Problem:** Three consecutive packets are sent at $t = 0, 20, 40$ ms from the source. They arrive at the destination at $t = 47, 72, 88$ ms respectively. Calculate the jitter for the second and third packet relative to the first.

**Step 1 — Calculate end-to-end delays.**

$$d_1 = 47 - 0 = 47 \text{ ms}$$
$$d_2 = 72 - 20 = 52 \text{ ms}$$
$$d_3 = 88 - 40 = 48 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate inter-packet delay variation (jitter) at receiver.**

$$\Delta t_{\text{source}} = 20 \text{ ms}$$
$$\Delta t_{1 \to 2, \text{recv}} = 72 - 47 = 25 \text{ ms}$$
$$\Delta t_{2 \to 3, \text{recv}} = 88 - 72 = 16 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute jitter for each consecutive pair.**

$$J_{1 \to 2} = \vert 25 - 20 \vert = 5 \text{ ms}$$
$$J_{2 \to 3} = \vert 16 - 20 \vert = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $J_{1 \to 2} = 5$ ms, $J_{2 \to 3} = 4$ ms. The variation shows the **streaming application would need a de-jitter buffer** to smooth playback.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Delay Component Trade-offs

The following matrix summarizes how each delay component is affected by key network parameters. This is high-yield for KTU Module 1.

| Parameter Increases → ↓ Delay | Packet Size $L$ | Link Rate $R$ | Distance $d_{\text{phys}}$ | Traffic Intensity $I$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d_{\text{trans}} = L/R$ | ↑ Increases (linear) | ↓ Decreases (linear) | — (no effect) | — (no effect) |
| $d_{\text{prop}} = d_{\text{phys}}/s$ | — (no effect) | — (no effect) | ↑ Increases (linear) | — (no effect) |
| $d_{\text{queue}}$ | ↑ Increases (more bits) | ↓ Decreases (faster drain) | — (no effect) | ↑↑ Increases (non-linear, blows up near 1) |
| $d_{\text{proc}}$ | ↑ Slightly increases | ↓ Slightly decreases | — (no effect) | ↑ Slightly increases |
| $d_{\text{end-end}}$ | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑↑ Increases (queueing dominates) |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Application Sensitivity

| Application | Throughput Sensitivity | Delay Sensitivity | Jitter Sensitivity | Loss Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Email / FTP** | Medium | Low | Low | **High** |
| **Web Browsing** | Medium | Medium | Low | **High** |
| **Audio Streaming** | High | Medium | **High** | Medium |
| **Video Streaming** | **High** | Medium | **High** | Medium |
| **VoIP / Telephony** | Medium | **High** | **High** | Medium |
| **Online Gaming** | Low | **High** | **High** | Medium |
| **Banking / Transactions** | Low | Medium | Low | **High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight — The 3-Way Trade-off:** There is no free lunch in networking. Optimizing for throughput (e.g., larger packets) typically *increases delay and jitter*. Optimizing for low delay (e.g., smaller packets) *wastes bandwidth* and *increases processing overhead*. Packet loss recovery (via TCP retransmissions) *increases delay and reduces throughput*. These trade-offs are central to QoS engineering.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Packet Journey Across N Routers

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the path of a single packet from source to destination, highlighting the four delay components encountered at every hop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Host A] -- d_trans_1 --> B[Router 1]
    B -- d_prop_1 --> C[Link 1]
    C -- d_proc_1 --> D[Router 2]
    D -- d_trans_2 --> E[Link 2]
    E -- d_prop_2 --> F[Router 3]
    F -- d_trans_3 --> G[Link 3]
    G -- d_prop_3 --> H[Destination Host B]
    
    B -. d_queue .-> B
    D -. d_queue .-> D
    F -. d_queue .-> F
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style E fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

**How to read this diagram:**
* **Solid arrows** carry the packet forward and contribute transmission ($d_{\text{trans}}$) or propagation ($d_{\text{prop}}$) delay.
* **Dotted self-loops** on routers represent the **queueing delay** ($d_{\text{queue}}$) — the time the packet spends in the buffer.
* **Processing delay** ($d_{\text{proc}}$) is incurred inside each router (e.g., between arrival and queueing).

---

### 4.2 The Four Delay Components — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Packet Arrives at Router] --> Q1{Bit Error Check}
    Q1 --> Q2[Header Inspection]
    Q2 --> Q3[Routing Table Lookup]
    Q3 --> Q4[Determine Output Link]
    Q4 --> Q5[Place Packet in Output Buffer]
    Q5 --> Q6{Buffer Empty?}
    Q6 -- No --> Q7[Wait in Queue]
    Q7 --> Q6
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q8[Transmit Bits onto Link]
    Q8 --> Q9[Bits Propagate Across Medium]
    Q9 --> R[Packet Arrives at Next Node]
    
    Q1 -. d_proc .-> Q2
    Q5 -. d_queue .-> Q8
    Q8 -. d_trans .-> Q9
    Q9 -. d_prop .-> R
    
    style P fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style R fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style Q5 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style Q6 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style Q7 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style Q8 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style Q9 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Key Engineering Insight from this Diagram:**
* The **loop** between "Buffer Empty?" and "Wait in Queue" is the **exact visual representation** of $d_{\text{queue}}$. If many packets are circulating in this loop, you have high traffic intensity.
* The **transmission** and **propagation** stages are sequential *after* the queueing completes. This is why heavy traffic → longer queue → longer end-to-end delay.

---

### 4.3 Jitter Visualization — Inter-Packet Arrival Spacing

```mermaid
gantt
    title Jitter Visualization: Source vs Destination Packet Timings
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %S
    
    section Source Timeline
    Pkt 1 (sent at 0 ms)    :a1, 0, 20
    Pkt 2 (sent at 20 ms)   :a2, 20, 20
    Pkt 3 (sent at 40 ms)   :a3, 40, 20
    Pkt 4 (sent at 60 ms)   :a4, 60, 20
    Pkt 5 (sent at 80 ms)   :a5, 80, 20
    
    section Destination Timeline
    Pkt 1 (recv at 50 ms)   :b1, 50, 20
    Pkt 2 (recv at 78 ms)   :b2, 78, 20
    Pkt 3 (recv at 92 ms)   :b3, 92, 20
    Pkt 4 (recv at 130 ms)  :b4, 130, 20
    Pkt 5 (recv at 150 ms)  :b5, 150, 20
```

**Interpretation:**
* **Source spacing** is uniform at 20 ms (perfect cadence).
* **Destination spacing** is **non-uniform**: 28 ms, 14 ms, 38 ms, 20 ms.
* The **deviation** from the 20 ms source spacing is the jitter per packet pair.
* This irregular arrival pattern would cause **buffer underruns** in a real-time media player unless a **de-jitter buffer** absorbs the variation.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of QoS-Aware Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application Layer] --> GEN[Packet Generator]
    GEN --> QOS1{QoS Classifier}
    QOS1 -->|High Priority| Q1[Priority Queue 1]
    QOS1 -->|Medium Priority| Q2[Priority Queue 2]
    QOS1 -->|Best Effort| Q3[Best Effort Queue 3]
    
    Q1 --> SCHED[Packet Scheduler]
    Q2 --> SCHED
    Q3 --> SCHED
    
    SCHED --> TX[Transmitter]
    TX --> NET[Network Path with Delay Jitter Loss]
    NET --> RX[Receiver]
    RX --> DJB[De-Jitter Buffer]
    DJB --> APP2[Destination Application]
    
    NET -. Feedback to QOS Controller .-> QOS1
    
    style APP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style APP2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style Q1 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style Q2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style Q3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style DJB fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style NET fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242
```

**Engineering Takeaway:** Modern networks use **QoS classification and scheduling** to *bound* delay and jitter for high-priority flows (e.g., VoIP) while allowing best-effort traffic to use leftover capacity. This is the foundation of **IntServ, DiffServ, and MPLS-TE**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Quality of Service. List the four primary QoS metrics used to evaluate packet-switched networks.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Quality of Service (QoS)** refers to the measurable performance characteristics of a network service as perceived by the end user. It quantifies *how well* the network delivers packets in terms of speed, reliability, and consistency.
>
> The **four primary QoS metrics** in packet-switched networks are:
> 1. **Throughput** — the rate of successful data delivery (in bps).
> 2. **Delay (Latency)** — the time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination (in seconds).
> 3. **Packet Loss** — the fraction of packets that fail to reach the destination (in %).
> 4. **Jitter** — the variation in end-to-end delay between consecutive packets of the same flow (in seconds).

**[3 Marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four metrics with units]**

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between transmission delay and propagation delay with suitable formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Transmission Delay | Propagation Delay |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Time to push **all bits** of a packet onto the link | Time for **one bit** to travel the physical distance of the link |
| **Depends on** | Packet size $L$ and link rate $R$ | Physical distance $d$ and propagation speed $s$ |
| **Formula** | $d_{\text{trans}} = L / R$ | $d_{\text{prop}} = d / s$ |
| **Affected by** | Smaller $L$ or larger $R$ → smaller delay | Shorter distance or faster medium → smaller delay |
| **KTU Example** | Sending a 1500-byte packet on a 1 Mbps link: 12 ms | Sending a bit across 2000 km of fiber: 10 ms |

> *Transmission delay is incurred at the **transmitter**; propagation delay is incurred on the **medium**.*

**[3 Marks: 1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for correct formulas]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **four components of end-to-end delay** in a packet-switched network with their formulas. *(7 marks)*
