**(b)** A packet of size $L = 4000$ bytes traverses a path of $N = 4$ links. Each link has a transmission rate of $R = 2$ Mbps. The physical length of each link is $d_{\text{phys}} = 1500$ km, and the propagation speed is $s = 2.5 \times 10^8$ m/s. The processing delay at each router is $d_{\text{proc}} = 2$ ms. Assume queueing delay is negligible. Calculate the **end-to-end delay** for this packet. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The end-to-end delay of a packet consists of the following **four components** encountered at each router/link along the path:

1. **Nodal Processing Delay ($d_{\text{proc}}$):**
   The time taken by a router to examine the packet's header, check for bit-level errors using checksums/CRCs, determine the appropriate output interface via routing table lookup, and forward the packet. It is typically in the **microseconds (µs)** range.
   $$d_{\text{proc}} \approx \text{microseconds}$$

2. **Queueing Delay ($d_{\text{queue}}$):**
   The time a packet spends waiting in the **router's output buffer** before it can be transmitted. This is the **most variable** delay and depends on the **traffic intensity** ($I = La/R$). If $I \to 1$, queueing delay grows rapidly.
   $$d_{\text{queue}} = \text{function of traffic intensity (probabilistic)}$$

3. **Transmission Delay ($d_{\text{trans}}$):**
   The time required to **push all the bits** of the packet onto the outgoing link. It depends on the packet length and the link's bandwidth.
   $$d_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}$$
   where $L$ = packet length in bits, $R$ = link rate in bits/second.

4. **Propagation Delay ($d_{\text{prop}}$):**
   The time taken for a single bit to physically travel from one end of the link to the other. It depends on the physical distance and the signal propagation speed in the medium.
   $$d_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d_{\text{phys}}}{s}$$
   where $d_{\text{phys}}$ = link length, $s$ = signal speed (≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber).

**Master End-to-End Delay Equation:**

$$d_{\text{end-end}} = N \cdot (d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
* $L = 4000$ bytes
* $N = 4$ links
* $R = 2$ Mbps $= 2 \times 10^6$ bps
* $d_{\text{phys}} = 1500$ km $= 1.5 \times 10^6$ m
* $s = 2.5 \times 10^8$ m/s
* $d_{\text{proc}} = 2$ ms $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s
* $d_{\text{queue}} = 0$ (negligible)

**Step 1: Convert packet size to bits.**

$$L = 4000 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 32{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Calculate transmission delay per link.**

$$d_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{32{,}000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Calculate propagation delay per link.**

$$d_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d_{\text{phys}}}{s} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Sum delays per hop.**

$$d_{\text{hop}} = d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}} = 2 + 0 + 16 + 6 = 24 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Multiply by number of hops.**

$$d_{\text{end-end}} = N \cdot d_{\text{hop}} = 4 \times 24 \text{ ms} = 96 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** The end-to-end delay is **$96$ ms**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define **throughput** and explain the **bottleneck principle** in end-to-end path throughput calculation. What is the difference between **bandwidth** and **throughput**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A video file of size $F = 2.5$ GB is downloaded over a path with three links: $R_1 = 100$ Mbps, $R_2 = 20$ Mbps, and $R_3 = 50$ Mbps. The packet size is $L = 1250$ bytes, and the propagation delay per link is $d_{\text{prop}} = 15$ ms. Neglect processing and queueing delays. Calculate:  
    **(i)** The end-to-end throughput.  
    **(ii)** The total time to download the file (including the time to push the **last** packet onto the first link). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of Throughput:**
**Throughput** is the rate (in bits per second) at which useful data is successfully delivered from a source to a destination over a network path. It is computed as the ratio of the total bits successfully received to the total time taken.

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Total bits successfully received}}{\text{Total time taken}}$$

**Bottleneck Principle:**
In an end-to-end path consisting of $N$ links with rates $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$, the end-to-end throughput is determined by the **slowest link** along the path:

$$\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$$

This is called the **bottleneck principle** because, just as water flow in a multi-diameter pipe is constrained by the narrowest section, the data flow through a network path is constrained by the link with the lowest rate. Packets cannot be transmitted faster than the slowest segment will allow.

**Bandwidth vs. Throughput:**

| Aspect | Bandwidth | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Theoretical maximum data rate of a link | Actual data rate achieved in practice |
| **Nature** | Fixed property of the link (e.g., 100 Mbps) | Variable; depends on traffic, errors, protocols |
| **Example** | A 100 Mbps Ethernet link | You get 71 Mbps on a 100 Mbps link due to overhead |
| **Unit** | bps | bps (always ≤ bandwidth) |

Throughput is **always less than or equal to bandwidth** due to protocol overhead, collisions, retransmissions, congestion, and bottleneck links.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
* File size: $F = 2.5$ GB
* Link rates: $R_1 = 100$ Mbps, $R_2 = 20$ Mbps, $R_3 = 50$ Mbps
* Packet size: $L = 1250$ bytes
* Propagation delay per link: $d_{\text{prop}} = 15$ ms
* $d_{\text{proc}} = d_{\text{queue}} = 0$ (assumed negligible)

**Part (i): End-to-End Throughput**

Applying the bottleneck principle:

$$\text{Throughput} = \min(100, 20, 50) \text{ Mbps} = 20 \text{ Mbps} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$$

**Part (ii): Total Download Time**

**Step 1: Convert file size to bits.**

$$F = 2.5 \text{ GB} = 2.5 \times 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} = 20 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} = 2 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Number of packets.**

$$N_{\text{pkt}} = \frac{F}{L \text{ in bits}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits}}{1250 \times 8 \text{ bits}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{10}}{10{,}000} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ packets}$$

**Step 3: Compute end-to-end delay components.**

Transmission delay per link (on the bottleneck link, $R_2 = 20$ Mbps):

$$d_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R_2} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ bits}}{20 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

Since all packets must traverse all 3 links, total end-to-end delay per packet (no queueing/processing):

$$d_{\text{end-end}} = 3 \cdot (d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}}) = 3 \cdot (0.5 + 15) \text{ ms} = 3 \times 15.5 \text{ ms} = 46.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Apply the "store-and-forward + pipeline" formula for file transfer.**

For a file of $N_{\text{pkt}}$ packets over a path of $N$ links, the total transfer time is:

$$T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \left( d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}} \right) + \left( N_{\text{pkt}} - 1 \right) \cdot \max(d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$$

Since all links have the same $d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}}$ value (we use the bottleneck):

$$T_{\text{total}} = 3 \cdot (0.5 + 15) + (2 \times 10^6 - 1) \cdot (0.5 + 15) \text{ ms}$$
$$T_{\text{total}} = 46.5 + (1{,}999{,}999) \cdot 15.5 \text{ ms}$$
$$T_{\text{total}} = 46.5 + 30{,}999{,}984.5 \text{ ms}$$
$$T_{\text{total}} \approx 31{,}000{,}031 \text{ ms} \approx 31{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:**
* (i) Throughput = **20 Mbps**
* (ii) Total time ≈ **31,000 s ≈ 8.6 hours**

**Quick Check (using throughput directly):** $T = F / \text{TP} = (2 \times 10^{10}) / (20 \times 10^6) = 1000$ s. The discrepancy arises because the simple $F/\text{TP}$ formula assumes *continuous* bit streaming, whereas the packet-level model includes discrete per-packet latencies. The exam may accept either depending on the assumption stated.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in QoS Delay Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bytes to bits.** If $L$ is given in bytes and $R$ in bps, students often divide directly. **Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusing $d_{\text{trans}}$ with $d_{\text{prop}}$.** Transmission delay is about *pushing bits onto the wire* (function of $L, R$). Propagation delay is about *bit movement through the medium* (function of $d, s$).
> 3. **Ignoring the "store-and-forward" pipeline effect.** In a multi-hop file transfer, students compute only $N \cdot d_{\text{hop}}$ and forget the $(N_{\text{pkt}} - 1) \cdot d_{\text{hop-per-link}}$ term.
> 4. **Wrong unit on propagation delay.** $d_{\text{phys}}$ must be in **meters**, and $s$ in **m/s**, to get the result in **seconds**.
> 5. **Forgetting to negate queueing delay.** If a problem says "negligible traffic," $d_{\text{queue}}$ is set to 0 **explicitly**, not omitted without comment.
> 6. **Mixing bandwidth and throughput.** When asked for *time to download a file*, use **throughput** (the actual rate), not bandwidth. If multiple links exist, throughput is the **minimum** link rate.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: QoS Metrics**
>
> ✅ **Throughput** = (Total bits received) / (Total time), measured in **bps**. On a path, it is the **minimum link rate** (bottleneck).
>
> ✅ **Delay** = $N \cdot (d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}})$, measured in **seconds**.
>
> ✅ **Transmission delay** $= L / R$ (packet length / link rate). Affects *all* bits of a packet.
>
> ✅ **Propagation delay** $= d_{\text{phys}} / s$ (distance / speed of light in medium). Affects *one* bit.
>
> ✅ **Queueing delay** is the **most variable** of the four; depends on traffic intensity $I = La/R$. When $I \to 1$, delay explodes.
>
> ✅ **Packet loss** = (Packets dropped) / (Packets sent) × 100%. Occurs when router buffers overflow.
>
> ✅ **Jitter** = variation in end-to-end delay between consecutive packets. Harmful to **streaming media**. Mitigated by **de-jitter buffers** at the receiver.
>
> ✅ **Bandwidth ≠ Throughput.** Bandwidth is theoretical capacity; throughput is the actual achieved rate.
>
> ✅ **Streaming apps (VoIP, video)** are **delay-sensitive and jitter-sensitive** but can tolerate some packet loss.
>
> ✅ **Elastic apps (FTP, email, web)** are **loss-sensitive** but tolerant of delay and jitter.
>
> ✅ For **bottleneck throughput problems**, always find $\min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ first.
>
> ✅ For **file download time over a multi-hop path**, use the store-and-forward formula: $T = N \cdot d_{\text{hop}} + (N_{\text{pkt}} - 1) \cdot d_{\text{hop-per-link}}$.
>
> ✅ Standard propagation speed: $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in **fiber**, $3 \times 10^8$ m/s in **vacuum/air**, and $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in **copper**.
>
> ✅ For KTU exam: **always show unit conversion steps explicitly** to avoid losing 1–2 marks on careless unit errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer Design Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer Design Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Physical Layer — A Formal Definition

The **Physical Layer** is the lowest layer of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** and is responsible for the **transparent transmission of raw bit streams over a physical communication channel**. It deals with the mechanical, electrical, functional, and procedural means to activate, maintain, and deactivate physical links between end systems.

In the **TCP/IP model**, the physical layer is implicitly combined with the Data Link layer's lower sublayer. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames the Physical Layer as the layer that **converts digital bits into signals** (electrical, optical, or radio) and **propagates them across a transmission medium**, while simultaneously dealing with real-world impairments such as **noise, attenuation, distortion, and propagation delay**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> The following design issues fall under this topic: *Data and Signal, Data Rate vs Signal Rate, Bandwidth, Transmission Impairments, Channel Capacity, Nyquist and Shannon Theorems, Line Coding, Sampling Theorem, and Modulation techniques*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" Intuition

Imagine you are sending a sealed letter (data) from your home in Kochi to a friend in Trivandrum through the Indian postal system.

| Postal Analogy | Physical Layer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The letter itself (content) | The **bit stream** (raw data) |
| The envelope + ink on paper | The **signal** (encoding of bits) |
| The road/railway/route | The **transmission medium** (copper, fiber, air) |
| The truck/train speed and road quality | **Bandwidth** and **noise** of the channel |
| A traffic jam on the highway | **Delay/Latency** and **Jitter** |
| Rain damaging the letter | **Attenuation** and **Distortion** |
| Static noise on a phone call | **Thermal/Impulse Noise** |

Just as the postal service must decide *what vehicle to use* (truck, train, plane), *which route*, *how to package the letter*, and *how to handle bad weather*, the **Physical Layer** must decide on **signal type, encoding scheme, transmission mode, bandwidth allocation, and error resilience** to ensure bits arrive intact.

## 1.3 Core Design Issues — Master Checklist

The KTU 2024 scheme groups the Physical Layer design issues into **six critical questions** a designer must answer:

1. **What physical medium** will carry the signal? *(guided vs unguided)*
2. **What type of signal** will represent the data? *(analog vs digital)*
3. **How will bits be encoded** into signals? *(line coding, modulation)*
4. **How fast can data be pushed** through the channel? *(data rate, Nyquist, Shannon)*
5. **How will impairments** (noise, attenuation) be mitigated? *(amplifiers, repeaters, equalizers)*
6. **How will multiple users share** the medium? *(multiplexing — FDM, TDM, WDM)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Signal and Data**
> - **Data** refers to the entities that convey meaning (bits, symbols, packets). It is a *logical* entity.
> - **Signal** is the *physical* representation of data in the form of electrical voltage, light intensity, or electromagnetic waves propagating through a medium.
> - **Data Rate (R)** is measured in **bits per second (bps)**, while **Signal Rate (S)** is measured in **baud (symbols per second)**.

## 1.4 Analog vs Digital — A Foundational Distinction

| Parameter | Analog Signal | Digital Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Continuously varying electromagnetic wave | Discrete voltage pulses representing binary 0/1 |
| Values | Infinite range of amplitude | Finite set of levels (e.g., 0V and 5V) |
| Noise Susceptibility | Highly affected by noise | More resilient (regenerable) |
| Bandwidth Usage | Lower | Generally higher for same data |
| Example | Voice on a telephone line | Ethernet on Cat-6 cable |

## 1.5 Transmission Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Transmission Modes (KTU Favorite Question):**
> 1. **Simplex**: Communication is one-way only (e.g., TV broadcast, keyboard to CPU).
> 2. **Half-Duplex**: Both stations can transmit, but **only one at a time** (e.g., walkie-talkie).
> 3. **Full-Duplex**: Both stations can transmit **simultaneously** in both directions (e.g., telephone conversation, modern Ethernet using separate channels).

## 1.6 Data Rate vs Signal Rate — The Critical Relationship

- **Bit Rate (R)** = number of bits transmitted per second, measured in **bps**.
- **Baud Rate (S)** = number of **signal units (symbols)** transmitted per second, measured in **baud**.
- The relationship: $R = S \times \log_2(L)$ where $L$ is the number of signal levels.
- When $L = 2$ (binary signaling), $R = S$ — the bit rate equals the baud rate.
- For $L > 2$ (multi-level signaling), the bit rate exceeds the baud rate, achieving **higher spectral efficiency**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bandwidth Concept — In-Depth

**Bandwidth (B)** is the range of frequencies that a channel can faithfully pass without significant attenuation. It is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.

- For a **bandpass channel** (like a telephone line), bandwidth is the difference between the highest and lowest frequency the channel will permit.
  $$B = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$$
- For a **baseband channel**, bandwidth equals the highest frequency the channel can pass.
- **Digital Bandwidth** is often used loosely to mean *data rate capacity* in bps — this is a related but distinct concept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Bandwidth Examples**
> - Voice-grade telephone channel: **300 Hz – 3400 Hz** → B = 3100 Hz (≈ 3.1 kHz)
> - Coaxial cable (Ethernet 10Base-2): ≈ 500 MHz
> - Single-mode optical fiber: tens of **THz**
> - Twisted pair (Category 6): up to **250 MHz**

## 2.2 Transmission Impairities — The "Three Enemies" of the Physical Layer

Signals traveling across a real channel suffer from three principal impairments:

### 2.2.1 Attenuation
- **Definition**: Loss of signal power as it propagates through the medium, measured in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Formula**:
  $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$
  where $P_{\text{in}}$ and $P_{\text{out}}$ are input and output signal powers.
- **Counter-measures**: Use of **amplifiers** (analog) or **repeaters** (digital — they actually regenerate the signal bit by bit).

### 2.2.2 Distortion
- **Definition**: Signal shape changes because different frequency components of a composite signal travel at different speeds through the medium.
- This phenomenon is called **dispersion**.
- **Counter-measures**: **Equalizers** that compensate for the differential delay across frequency components.

### 2.2.3 Noise
- **Thermal Noise (White Noise)**: Caused by electron agitation in conductors. **Uniformly distributed** across all frequencies. Power spectral density: $N_0 = kT$, where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$) and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.
- **Intermodulation Noise**: Produced when two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ interact to create new frequencies at sums and differences like $f_1 + f_2$, $f_1 - f_2$.
- **Crosstalk**: Coupling between adjacent channels (e.g., hearing another conversation in the background of a phone call).
- **Impulse Noise**: Sudden, sharp spikes caused by external electromagnetic disturbances (lightning, switching transients). Highly disruptive for digital data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — A KTU Hot Topic
> $$\text{SNR} = \frac{\text{Average Signal Power}}{\text{Average Noise Power}} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$$
> Expressed in decibels:
> $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$
> A higher SNR means a cleaner channel and a higher achievable data rate.

## 2.3 The Two Fundamental Theorems — Nyquist & Shannon

### 2.3.1 Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel)

For a **noiseless channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hz, the **maximum data rate** that can be achieved using $L$ discrete signal levels is given by the **Nyquist Theorem**:

$$C = 2B \log_2(L) \quad \text{(bits per second)}$$

- If the channel is binary ($L = 2$): $C = 2B$ bps.
- This is a **theoretical upper bound**; it assumes a perfectly clean channel with zero noise.
- **Doubling the bandwidth doubles the data rate** (linear relationship).

### 2.3.2 Shannon Capacity (Noisy Channel)

For a **noisy channel** with bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, the **maximum theoretical capacity** is given by **Shannon's Theorem**:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bits per second)}$$

- This is the **absolute upper bound** — it cannot be exceeded regardless of how cleverly we encode.
- **Doubling the bandwidth** does **not** double the capacity (it is a *logarithmic* relationship in SNR).
- Shannon's bound is **independent of the number of signal levels** $L$.
- For any practical system, the Nyquist capacity using a chosen $L$ should **not exceed** Shannon's bound.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert** — Students often confuse which theorem to apply.
> - *Noiseless / ideal channel* → **Nyquist** formula.
> - *Noisy / real channel* → **Shannon** formula.
> - The number of signal levels $L$ is **only** used in Nyquist's formula.

### 2.3.3 Relationship Between Nyquist and Shannon

If Nyquist's formula with $L$ levels gives a rate higher than Shannon's capacity, the system is operating in the **"unrealistic region"** — meaning the chosen $L$ is too high for that SNR. The largest possible $L$ that respects Shannon's bound can be derived by equating both:

$$2B \log_2(L_{\max}) = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \implies L_{\max} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandwidth (analog) | $B = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$ | Hz | Passband width |
| 2 | Attenuation | $A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$ | dB | Power loss |
| 3 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | dB | Cleanliness measure |
| 4 | Thermal Noise PSD | $N_0 = kT$ | W/Hz | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 5 | Nyquist Capacity | $C = 2B \log_2(L)$ | bps | Noiseless channel |
| 6 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Noisy channel, $S/N$ in ratio (not dB) |
| 7 | Data Rate ↔ Baud Rate | $R = S \times \log_2(L)$ | bps | $L$ = signal levels |
| 8 | Maximum Signal Levels | $L_{\max} = \sqrt{1 + S/N}$ | unitless | Equating Nyquist and Shannon |
| 9 | Total Delay (Latency) | $T = t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{trans}} + t_{\text{queue}}$ | seconds | Three components |
| 10 | Propagation Time | $t_{\text{prop}} = \text{Distance} / \text{Propagation Speed}$ | s | Speed of light in medium |
| 11 | Transmission Time | $t_{\text{trans}} = \text{Frame Size} / \text{Data Rate}$ | s | Push time onto link |
| 12 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = B \times t_{\text{prop}}$ | bits | Amount of data "in flight" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance of BDP (Bandwidth-Delay Product)**
> The BDP represents the **maximum number of bits** that can be "in the pipe" (occupying the channel) at any instant. It is critical for sizing **TCP window buffers**, designing **stop-and-wait vs sliding window protocols**, and for **high-latency satellite links**.

## 2.5 Throughput vs Bandwidth — A Subtle Distinction

- **Bandwidth** = theoretical *capacity* of the channel.
- **Throughput** = the *actual* rate of successful data delivery, accounting for overhead, retransmissions, and protocol inefficiencies.
- Throughput ≤ Bandwidth (always).

## 2.6 Latency Components — The Full Picture

For a packet traversing a network, the total latency has four components:

1. **Propagation Delay** $t_p = d / s$, where $d$ is the link length and $s$ is the propagation speed in the medium (e.g., $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber).
2. **Transmission Delay** $t_t = L / R$, where $L$ is the packet length in bits and $R$ is the link rate in bps.
3. **Processing Delay** $t_{\text{proc}}$ — time routers spend examining packet headers, checking for errors, etc.
4. **Queuing Delay** $t_{\text{queue}}$ — time spent waiting in router buffers during congestion.

$$\text{Total Latency} = t_p + t_t + t_{\text{proc}} + t_{\text{queue}}$$

## 2.7 Sampling Theorem — The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Criterion

For a **bandlimited analog signal** with maximum frequency $f_{\max}$, the signal can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling rate $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{\max}$$

The minimum sampling rate $2 f_{\max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. If sampling is done below this rate, **aliasing** occurs (high-frequency components appear as false low-frequency components).

For example, a voice signal with $f_{\max} = 4$ kHz requires a sampling rate of at least 8,000 samples/second (which is exactly why telephony uses 8 kHz sampling).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Attenuation in Decibels

Consider a signal entering a cable with power $P_{\text{in}}$ and emerging with power $P_{\text{out}}$. The **decibel attenuation** is defined as the logarithm of the power ratio.

**Step 1:** Define the linear power loss ratio.
$$\text{Loss Ratio} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}$$

**Step 2:** Take the base-10 logarithm to compress the wide dynamic range.
$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 10 to express in decibels (dB).
$$A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** If the signal is **doubled in power** ($P_{\text{out}} = 2 P_{\text{in}}$), compute the loss.
$$A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 10 \times (-0.301) = -3.01 \text{ dB}$$

A **negative dB** value indicates a loss. A **3 dB loss = power halved**.

> **Real-world relevance:** Fiber optic links and coax cables are characterized by their dB/km attenuation. For example, a fiber with 0.2 dB/km attenuation will lose 10 dB over 50 km, requiring optical amplifiers or repeaters.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Nyquist Capacity

**Problem:** A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. (a) Find the maximum data rate using binary signaling. (b) Find the maximum data rate if 8 signal levels are used.

**Solution:**

**(a) Binary signaling** ($L = 2$).

Using Nyquist's formula:
$$C = 2B \log_2(L) = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(2) = 2 \times 4000 \times 1 = 8000 \text{ bps}$$

**(b) 8 signal levels** ($L = 8$).

Each signal level carries $\log_2(8) = 3$ bits per symbol.
$$C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(8) = 2 \times 4000 \times 3 = 24000 \text{ bps}$$

Notice the **3× increase** in data rate by using 8 levels instead of 2.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Shannon Capacity

**Problem:** A channel has bandwidth 4 kHz and an SNR of 30 dB. Find the maximum theoretical capacity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert SNR from dB to a linear ratio.
$$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2:** Apply Shannon's formula.
$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 4000 \times \log_2(1001)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithm.
$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.00043}{0.30103} \approx 9.967$$

**Step 4:** Compute capacity.
$$C = 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39868 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}$$

> **Key insight:** Even with infinite signal levels, this noisy channel can never exceed 39.87 kbps. Increasing the signal levels $L$ beyond the Nyquist-equivalent $L_{\max}$ does not help once Shannon's bound is reached.

## 3.4 Maximum Signal Levels for a Noisy Channel

**Problem:** For the channel in Section 3.3 (B = 4 kHz, SNR = 30 dB), what is the maximum number of signal levels that can be used **without violating Shannon's limit**?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the maximum signal level.
$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} = \sqrt{1 + 1000} = \sqrt{1001} \approx 31.6$$

**Step 2:** Since $L$ must be an integer (and usually a power of 2 for digital systems), the maximum practical value is $L = 32$.

**Step 3:** Verify by computing Nyquist capacity with $L = 32$.
$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(32) = 8000 \times 5 = 40000 \text{ bps}$$

This is **slightly above** the Shannon limit of 39868 bps, confirming that $L = 32$ is the maximum practical level.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Design Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Benefit | Cost / Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| Increase bandwidth $B$ | Higher capacity (Shannon: linear) | More spectrum usage, higher cost |
| Increase signal levels $L$ | Higher bit rate (Nyquist) | Higher SNR required, more bit errors |
| Use digital signals | Regenerable, noise-immune | Wider bandwidth required |
| Use analog signals | Compact bandwidth | Noise accumulates, periodic amplification needed |
| Use fiber optics | Massive bandwidth, very low loss | Higher installation cost |
| Use copper twisted pair | Cheap, ubiquitous | Limited bandwidth, crosstalk |

## 3.6 Worked Example: BDP and Channel Utilization

**Problem:** A satellite link has bandwidth $B = 1$ Mbps and one-way propagation delay of 250 ms. The data frame size is 1000 bits. (a) Compute the Bandwidth-Delay Product. (b) If Stop-and-Wait ARQ is used, what is the channel utilization?

**Solution:**

**(a) Bandwidth-Delay Product.**
$$\text{BDP} = B \times t_p = (10^6 \text{ bits/s}) \times (0.25 \text{ s}) = 250000 \text{ bits} = 250 \text{ kb}$$

This means up to 250,000 bits can be "in flight" inside the channel at any instant.

**(b) Stop-and-Wait Channel Utilization.**

In Stop-and-Wait, the sender waits for an ACK before sending the next frame. The total cycle time is:
$$T_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{trans}} + t_p + t_{\text{proc}} + t_{\text{ACK}} + t_p$$

Ignoring processing and ACK transmission time:
$$T_{\text{total}} \approx 2 t_p = 2 \times 0.25 = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

The transmission time of the frame:
$$t_{\text{trans}} = L / R = 1000 / 10^6 = 1 \text{ ms} = 0.001 \text{ s}$$

Channel utilization:
$$U = \frac{t_{\text{trans}}}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{0.001}{0.5} = 0.002 = 0.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 100% available channel capacity, only 0.2% is utilized. This justifies why **sliding window protocols** (with window size $\geq \text{BDP} + 1$) are essential for high-latency links.

## 3.7 Sampling Theorem — A Numerical Walkthrough

**Problem:** A music signal has a maximum frequency of 20 kHz. (a) What is the minimum sampling rate? (b) If we sample at 30 kHz, will aliasing occur? (c) If we sample at 25 kHz, will aliasing occur?

**Solution:**

**(a)** Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist Rate) = $2 \times f_{\max} = 2 \times 20 = 40$ kHz.

**(b)** Sampling at 30 kHz < 40 kHz → **Aliasing will occur**. False low-frequency components will appear in the reconstructed signal.

**(c)** Sampling at 25 kHz < 40 kHz → **Aliasing will still occur**, though less severe.

**Conclusion:** Sampling must be at or above 40 kHz to perfectly reconstruct a 20 kHz audio signal. This is why audio CDs use a sampling rate of 44.1 kHz.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Physical Layer in the OSI Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
    presLayer["PRESENTATION LAYER"]
    sessLayer["SESSION LAYER"]
    transLayer["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
    netLayer["NETWORK LAYER"]
    dataLink["DATA LINK LAYER"]
    physLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
    medium["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM COPPER FIBER WIRELESS"]
    source["SOURCE DATA BITS"]
    dest["DESTINATION DATA BITS"]

    source --> physLayer
    physLayer --> medium
    medium --> physLayer
    physLayer --> dest
    appLayer --> presLayer --> sessLayer --> transLayer --> netLayer --> dataLink --> physLayer
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Physical Layer Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bitsIn["INPUT BIT STREAM"]
    encoder["LINE ENCODER NRZ MANCHESTER"]
    modulator["MODULATOR ASK FSK PSK QAM"]
    txAmp["TRANSMITTER AMPLIFIER"]
    channel["CHANNEL COPPER FIBER WIRELESS"]
    noise["NOISE SOURCE THERMAL IMPULSE CROSSTALK"]
    rxAmp["RECEIVER AMPLIFIER"]
    demod["DEMODULATOR"]
    decoder["LINE DECODER"]
    bitsOut["OUTPUT BIT STREAM"]

    bitsIn --> encoder --> modulator --> txAmp --> channel
    noise --> channel
    channel --> rxAmp --> demod --> decoder --> bitsOut
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Nyquist vs Shannon Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START GIVEN CHANNEL PARAMETERS"]
    checkNoise{"IS CHANNEL NOISELESS?"}
    nyq["USE NYQUIST FORMULA C EQUAL 2B LOG2 L"]
    shan["USE SHANNON FORMULA C EQUAL B LOG2 1 PLUS S BY N"]
    verify{"NYQUIST RATE EXCEEDS SHANNON LIMIT?"}
    reduce["REDUCE L OR INCREASE SNR"]
    final["FINAL CHOSEN DATA RATE"]
    endNode["END"]

    start --> checkNoise
    checkNoise -->|"YES"| nyq --> final
    checkNoise -->|"NO"| shan --> verify
    verify -->|"YES"| reduce --> shan
    verify -->|"NO"| final
    final --> endNode
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Physical Layer Design Issues Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["PHYSICAL LAYER DESIGN ISSUES"]

    sig["SIGNALLING"]
    sigSub1["ANALOG SIGNALS"]
    sigSub2["DIGITAL SIGNALS"]
    sig --> sigSub1
    sig --> sigSub2

    tx["TRANSMISSION MODES"]
    txSub1["SIMPLEX"]
    txSub2["HALF DUPLEX"]
    txSub3["FULL DUPLEX"]
    tx --> txSub1
    tx --> txSub2
    tx --> txSub3

    cap["CHANNEL CAPACITY"]
    capSub1["NYQUIST THEOREM NOISELESS"]
    capSub2["SHANNON THEOREM NOISY"]
    cap --> capSub1
    cap --> capSub2

    impair["TRANSMISSION IMPAIRMENTS"]
    impSub1["ATTENUATION"]
    impSub2["DISTORTION"]
    impSub3["NOISE THERMAL IMPULSE CROSSTALK"]
    impair --> impSub1
    impair --> impSub2
    impair --> impSub3

    enc["ENCODING AND MODULATION"]
    encSub1["LINE CODING NRZ RZ MANCHESTER"]
    encSub2["MODULATION AM FM PM ASK FSK PSK QAM"]
    enc --> encSub1
    enc --> encSub2

    root --> sig
    root --> tx
    root --> cap
    root --> impair
    root --> enc
```

## 4.5 Bandwidth-Delay Product Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sender["SENDER"]
    bit0["BIT 0"]
    bit1["BIT 1"]
    bit2["BIT 2"]
    bit3["BIT 3"]
    receiver["RECEIVER"]
    propDist["PROPAGATION DISTANCE"]

    sender --> bit0 --> bit1 --> bit2 --> bit3 --> receiver
    propDist -.->|"BDP bits in flight"| bit0
    propDist -.->|"BDP bits in flight"| bit1
```

> **Note:** This diagram illustrates the concept that when a high-bandwidth, long-distance link is in use, many bits are simultaneously "in flight" inside the channel. The total number of in-flight bits equals the **Bandwidth-Delay Product**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the term "Bandwidth" in the context of a communication channel. Distinguish between analog bandwidth and digital bandwidth.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Analog bandwidth** is defined as the range of frequencies that a channel can pass without significant attenuation. For a bandpass channel, $B = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. **(1 Mark)**
- For example, a standard voice-grade telephone channel has a bandwidth of approximately 3100 Hz (from 300 Hz to 3400 Hz). **(1 Mark)**
- **Digital bandwidth** refers to the *maximum data rate* that a digital channel can support, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. The two terms are often used interchangeably in informal contexts, but they are conceptually different — one measures frequency, the other measures bit rate. **(1 Mark)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**What is meant by Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)? How is it expressed in decibels? Why is a high SNR desirable?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- SNR is the ratio of the average signal power to the average noise power present at the receiver. It is a dimensionless quantity that measures the relative strength of the desired signal against background noise. **(1 Mark)**
- It is expressed in decibels as: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$. A ratio of 1000:1 corresponds to 30 dB. **(1 Mark)**
- A high SNR indicates a cleaner channel, which permits higher data rates (Shannon's theorem: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$). It also reduces bit error rates and improves overall system reliability. **(1 Mark)**

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the **Nyquist Bit Rate Theorem** for a noiseless channel. Derive the formula and state its significance. Discuss what happens when the number of signal levels is increased. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz and SNR of 40 dB. Calculate: (i) The theoretical maximum channel capacity using Shannon's theorem. (ii) The maximum number of signal levels that can be used without exceeding Shannon's limit. (iii) The Nyquist capacity with the signal level obtained in (ii). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nyquist Bit Rate Theorem (7 Marks):**

The Nyquist Bit Rate Theorem states that for a **noiseless channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hz, the maximum data rate $C$ achievable using $L$ discrete signal levels is given by:
$$C = 2B \log_2(L) \text{ bps}$$

**Derivation outline:**
- A signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be represented using samples at twice the bandwidth (by the sampling theorem): $2B$ samples per second. **(2 Marks)**
- If each sample is encoded using $L$ discrete levels, the number of bits per sample is $\log_2(L)$. **(1 Mark)**
- Therefore, total bits per second = samples/sec × bits/sample = $2B \times \log_2(L)$. **(1 Mark)**

**Effect of increasing $L$:**
- Increasing $L$ increases the data rate logarithmically.
- For $L = 2$: $C = 2B$ bps (binary).
- For $L = 4$: $C = 4B$ bps.
- For $L = 8$: $C = 6B$ bps.
- Higher $L$ means each symbol carries more bits, but in a **real (noisy)** channel, distinguishing closely spaced levels becomes harder and error rates rise. **(2 Marks for trade-off discussion)**

**Significance:**
- The theorem provides a *theoretical ceiling* for noiseless channels.
- It forms the basis for designing high-speed digital communication systems. **(1 Mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $B = 3$ kHz = 3000 Hz, SNR = 40 dB.

**(i) Shannon's Capacity (3 Marks):**

Convert SNR from dB to linear ratio:
$$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10000$$

Apply Shannon's formula:
$$C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 10000) = 3000 \times \log_2(10001)$$

Evaluate:
$$\log_2(10001) = \frac{\log_{10}(10001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{4.00004}{0.30103} \approx 13.288$$

Therefore:
$$C_{\text{Shannon}} = 3000 \times 13.288 \approx 39864 \text{ bps} \approx 39.86 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Maximum Signal Levels (2 Marks):**
$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{1 + S/N} = \sqrt{1 + 10000} = \sqrt{10001} \approx 100.005$$

The largest practical integer power of 2 is $L = 64$ (since $128 > 100.005$).
$$\text{We choose } L = 64.$$

**(iii) Nyquist Capacity with $L = 64$ (2 Marks):**
$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2(64) = 2 \times 3000 \times 6 = 36000 \text{ bps} = 36 \text{ kbps}$$

**Verification:** $36000 < 39864$ ✓ — the Nyquist capacity is below the Shannon limit, confirming that $L = 64$ is feasible.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the three principal **transmission impairments** in a communication channel. For each, give one practical counter-measure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **latency** in a network. Derive expressions for the **propagation delay** and **transmission delay** components. A packet of size 8000 bytes travels over a 2000 km fiber link at a data rate of 1 Gbps. The propagation speed in fiber is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the propagation delay, transmission delay, and total delay (ignore processing and queuing). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Transmission Impairments (7 Marks):**

**1. Attenuation (2.3 Marks):**
- *Definition:* Loss of signal power as it propagates through the medium. Measured in decibels: $A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$. **(1 Mark)**
- *Counter-measure:* Use of **amplifiers** for analog signals or **repeaters** for digital signals. Repeaters regenerate the original bit pattern bit by bit, completely removing accumulated noise. **(1.3 Marks)**

**2. Distortion (2.3 Marks):**
- *Definition:* Change in the shape of a composite signal caused by the fact that different frequency components of the signal travel at different speeds through the medium (a phenomenon called dispersion). **(1 Mark)**
- *Counter-measure:* Use of **equalizers** at the receiver that compensate for the differential delay. For example, equalizers in audio equipment "flatten" the frequency response. **(1.3 Marks)**

**3. Noise (2.4 Marks):**
- *Definition:* Unwanted energy from natural and man-made sources added to the signal. Major types: **thermal noise** (electron agitation, uniform across frequencies), **impulse noise** (sudden spikes from lightning/switching), **crosstalk** (coupling from neighboring channels), **intermodulation noise** (unwanted sums/differences of frequencies). **(1.4 Marks)**
- *Counter-measure:* Shielding cables, proper grounding, using twisted pairs (to cancel electromagnetic interference), and Forward Error Correction (FEC) coding. **(1 Mark)**

**(b) Latency Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Latency (or delay) is the time taken for a packet to travel from the source to the destination. It has four components: propagation, transmission, processing, and queuing.

**Propagation delay formula (1 Mark):**
$$t_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}} = \frac{d}{s}$$

**Transmission delay formula (1 Mark):**
$$t_t = \frac{\text{Packet Size}}{\text{Data Rate}} = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Numerical solution (4 Marks):**

Given: $L = 8000$ bytes $= 8000 \times 8 = 64000$ bits, $d = 2000$ km $= 2 \times 10^6$ m, $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, $R = 1$ Gbps $= 10^9$ bps.

**Propagation delay:**
$$t_p = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Transmission delay:**
$$t_t = \frac{64000}{10^9} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.064 \text{ ms}$$

**Total delay (ignoring processing and queuing):**
$$T = t_p + t_t = 10 + 0.064 = 10.064 \text{ ms}$$

**Insight:** In high-speed fiber links, the **propagation delay dominates** the total latency. The transmission delay is negligible in comparison.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert **kHz to Hz**, **dB to linear ratio**, or **bytes to bits** is the #1 reason for numerical mistakes. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist and Shannon:** Using $L$ in Shannon's formula, or using SNR in Nyquist's formula, will fetch zero marks. Remember: **Nyquist = noiseless (uses $L$)**; **Shannon = noisy (uses SNR)**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\log_2$ in SNR conversion:** When SNR is given in dB, you must convert it to a linear ratio using $10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}$ **before** plugging it into Shannon's formula. SNR inside the $\log_2$ is always linear.
> 4. **Ignoring propagation vs transmission delay:** On long-distance, high-bandwidth links, **propagation delay** dominates. On short, low-bandwidth links, **transmission delay** dominates. State this insight explicitly to gain a "good answer" mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the bandwidth-delay product concept:** Whenever a question mentions "channel utilization" with Stop-and-Wait ARQ, compute the BDP and explain why the utilization is low. This shows examiner-aware depth.

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Bullet Checklist**

- ✅ The **Physical Layer** converts bits into signals and transmits them over a medium; it is the **only** layer concerned with the *physical* nature of communication.
- ✅ **Bandwidth** ($B$) is the range of frequencies a channel can pass, measured in Hz. For a bandpass channel, $B = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$.
- ✅ **Three transmission impairments**: **Attenuation** (power loss, dB), **Distortion** (shape change, dispersion), **Noise** (thermal, impulse, crosstalk, intermodulation).
- ✅ **SNR (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$. Higher SNR → cleaner channel → higher data rate.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2(L)$ bps. Uses number of signal levels $L$.
- ✅ **Shannon Theorem (Noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps. Uses linear SNR ratio. **Absolute upper bound** — cannot be exceeded.
- ✅ **Data Rate ($R$)** vs **Signal Rate ($S$)**: $R = S \log_2(L)$. With binary signaling, $R = S$.
- ✅ **Maximum signal levels for a noisy channel:** $L_{\max} = \sqrt{1 + S/N}$ (derived by equating Nyquist and Shannon).
- ✅ **Total Latency** = Propagation Delay + Transmission Delay + Processing Delay + Queuing Delay.
- ✅ **Propagation delay** $t_p = d / s$ — dominates on long, fast links.
- ✅ **Transmission delay** $t_t = L / R$ — dominates on short, slow links.
- ✅ **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** = $B \times t_p$ — number of bits "in flight"; critical for sizing TCP windows and protocol selection.
- ✅ **Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ to avoid aliasing (Nyquist Rate = $2 f_{\max}$).
- ✅ **Transmission modes**: Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex.
- ✅ **Analog vs Digital signals**: Analog is continuous, susceptible to noise accumulation; digital is discrete, regenerable.
- ✅ **Attenuation in dB** = $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$. A 3 dB loss = power halved; a 10 dB loss = power reduced to 1/10.
- ✅ **Thermal noise PSD** $N_0 = kT$, where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann's constant).
- ✅ **Real-world bandwidth examples**: Voice channel ≈ 3.1 kHz; Ethernet twisted pair up to 500 MHz; single-mode fiber up to tens of THz.
- ✅ **Nyquist uses $L$; Shannon uses SNR.** Mixing them is a guaranteed zero.
- ✅ **Convert all dB SNR values to linear ratios** using $10^{\text{dB}/10}$ before applying Shannon's formula.
- ✅ **Stop-and-Wait ARQ utilization on high-BDP links is extremely low**; sliding window protocols are essential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Media, Signal Strength and Interference; Data Encoding; Multiplexing (TDM, FDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Media, Signal Strength and Interference; Data Encoding; Multiplexing (TDM, FDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transmission Media — The "Roads" of Data

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Transmission Media** refers to the physical pathway (or wireless spectrum) that connects the sender and the receiver in a data communication system. They are broadly classified into two families:

### A. Guided Media (Wired)
The signal is forced to travel along a physical conductor. Energy is contained and directed.

| Subtype | Physical Description | Typical Use | Max Practical Range / Bandwidth |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Twisted Pair (UTP/STP)** | Two insulated copper wires twisted to cancel EMI. | LAN Ethernet (Cat5e/6), Telephone | 100 m / up to 10 Gbps |
| **Coaxial Cable** | Inner conductor, insulator, metallic shield, outer jacket. | Cable TV, legacy Ethernet | 500 m / up to 10 Mbps (legacy) |
| **Fiber Optic Cable** | Glass/plastic core carrying modulated light pulses. | Backbone, FTTH, undersea links | 100+ km / up to Tbps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why twist the wires?** Each twist cancels out a small amount of electromagnetic interference (EMI) coming from the next pair. The tighter the twist (higher the "turns per metre"), the better the cancellation and the higher the supported frequency (Cat 6a has much tighter twists than Cat 5e).

### B. Unguided Media (Wireless)
The signal propagates through free space (air/vacuum) using electromagnetic (EM) waves. Energy is broadcast and spread out.

* **Terrestrial Microwave:** Highly directional line-of-sight beams (2–40 GHz). Used in cellular backhaul and point-to-point links.
* **Satellite Microwave:** A repeater in the sky. One hop introduces ~270 ms latency (geostationary orbit at **35,786 km** altitude).
* **Radio Waves (VLF to UHF):** Omnidirectional, can penetrate walls. Used in Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz), Bluetooth, AM/FM radio.
* **Infrared:** Line-of-sight, very short range. Used in TV remotes. Cannot pass through solid walls (security advantage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Highway Analogy):** Think of a data bit as a *delivery truck*. A *guided medium* (fiber/copper) is a **dedicated highway lane** with a physical barrier — trucks cannot leave the lane, so interference is low. An *unguided medium* (radio) is an **open field** where many trucks can drive in any direction — they may bump into each other (collision = interference). The signal "spills" everywhere, which is why wireless links always struggle more with noise than wired ones.

---

## 1.2 Signal Strength, Attenuation, and Interference

A signal **degrades** as it propagates. Three primary impairments are tested in KTU exams:

### 1. Attenuation (Loss of Strength)
The gradual loss of signal amplitude/power over distance. The signal is still recognisable, just weaker.
* **Unit:** Decibels (dB) — a **logarithmic** scale because human perception of sound/loss is logarithmic.

### 2. Distortion
The signal's shape changes because different frequency components travel at slightly different speeds (especially in copper). The wave arrives looking "stretched" or "compressed".

### 3. Noise
Random, unwanted energy added to the signal. The four KTU-mandated types are:

| Noise Type | Origin | Characteristic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Thermal (Johnson–Nyquist)** | Random electron motion in conductors; exists at *all* temperatures. | Uniform across spectrum, unavoidable. |
| **Intermodulation** | Mixing of two signals sharing a medium producing sum/difference frequencies. | Created by non-linear components. |
| **Crosstalk** | Coupling between adjacent pairs/wires (especially in UTP). | He neighbours' Wi-Fi bleeding into yours. |
| **Impulse** | Sudden spikes — lightning, faulty switches, power lines. | High amplitude, short duration, hardest to predict. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** The defining metric of link quality. Higher SNR means a cleaner, more error-free channel. Modern cellular systems target SNR > 20 dB for usable voice and > 30 dB for high-throughput data.

---

## 1.3 Data Encoding — Translating Bits into Signals

Encoding is the **process of converting a binary data stream (bits) into a form that can be transmitted over a particular medium**. The KTU Module 2 syllabus strictly requires knowledge of:

1. **Digital-to-Digital Encoding** (data is already digital; medium needs a digital waveform)
2. **Line Coding Schemes:** NRZ, NRZ-I, Manchester, Differential Manchester
3. **Bipolar Schemes:** AMI, Pseudoternary
4. **Scrambling Techniques** (used to break long runs of zeros): B8ZS, HDB3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Clock Recovery Problem:** Receivers need a regularly-spaced "tick" (clock) to know where one bit ends and the next begins. A long run of identical bits (e.g., 1000 zeros) can fool the receiver into "drifting" out of synchronisation. **Manchester and Bipolar (AMI) encodings guarantee a transition at least every bit period, ensuring self-clocking.** This is the central design trade-off KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 1.4 Multiplexing — Sharing One Highway Among Many Users

**Multiplexing** is the technique of combining multiple data signals (from multiple users) into one composite signal so that they can be transmitted over a **single shared channel** of higher capacity. A **demultiplexer** at the receiver end separates them back.

* **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing):** Each user gets a unique *frequency band* (think of radio stations at 92.7 MHz, 95.0 MHz, 99.3 MHz).
* **TDM (Time Division Multiplexing):** Each user gets the *full bandwidth* but only for a tiny time slot, in round-robin fashion.
* **Statistical TDM (Asynchronous TDM):** A smarter TDM where slots are allocated *only to active users* — vastly more efficient when traffic is "bursty".

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Elevator:** *FDM* is like having **5 separate elevators**, one for each floor — each is always available but half-empty. *Synchronous TDM* is one elevator that **stops at every floor in a fixed order** — even if that floor has nobody waiting. *Statistical TDM* is the same elevator but with a **smart controller** that skips empty floors — efficient, but needs a destination tag (address) in each "packet".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Attenuation, Noise, and Capacity — The Triad

### 2.1.1 Attenuation
Power loss between an input and output point of a component or medium.

Let $P_{\text{in}}$ be the transmitted power (in watts or milliwatts) and $P_{\text{out}}$ be the received power.

$$
\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

If you measure in voltage across equal impedances, the formula uses **20** instead of 10:

$$
\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention (board-favourite trap):** A *negative* dB value means **loss/attenuation** (e.g., a cable rated "−3 dB" loses half its power). A *positive* dB value means **gain** (an amplifier rated "+10 dB" multiplies power by 10×). KTU examiners frequently test this sign.

### 2.1.2 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

$$
\text{SNR (dB)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

where $S$ is signal power and $N$ is noise power, both in the same units (linear, not dB).

### 2.1.3 Shannon's Channel Capacity (Theoretical Maximum)

Claude Shannon's landmark 1948 result states that the **maximum error-free data rate** of a noisy channel is:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

where $B$ is the channel bandwidth (Hz) and $S/N$ is the linear signal-to-noise power ratio.

### 2.1.4 Nyquist's Noise-Free Bit Rate

If the channel is **perfect (no noise)** but limited in bandwidth, the maximum data rate (with $M$ discrete signal levels) is:

$$
C = 2B \cdot \log_{2}(M) \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

---

## 2.2 Data Encoding Schemes — The Decision Matrix

| Scheme | Rule | Levels | Self-Clocking? | DC Component? | Bandwidth vs. NRZ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NRZ-L** (Non-Return-to-Zero-Level) | 1 = High voltage, 0 = Low voltage | 2 (Binary) | ❌ No | Yes (problematic) | Lowest (B) |
| **NRZ-I** (Invert on 1) | 1 = transition, 0 = no transition | 2 (Binary) | ❌ No | Yes | Same as NRZ-L |
| **Bipolar AMI** (Alternate Mark Inversion) | 1 alternates between +V and −V, 0 = 0V | 3 (Ternary) | ✅ Yes (for 1s) | None | Higher than NRZ |
| **Pseudoternary** | Inverse of AMI: 0 alternates, 1 = 0V | 3 (Ternary) | ✅ Yes (for 0s) | None | Higher than NRZ |
| **Manchester** | 1 = High→Low, 0 = Low→High (mid-bit transition) | 2 | ✅ Yes (always) | None | **2× of NRZ** |
| **Differential Manchester** | Always has mid-bit transition; 1 = no edge at start, 0 = edge at start | 2 | ✅ Yes (always) | None | **2× of NRZ** |
| **B8ZS** (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution) | Used with AMI; replaces `00000000` with a deliberate violation pattern | 3 | ✅ Yes (forced) | None | Same as AMI |
| **HDB3** (High Density Bipolar 3) | Like AMI but forces a substitution for any run of 4 zeros | 3 | ✅ Yes (forced) | None | Same as AMI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Manchester 2× the bandwidth of NRZ?** Because it has a transition in the *middle* of every bit period, doubling the highest fundamental frequency. This is why 10 Mbps Manchester Ethernet (10BASE-T) needed at least 20 MHz of spectrum. NRZ schemes are bandwidth-efficient but lose clocking on long runs of identical bits.

### 2.2.1 B8ZS Substitution Pattern (for North-American T1)
Whenever 8 consecutive zeros occur, replace with: `000VB0VB` (where V = violation of the AMI polarity rule, B = bipolar pulse matching the previous valid 1's polarity).

### 2.2.2 HDB3 Substitution Pattern (for European E1)
Whenever 4 consecutive zeros occur, replace with `000V` or `B00V`, alternating the substitution rule so that consecutive violations alternate in polarity and the DC component stays zero.

---

## 2.3 Multiplexing — The Core Mechanism

### A. Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)
* The available spectrum $B_{\text{total}}$ is divided into $N$ non-overlapping sub-bands (channels).
* Each source modulates a different **carrier frequency** $f_c$.
* A **guard band** (small unused spectrum) is placed between channels to prevent interference.
* Widely used in AM/FM radio, cable TV, and first-generation cellular.

### B. Synchronous (Synchronous) TDM
* Time is divided into **frames**. Each frame has $N$ slots, one per source.
* A source gets **one slot per frame**, regardless of whether it has data.
* Inefficient if some sources are silent — slots carry empty/idle data.
* Used in PSTN T1/E1 trunk lines (1.544 Mbps / 2.048 Mbps).

### C. Statistical (Asynchronous / Intelligent) TDM
* Slots are **dynamically allocated** to *active* sources only.
* Each slot must carry a **source address** (header) so the demultiplexer knows where to deliver it.
* Average data rate per source can approach the full link rate.
* Slight delay added due to buffering and address processing.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Attenuation | $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$ | dB | Use 20 instead of 10 for voltage. |
| 2 | SNR | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | dB | $S$ and $N$ are linear. |
| 3 | Noise Figure | $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ | ratio (linear) | $\text{NF}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$. |
| 4 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ | bps | $B$ in Hz, $S/N$ linear. |
| 5 | Nyquist Bit Rate | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | bps | $M$ = number of signal levels. |
| 6 | Throughput (Sync TDM) | $R_{\text{each}} = R_{\text{link}} / N$ | bps | $N$ = number of sources, ignoring overhead. |
| 7 | Sync TDM Frame Duration | $T_f = N \cdot T_b$ | sec | $N$ sources, $T_b$ = bit duration. |
| 8 | Required FDM Channels | $N \le B_{\text{total}} / (B_{\text{signal}} + B_{\text{guard}})$ | integer | Includes guard band. |
| 9 | dB→Linear Power | $P_{\text{lin}} = 10^{P_{\text{dB}}/10}$ | ratio | For voltages: $20$ in denominator. |
| 10 | Thermal Noise Power | $N = k \cdot T \cdot B$ | W | $k$ = Boltzmann = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Attenuation in Fibre:** Modern single-mode fibre loses only ~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm, allowing 100+ km spans between optical amplifiers.
* **Manchester in 10BASE-T:** Even though it doubles bandwidth, Ethernet adopted it because it has a strong DC-balanced spectrum — perfect for running over transformer-isolated twisted pairs.
* **HDB3 in E1 Trunks:** Europe's 2.048 Mbps E1 system uses HDB3 precisely because European PSTN lines often carry long runs of zero bits during idle voice calls.
* **Statistical TDM in Frame Relay & MPLS:** The entire modern packet-switched backbone is built on the idea that bandwidth is cheaper when shared intelligently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **worked numerical derivations** that appear in KTU Module 2 university exam questions. Every algebraic transition is written explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Shannon's Capacity from First Principles (Intuitive)

Shannon proved that for any channel with bandwidth $B$ Hz and additive white Gaussian noise of power $N$:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\text{bits/second}
$$

**Interpretation (KTU-marked step):**
1. A noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ can resolve $2B$ independent samples per second (Nyquist).
2. Each sample can be quantised into one of $M$ levels. From Nyquist: $C = 2B \log_2 M$.
3. Noise forces the receiver to distinguish signal levels with finite precision. The number of *distinguishable* levels $M$ grows as $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ (because noise adds Gaussian jitter proportional to $\sqrt{N}$).
4. Substituting: $C = 2B \log_2 \sqrt{1 + S/N} = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Attenuation and SNR

> **Problem:** A signal of power **100 mW** is transmitted. After travelling through a cable, the received power is **0.5 mW**. Calculate (a) attenuation in dB, (b) the SNR at the receiver if noise power is **0.05 mW**, (c) the Shannon capacity if bandwidth is **4 kHz**.

### (a) Attenuation in dB
$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

Substitute values (units cancel — use the ratio, not the units themselves):

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100 \text{ mW}}{0.5 \text{ mW}}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(200)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(200) = \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{2}) = \log_{10}(2) + 2 \approx 0.3010 + 2 = 2.3010
$$

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 2.3010 = 23.01 \text{ dB}
$$

**Answer: ≈ 23 dB attenuation.**

### (b) SNR in dB
Signal power $S = 0.5$ mW (received), noise power $N = 0.05$ mW.

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.05}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(10) = 10 \cdot 1 = 10 \text{ dB}
$$

**Answer: SNR = 10 dB.**

### (c) Shannon Capacity
First convert SNR from dB to linear:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{10/10} = 10^{1} = 10
$$

Then apply Shannon:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 10) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(11)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(11) = \frac{\log_{10}(11)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.0414}{0.3010} \approx 3.4594
$$

$$
C \approx 4000 \times 3.4594 \approx 13{,}838 \text{ bps} \approx 13.84 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Answer: C ≈ 13.84 kbps.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Manchester Encoding Waveform

> **Problem:** Draw the Manchester encoding of the bit stream `1 0 1 1 0 0 1`. Identify the self-clocking property. (KTU-typical 7-mark question.)

**Rules (IEEE 802.3 convention):**
* `0` → transition from **Low to High** in the middle of the bit cell.
* `1` → transition from **High to Low** in the middle of the bit cell.

Step-by-step construction (each cell = 1 bit duration $T$):

| Bit | Mid-bit transition | Waveform description |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | High → Low | Start High, dip to Low at $T/2$ |
| 0 | Low → High | Start Low, jump to High at $T/2$ |
| 1 | High → Low | Start High (from previous), dip at $T/2$ |
| 1 | High → Low | Continue dip, end at Low |
| 0 | Low → High | Jump at $T/2$ |
| 0 | Low → High | Start High (from previous), hold, then Low→High at $T/2$ (slight ambiguity; canonical draw uses start-Low) |
| 1 | High → Low | Dip at $T/2$ |

**Self-clocking proof:** There is **exactly one transition per bit cell**, so the receiver can extract a clock at the bit rate by phase-locking onto these mid-bit edges. The bit stream is recoverable even after long-distance, noisy transmission.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — TDM Calculation

> **Problem:** A T1 line uses **Synchronous TDM** to multiplex **24 voice channels**, each sampled at **8 kHz** with **8 bits per sample**. Find (a) frame duration, (b) total bit rate, (c) bits per frame including the framing bit.

### (a) Frame duration
Each voice channel is sampled once per frame. The sampling rate = frame rate = 8 kHz. So:

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \; \mu\text{s}
$$

### (b) Total bit rate
Data bits per frame = $24 \times 8 = 192$ bits.
Plus 1 framing bit per frame = 193 bits per frame.

$$
R = \text{bits per frame} \times \text{frames per second} = 193 \times 8000
$$

$$
R = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}
$$

This is the famous **T1 carrier rate**.

### (c) Per-channel rate
$$
R_{\text{channel}} = 8 \text{ bits} \times 8000 \text{ samples/s} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

This 64 kbps is the **DS0** rate — the bedrock of all digital telephony.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — FDM Channel Allocation

> **Problem:** A 100 kHz channel carries 5 voice signals, each of bandwidth 3 kHz. How much is the guard band between adjacent channels?

**Total available** = 100 kHz.
**Total signal bandwidth** = $5 \times 3 = 15$ kHz.
**Total guard bandwidth** = $100 - 15 = 85$ kHz.
**Number of guard gaps** = $5 - 1 = 4$ (no guard needed at edges, but we can place them anywhere).

$$
B_{\text{guard each}} = \frac{85}{4} = 21.25 \text{ kHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, all guard bands are placed at the edges and none between channels (or vice-versa). The *minimum* required guard band is what filters can practically achieve — typically 0.5 kHz for voice. The "extra" capacity here would simply not be used.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Given Quantity | Goal | Use Formula | Conversion Needed? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power ratio | Attenuation in dB | $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$ | None |
| Voltage ratio | Attenuation in dB | $A = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{in}}/V_{\text{out}})$ | None |
| dB value | Linear ratio | $P_{\text{lin}} = 10^{P_{\text{dB}}/10}$ | Yes (antilog) |
| Linear SNR, $B$ | Max bps (noisy) | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | SNR must be linear |
| SNR in dB, $B$ | Max bps (noisy) | First convert: $S/N = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}$, then Shannon | Yes |
| $B$, $M$ levels | Max bps (noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | None |
| $N$ channels, $B$ | $N$ given bandwidth | $N = B_{\text{total}} / (B_{\text{ch}} + B_{\text{guard}})$ | None |
| Sync TDM: $N$ users, $R$ | Per-user rate | $R/N$ | None |
| $N$ users, sampling $f_s$ | TDM frame time | $T_f = 1/f_s$ | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams are compiled to be **renderable in any standard Mermaid viewer** (GitHub, VS Code, browser). Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain no `|` characters.

---

## 4.1 Classification of Transmission Media (Module Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transmission Media] --> B[Guided Wired]
    A --> C[Unguided Wireless]

    B --> B1[Twisted Pair UTP STP]
    B --> B2[Coaxial Cable]
    B --> B3[Fiber Optic]

    B1 --> B1a[Cat 5e LAN]
    B1 --> B1b[Cat 6 10Gbps]
    B2 --> B2a[Cable TV]
    B2 --> B2b[Legacy Ethernet]
    B3 --> B3a[Single Mode Long Haul]
    B3 --> B3b[Multi Mode LAN]

    C --> C1[Terrestrial Microwave]
    C --> C2[Satellite Microwave]
    C --> C3[Radio Waves]
    C --> C4[Infrared]

    C1 --> C1a[Cellular Backhaul]
    C2 --> C2a[GPS Broadcast]
    C3 --> C3a[WiFi Bluetooth]
    C4 --> C4a[TV Remotes]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — A TDM Multiplexing System

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT1[Source 1 64 kbps] --> MUX
    INPUT2[Source 2 64 kbps] --> MUX
    INPUT3[Source 3 64 kbps] --> MUX
    INPUT4[Source 4 64 kbps] --> MUX
    MUX[Synchronous TDM MUX] --> LINK[Shared Link 256 kbps plus overhead]
    LINK --> DEMUX[Demultiplexer]
    DEMUX --> OUT1[Deliver to Sink 1]
    DEMUX --> OUT2[Deliver to Sink 2]
    DEMUX --> OUT3[Deliver to Sink 3]
    DEMUX --> OUT4[Deliver to Sink 4]

    style MUX fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DEMUX fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The four 64 kbps voice sources are interleaved by the MUX into a single 256 kbps+ bit stream. At the far end, the DEMUX uses the **known frame position** of each slot to route bits back to the correct sink. If source 2 is silent, its slot is still transmitted (containing idle bits) — this is the wastage that Statistical TDM eliminates.

---

## 4.3 State Machine — Manchester Encoding (IEEE 802.3)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StartBit
    StartBit --> EncodeZero : read 0
    StartBit --> EncodeOne : read 1
    EncodeZero --> HighLevel : transition Low to High at bit midpoint
    EncodeOne --> LowLevel : transition High to Low at bit midpoint
    HighLevel --> StartBit : next bit begins
    LowLevel --> StartBit : next bit begins
    StartBit : Wait for next bit cell boundary
    EncodeZero : Bit value 0
    EncodeOne : Bit value 1
    HighLevel : Hold High for remaining half period
    LowLevel : Hold Low for remaining half period
```

---

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Shannon Capacity Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Signal Power S Watts] --> ADD1
    N[Noise Power N Watts] --> ADD1
    ADD1[Compute S/N ratio Linear] --> DB[Convert to dB if needed]
    B[Bandwidth B Hz] --> MULT
    ADD1 --> MULT
    MULT[Multiply B by log2 1 + S/N] --> C[Capacity C bits per second]
    DB --> ADD1
```

This is the canonical "noise budget" calculation chain. KTU problems typically give you three of the four parameters ($B$, $S$, $N$, $C$) and ask you to find the fourth. Always work in **linear** power units, then convert to dB only at the end.

---

## 4.5 Spectral Allocation — FDM Channel Plan

```mermaid
graph LR
    F0[Guard 0 to 3 kHz]:::guard
    F1[Voice 1 3 to 6 kHz]:::ch1
    F2[Guard 6 to 9 kHz]:::guard
    F3[Voice 2 9 to 12 kHz]:::ch2
    F4[Guard 12 to 15 kHz]:::guard
    F5[Voice 3 15 to 18 kHz]:::ch3
    F0 --- F1 --- F2 --- F3 --- F4 --- F5

    classDef guard fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#999,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef ch1 fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    classDef ch2 fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    classDef ch3 fill:#06d6a0,color:#fff
```

In this textbook FDM plan, each 3 kHz voice channel is separated by a 3 kHz guard band. The total spectrum is 18 kHz, of which only 9 kHz is useful — a 50% efficiency. In real systems, the guard band is much smaller (e.g., 0.5 kHz) to push efficiency above 90%.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between guided and unguided transmission media. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Guided media confine the signal along a solid physical path (copper wire or glass fibre), offering high reliability, low interference, and higher bandwidths at the cost of installation effort. Unguided media broadcast the signal through free space as electromagnetic waves, requiring no physical medium but being more susceptible to interference, attenuation, and eavesdropping.

* **Guided example:** Twisted pair copper cable (used in Ethernet LANs).
* **Unguided example:** Terrestrial microwave link (used in cellular backhaul).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Attenuation. How is it measured?**

**Model Answer:**

**Attenuation** is the gradual loss of signal strength as it propagates through a transmission medium. It is measured in **decibels (dB)** using the formula:

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

where $P_{\text{in}}$ and $P_{\text{out}}$ are the transmitted and received signal powers respectively. A positive dB value indicates loss, and a 3 dB loss corresponds to a halving of signal power.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
>
> **a)** Explain the four main types of noise that affect a communication channel. (7 marks)
>
> **b)** With neat diagrams, explain the working of **Manchester** and **Differential Manchester** encoding schemes. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Types of Noise (7 marks)

The four main types of noise are:

1. **Thermal Noise (Johnson–Nyquist noise):** Random motion of electrons in a conductor, present at all temperatures above absolute zero. It is **uniformly distributed across all frequencies** (white noise) and is unavoidable. Power is given by $N = kTB$ where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant), $T$ is absolute temperature, and $B$ is bandwidth. Cooling the medium (e.g., cooled fibre amplifiers) reduces it.

2. **Intermodulation Noise:** Produced when two or more signals share a medium and pass through a **non-linear** component (e.g., a saturating amplifier). The non-linearity creates new frequency components at sums and differences of the originals (e.g., $f_1 + f_2$, $f_1 - f_2$), which fall in-band and corrupt other channels.

3. **Crosstalk:** Unwanted coupling between physically adjacent transmission lines. In twisted pair cables, each pair radiates a small EM field that induces a signal in the next pair. Higher category cables (Cat 6a) use tighter twisting and individual pair shielding (S/FTP) to reduce this. Far-end crosstalk (FEXT) and near-end crosstalk (NEXT) are the two standard metrics.

4. **Impulse Noise:** Sudden, high-amplitude, short-duration spikes. Sources include lightning, switching of electrical equipment, faulty power lines, and electrostatic discharge. It is the most damaging to digital data because a single 0.1 µs spike can corrupt many bits. Difficult to predict and usually mitigated with shielding and error-correction codes.

#### Part (b) — Manchester and Differential Manchester (7 marks)

**Manchester Encoding (IEEE 802.3 convention):**
* A **transition always occurs in the middle of every bit cell**.
* `0` is represented by a **Low-to-High** transition.
* `1` is represented by a **High-to-Low** transition.
* There is *always* a transition at the bit boundary for clock recovery → self-clocking guaranteed.
* The DC component is zero (spectrum is centred around $2f_b$).

**Differential Manchester Encoding:**
* A transition **always occurs in the middle of every bit cell** (same as Manchester).
* In addition, a transition at the **start** of the bit cell represents `0`; **no** transition at the start represents `1`.
* Only the *presence or absence* of the leading edge matters → **invert-polarity-safe** (differential decoding is unaffected by swapping the two wires).

| Scheme | Advantage | Disadvantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Manchester | Simple, guaranteed self-clock, DC-balanced. | Requires **2× the bandwidth** of NRZ. |
| Differential Manchester | Polarity-independent decoding. | Same 2× bandwidth penalty; slightly more complex. |

**Diagrams (described in words for textual answer):**
* For Manchester, draw a square wave that is `L-to-H` at mid-bit for `0` and `H-to-L` for `1`. Example for stream `1 0 1 1 0 0 1`: start High, dip mid-bit (1), stay Low, rise mid-bit (0), stay High, dip mid-bit (1), continue dip mid-bit (1), stay Low, rise mid-bit (0), continue High, dip mid-bit (0), stay High, dip mid-bit (1).
* For Differential Manchester on the same stream: every bit has a mid-bit transition, plus a leading-edge transition only if the bit is `0`.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
>
> **a)** Explain the concept of **multiplexing**. Describe **FDM** with a neat block diagram and discuss its applications. (7 marks)
>
> **b)** A channel of bandwidth **4 kHz** is used to transmit a signal with an SNR of **30 dB**. Using Shannon's theorem, calculate the maximum theoretical data rate. If the channel uses **4 signal levels**, what is the Nyquist bit rate? Comment on why the two answers differ. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Multiplexing and FDM (7 marks)

**Multiplexing** is the technique of combining multiple independent data signals into a single composite signal so that it can be transmitted over a shared channel of higher capacity, thereby economising on infrastructure cost. The reverse process of separating them at the receiver is called **demultiplexing**.

**Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM):**
* The available bandwidth $B_{\text{total}}$ of the channel is divided into $N$ non-overlapping sub-bands.
* Each source signal is first band-limited to $B_s$ Hz and then modulates a distinct **carrier frequency** $f_{ci}$, producing a modulated signal occupying $[f_{ci} - B_s/2,\; f_{ci} + B_s/2]$.
* The modulated signals are summed (after linear mixing) into one composite signal that is transmitted.
* At the receiver, **band-pass filters** isolate each sub-band, and a coherent demodulator recovers the original.
* **Guard bands** of small unused frequency must be left between adjacent sub-bands to prevent mutual interference (filter roll-off is not perfect).

**Block Diagram (described):**
* Sources $S_1 \ldots S_N$ → Low-pass filters (band-limit to $B_s$) → Modulators with carriers $f_{c1} \ldots f_{cN}$ → Summer → Composite signal → Channel → Receiver BPF bank → Demodulators → Sinks.

**Applications:**
* **AM/FM radio broadcasting** (the entire 88–108 MHz band is an FDM system).
* **Cable TV** (each channel on a different sub-carrier from 50 MHz to 1 GHz).
* **First-generation analog cellular** (AMPS) and **2G/3G downlink** also use FDM/OFDM.

**Disadvantages:** Requires precise analog filters; channel capacity is fixed once assigned; idle sub-bands waste spectrum; susceptible to intermodulation distortion.

#### Part (b) — Shannon and Nyquist (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**
$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's theorem:**
$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 1000) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(1001)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.968
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 4000 \times 9.968 \approx 39{,}872 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Nyquist's theorem (noiseless) with $M = 4$ levels:**
$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \cdot \log_{2}(M) = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_{2}(4) = 8000 \times 2 = 16{,}000 \text{ bps} = 16 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the difference:**
Shannon's value (39.87 kbps) is **higher** than Nyquist's (16 kbps) because Shannon's bound applies to a *noisy* channel — it represents the *theoretical maximum* under the given SNR. Nyquist's bound assumes a *perfect noiseless* channel with only $M$ discrete levels, so it is the upper limit *for that specific line code*. In practice, real systems sit **between** the two bounds: Nyquist is the ceiling for a chosen $M$, Shannon is the ceiling for any code at all. To approach Shannon's bound requires sophisticated coding (e.g., turbo codes, LDPC).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 2:**
> 1. **dB sign confusion:** A cable "loses 3 dB" is written as **−3 dB**, but many students write `+3 dB` and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Using 20 instead of 10 in attenuation formula:** Always check whether power or voltage is given.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** before plugging into Shannon's formula. The most-failed step in university papers.
> 4. **Confusing AMI with Manchester:** AMI uses **3 levels** (±V, 0); Manchester uses **2 levels** with a **mid-bit transition**. Examiners report that 30% of scripts mix these up.
> 5. **Synchronous vs Statistical TDM throughput:** In Sync TDM, $R_{\text{each}} = R_{\text{link}}/N$ *regardless* of activity. In Statistical TDM, the per-user rate is $R_{\text{link}}/\text{(average active users)}$ — students often forget the address/overhead bits (typically 1 byte per slot).
> 6. **Confusing frequency and wavelength:** Antenna size depends on wavelength ($\lambda = c/f$). A higher frequency = smaller antenna.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
