* **Media:** Guided = UTP, Coaxial, Fibre. Unguided = Microwave, Satellite, Radio, IR. **Fibre > Coax > UTP** in bandwidth and range.
* **Attenuation formula:** $A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$. 3 dB = half power. 10 dB = 10× power.
* **Four noises:** Thermal (unavoidable), Intermodulation (non-linearity), Crosstalk (adjacent wires), Impulse (sudden spikes).
* **SNR formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$.
* **Shannon:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — use **linear** $S/N$.
* **Nyquist:** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ — noiseless, $M$ discrete levels.
* **Manchester:** Mid-bit transition always. `0` = L→H, `1` = H→L. 2× bandwidth of NRZ. Self-clocking.
* **Differential Manchester:** Mid-bit always, leading edge only for `0`.
* **AMI / Pseudoternary:** Three levels. No DC component. Self-clocking for one of the bits.
* **B8ZS** (North American T1): 8-zero string → `000VB0VB`. **HDB3** (European E1): 4-zero string → `B00V` or `000V` (alternating).
* **FDM:** Each user gets a unique frequency band. Needs guard bands. Used in radio, TV.
* **Sync TDM:** Each user gets a fixed slot per frame. Inefficient for bursty traffic.
* **Statistical TDM:** Slots given to active users only. Needs source addressing. ~2–4× more efficient.
* **T1 carrier:** 24 DS0 channels × 8 bits + 1 framing bit × 8 kHz = **1.544 Mbps**.
* **E1 carrier:** 32 DS0 slots × 8 bits × 8 kHz = **2.048 Mbps**.
* **Thermal noise power:** $N = kTB$ where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
* **Always draw diagrams** for Manchester, AMI, and TDM frame allocation — they typically earn 1–2 extra marks in KTU 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Link Layer Design Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Link Layer Design Issues # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Data Link Layer in the OSI/TCP-IP Architecture The **Data Link Layer (DLL)** is **Layer 2** of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) reference model** and sits directly above the Physical Layer. Its fundamental responsibility is to provide **reliable, error-free transfer of data frames** between two adjacent nodes connected by a physical link. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, "Data Link Layer Design Issues" refers to the set of engineering problems and architectural decisions a protocol designer must resolve to convert an inherently *unreliable* physical medium into a *reliable* logical pipeline for the Network Layer above. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBCCT304 - Module 2):** > The module explicitly requires students to understand the *design issues* of the DLL, namely: **(1) Services to the Network Layer, (2) Framing, (3) Error Control, and (4) Flow Control.** These are the "four pillars" frequently tested in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. ### 1.2 Formal Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Forouzan / Tanenbaum Standard):** > The Data Link Layer transforms a stream of bits received from the Physical Layer into a stream of **frames**, and is responsible for **node-to-node delivery**, **framing**, **physical addressing**, **error control**, **flow control**, and **access control** of the shared medium. In simple engineering terms, if the **Physical Layer** delivers *raw electrical/optical pulses* (think of them as unverified whispers), the **Data Link Layer** is the *postman* who packages those whispers into sealed envelopes (**frames**), confirms the address is valid, checks whether the contents are damaged, and ensures the recipient is not overwhelmed. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Envelope" Model Imagine you are sending a 500-page novel to your friend across town through a series of *unreliable bicycle messengers*: | Real-World Element | Data Link Layer Equivalent | |---|---| | The bicycle messenger | **Physical Layer** (may drop pages, smear ink) | | Putting pages into a sealed envelope with a label | **Framing** (delimiting start/end of frame) | | Writing a tracking number on the envelope | **Physical / MAC Addressing** | | A quality stamp saying "Verified Intact" | **Error Detection (CRC, Checksum, Parity)** | | A redo-request slip if pages are smudged | **Error Correction / ARQ (ARQ = Automatic Repeat reQuest)** | | Asking the messenger to wait if the friend's mailbox is full | **Flow Control** | | The zip code and house number | **Logical Link Control (LLC) Sublayer** | > [!TIP] > **Intuition Hook for Exams:** Whenever a question says "the DLL converts bits to frames," visualize the DLL as the layer that adds **structure, reliability, and synchronization** to the otherwise chaotic bitstream of Layer 1. ### 1.4 The Four Core Design Issues (KTU High-Yield List) The four design issues students *must* internalize are: 1. **Service to the Network Layer** — *What kind of service* (reliable/unreliable, connectionless/connection-oriented) should the DLL offer upward? 2. **Framing** — *How* does the layer break the bitstream into discrete, recognizable units (frames) and handle transparency (escaping delimiter bytes)? 3. **Error Control** — *How* are bit errors caused by the physical medium detected and corrected (ARQ strategies: Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat)? 4. **Flow Control** — *How* is a fast sender prevented from drowning a slow receiver (Stop-and-Wait, Sliding Window protocols)? ### 1.5 Sub-Layering of the Data Link Layer (LAN Context) In modern LAN standards (IEEE 802.x), the DLL is split into two sublayers, which is a common 3-mark question: * **LLC (Logical Link Control — IEEE 802.2):** Upper sublayer; handles framing, error handling, and flow control. Provides a uniform interface to the Network Layer. * **MAC (Medium Access Control — IEEE 802.3/802.11):** Lower sublayer; governs *who* transmits next on the shared medium (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA, Token Passing, etc.). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Issue 1 — Services Provided to the Network Layer The DLL offers three classical categories of service upward to Layer 3: | Service Type | Connection | Reliability | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Unacknowledged Connectionless Service** | None | No error recovery; no flow control | LANs (Ethernet), real-time voice, where retransmission is worse than lost data | | **Acknowledged Connectionless Service** | None | Acknowledged frames; lost frames retransmitted | Wireless networks (Wi-Fi 802.11) | | **Acknowledged Connection-Oriented Service** | Yes (virtual circuit) | Frames numbered, ack'd, in order; flow + error control | Satellite links, long-haul noisy links (classical HDLC) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** For LANs, the preferred service is **unacknowledged connectionless** because the higher layers (Transport, typically TCP) can handle reliability more efficiently, and the DLL is kept simple/fast. For noisy/long links, **acknowledged connection-oriented** is preferred. ## 2.2 Issue 2 — Framing Framing divides the bitstream into manageable, addressable units. Four classic techniques are tested: ### 2.2.1 Character Count Framing The frame header begins with a field specifying the **number of characters** in the frame. A count error (e.g., caused by a line hit) **desynchronizes the receiver permanently** — a fatal weakness. ### 2.2.2 Flag Byte / Byte Stuffing Framing Each frame begins and ends with a special **flag byte** (e.g., `01111110` in HDLC). To prevent flag bytes from appearing in payload, the sender inserts an **ESC (escape) byte** before any flag-like sequence in the data (byte stuffing). The receiver removes the ESC on receipt. ### 2.2.3 Bit Stuffing Framing The start/end flag is the bit pattern **`01111110`**. Whenever the sender sees **five consecutive 1's** in the payload, it *automatically stuffs* a `0` bit. The receiver, upon seeing five 1's followed by a `0`, deletes the `0`. This is the HDLC/PPP standard and is **more general than byte stuffing** because it works on raw bit streams. ### 2.2.4 Physical Layer Coding Violations Used in token-ring/IEEE 802.5 networks. The physical layer uses signals that *never appear in user data* (e.g., differential Manchester violations) to delimit frames — completely eliminates transparency issues. ## 2.3 Issue 3 — Error Control Errors are of two types: * **Single-Bit Error:** Only one bit flipped in a frame (rare in practice; common in serial links). * **Burst Error:** Two or more consecutive bits in error (dominant in real channels). Length measured in bits, $B$. ### 2.3.1 Error Detection Codes (KTU High-Yield) | Code | Mechanism | Detection Power | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | **Parity Bit (1-D)** | Append parity bit (even/odd) | All single-bit errors | ASCII, simple UART | | **2-D Parity** | Arrange bits in a matrix; add row + column parity | Detects all $\le 3$ bit errors; corrects 1-bit | Legacy storage | | **Checksum (Internet)** | 16-bit ones'-complement sum of 16-bit words | Detects most error bursts | TCP, UDP, IP, ICMP | | **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)** | Polynomial division in $\text{GF}(2)$ | Detects all single-bit, all double-bit, all odd-count errors; all burst errors $\le r$ bits where $r$ is degree of generator | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, HDLC, ZIP | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Sheet — CRC Engineered Polynomial Properties:** > If generator polynomial $G(x)$ has degree $r$, then the CRC is $r$ bits long and the following are guaranteed to be detected: > 1. All **single-bit errors**. > 2. All **double-bit errors**, provided $G(x)$ has a factor with at least two terms (e.g., $x+1$). > 3. Any **odd number of bit errors**, provided $G(x)$ contains factor $(x+1)$. > 4. All **burst errors** of length $\le r$ bits. > 5. A fraction $1 - 2^{-r}$ of burst errors of length $> r$ bits. ### 2.3.2 Error Correction — Hamming Code The **Hamming distance** $d(x,y)$ between two bit strings is the number of bit positions in which they differ. To **detect** $d$ errors, codewords must have $d \ge s+1$. To **correct** $t$ errors, codewords must satisfy $d \ge 2t+1$. For a Hamming code with $m$ parity bits and $n = 2^m - 1$ total codeword length, the data bits are $k = n - m = 2^m - m - 1$. The number of redundant bits needed is the smallest $m$ such that: $$2^m \ge m + k + 1$$ ## 2.4 Issue 4 — Flow Control The receiver must not be overwhelmed by the sender. The two canonical strategies: ### 2.4.1 Stop-and-Wait ARQ The sender transmits one frame, then **waits for an ACK** before sending the next. If no ACK arrives within a timeout, the frame is retransmitted. Simple but **inefficient** for high-bandwidth-delay product links. ### 2.4.2 Sliding Window ARQ The sender can transmit up to $W$ (window size) unacknowledged frames at a time. The receiver advertises a window of $W$ in every ACK. Two variants: * **Go-Back-N (GBN):** Receiver discards out-of-order frames; sender must retransmit from the lost frame onward. * **Selective Repeat (SR):** Receiver buffers out-of-order frames; sender retransmits *only* the lost frame. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | |---|---|---| | **Frame Transmission Time** | $T_f = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Time to push a frame of $L$ bits onto a link of rate $R$ bps | | **Propagation Delay** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Time for a signal to traverse distance $d$ at propagation speed $v$ ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper) | | **Round Trip Time** | $\text{RTT} = 2 T_p$ | Time for a packet to travel to receiver and ACK to return | | **Link Utilization (Stop-and-Wait)** | $U = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + 2 T_p} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = T_p / T_f$ | Fraction of time the link carries useful data | | **Efficiency Bound (Stop-and-Wait)** | $U \le 1$ | Approaches 1 only when $T_f \gg T_p$ | | **Sliding Window Efficiency** | $U = \dfrac{W \cdot T_f}{T_f + 2 T_p} = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ | Sender pipeline of $W$ frames | | **Minimum Window for Full Utilization** | $W_{\min} = 1 + 2a$ | Smallest window to keep the pipe full | | **CRC Generator Degree** | $r$ | Number of redundant bits in the CRC | | **CRC Burst Error Detection (len $\le r$)** | 100% detected | All short bursts caught | | **Hamming Code Redundancy** | $2^m \ge m + k + 1$ | $m$ parity bits for $k$ data bits | | **Minimum Hamming Distance (Detect $s$ errors)** | $d_{\min} \ge s + 1$ | Code separation requirement | | **Minimum Hamming Distance (Correct $t$ errors)** | $d_{\min} \ge 2t + 1$ | Code separation requirement | > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The link utilization formula $U = 1/(1+2a)$ explains why Stop-and-Wait is **dead for satellite links** (where $T_p \approx 270$ ms). A 1500-byte frame at 1 Gbps has $T_f = 12\,\mu s$, giving $a = 22{,}500$ and $U \approx 0.0022\%$ — only **2.2 bps of useful throughput out of 1 Gbps**. This is why satellite TCP uses **Selective Repeat** or **TCP split-connection proxies** (e.g., PEP — Performance Enhancing Proxies). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) Bit Computation The CRC is computed via **polynomial division over GF(2)** (Galois Field of order 2 — i.e., modulo-2 arithmetic, where addition = subtraction = XOR). Let: $$M(x) = \text{message polynomial}, \quad G(x) = \text{generator polynomial of degree } r$$ The transmitted codeword polynomial is: $$T(x) = x^r \cdot M(x) + R(x)$$ where $R(x)$ is the remainder when $x^r \cdot M(x)$ is divided by $G(x)$: $$x^r \cdot M(x) = Q(x) \cdot G(x) + R(x)$$ At the receiver, the incoming polynomial $T'(x)$ is divided by $G(x)$; a **zero remainder** means no detectable error. ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard) > **Problem:** Compute the CRC for message $M = 1001\,100$ (7 bits) using generator $G = 1001$ (degree $r = 3$). Show all steps. **Step 1 — Identify the polynomials.** $$M(x) = x^6 + x^3 + x^2 + 1 \quad \text{(positions 6, 3, 2, 0)}$$ $$G(x) = x^3 + 1$$ **Step 2 — Append $r$ zeros to $M$.** $$x^3 \cdot M(x) = 1\,0011\,00\underbrace{000}_{r=3 \text{ zeros}} = 1001\,100\,000 \text{ (10 bits)}$$ **Step 3 — Perform modulo-2 division of $x^3 M(x)$ by $G(x) = 1001$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dividend: } & 1001100000 \\ \text{Divisor: } & 1001 \\ \hline \text{Step A: } & 1001 \oplus 1001 = 0000, \text{ drop, bring down next bit } \Rightarrow 0001\,100000 \\ \text{Step B: } & 0001 \oplus 0000 = 0001, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 0011\,00000 \\ \text{Step C: } & 0011 \oplus 0000 = 0011, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 0110\,0000 \\ \text{Step D: } & 0110 \oplus 0000 = 0110, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 1100\,000 \\ \text{Step E: } & 1100 \oplus 1001 = 0101, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 1010\,00 \\ \text{Step F: } & 1010 \oplus 1001 = 0011, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 0110\,0 \\ \text{Step G: } & 0110 \oplus 0000 = 0110, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 1100 \\ \text{Step H: } & 1100 \oplus 1001 = 0101, \text{ bring down } \Rightarrow 1010 \\ \text{Step I: } & 1010 \oplus 1001 = 0011 \quad \textbf{(Remainder)} \end{aligned} $$ > Each subtraction is bitwise XOR (since GF(2) addition = subtraction). **Step 4 — Read the remainder.** $R(x) = 011$ (a 3-bit number, zero-padded on the left). **Step 5 — Form the transmitted codeword.** $$T = M \Vert R = 1001100 \Vert 011 = \underbrace{1001100}_{M}\,\underbrace{011}_{\text{CRC}}$$ **Step 6 — Verification at the receiver.** Receiver divides $T$ by $G = 1001$ and gets a remainder of **000** ⇒ no error. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always **pad the remainder to $r$ bits** by prepending leading zeros (e.g., remainder `11` for $r=3$ becomes `011`). Examiners deduct marks for unpadded remainders. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hamming Code Construction > **Problem:** Construct a Hamming code for a 4-bit data word $D = d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0 = 1\,0\,1\,1$ using **even parity**. List all 7 codeword bits. **Step 1 — Find the number of parity bits $m$ such that $2^m \ge m + k + 1$.** With $k = 4$ data bits, test $m = 3$: $2^3 = 8 \ge 3 + 4 + 1 = 8$. ✓ So $m = 3$, total codeword length $n = 7$. **Step 2 — Assign bit positions and parity-bit positions.** Positions that are powers of 2 (1, 2, 4) are parity bits; the rest (3, 5, 6, 7) carry data. | Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Role | $d_3$ | $d_2$ | $d_1$ | $p_2$ | $d_0$ | $p_1$ | $p_0$ | | Binary | 111 | 110 | 101 | 100 | 011 | 010 | 001 | **Step 3 — Map data bits into data positions.** The mapping is: $d_3$ at pos 7, $d_2$ at pos 6, $d_1$ at pos 5, $d_0$ at pos 3. Plugging $D = 1011$: | Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Bit | 1 | 0 | 1 | $p_2$ | 1 | $p_1$ | $p_0$ | **Step 4 — Compute parity bits (even parity).** * $p_0$ (pos 1) checks positions whose binary has bit 0 = 1: positions **1, 3, 5, 7**. Sum = $0+1+1+1 = 3$ (odd), so $p_0 = 1$. * $p_1$ (pos 2) checks positions with bit 1 = 1: positions **2, 3, 6, 7**. Sum = $0+1+0+1 = 2$ (even), so $p_1 = 0$. * $p_2$ (pos 4) checks positions with bit 2 = 1: positions **4, 5, 6, 7**. Sum = $0+1+0+1 = 2$ (even), so $p_2 = 0$. **Step 5 — Write the codeword.** Reading positions 7 down to 1: $$\text{Codeword} = 1\,0\,1\,0\,1\,0\,1 = 1010101$$ **Step 6 — Decoding example (single-bit error).** Suppose bit at position 6 flips to 1 (received = $1110101$). Recompute syndrome bits: * $s_0$ = parity of {1, 3, 5, 7} = $1+1+1+1 = 0$ (even) ⇒ $s_0 = 0$. * $s_1$ = parity of {2, 3, 6, 7} = $0+1+1+1 = 1$ (odd) ⇒ $s_1 = 1$. * $s_2$ = parity of {4, 5, 6, 7} = $0+1+1+1 = 1$ (odd) ⇒ $s_2 = 1$. Syndrome $S = s_2 s_1 s_0 = 110_2 = 6$. **Error at position 6 — flip it back to 0.** ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link Utilization for Stop-and-Wait > **Problem:** A channel has bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps, one-way propagation delay $T_p = 20$ ms, and the frame length is $L = 2000$ bits. Find the link utilization assuming Stop-and-Wait ARQ. What window size $W$ is required for Sliding Window to achieve near 100% utilization? **Step 1 — Compute the frame transmission time.** $$ T_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the parameter $a$.** $$ a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{2 \text{ ms}} = 10 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Stop-and-Wait utilization formula.** $$ U_{\text{S\&W}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 20} = \frac{1}{21} \approx 0.0476 = 4.76\% $$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** Only 4.76% of the link's capacity is used for productive data. The link is **starved** by waiting for ACKs. **Step 5 — Sliding Window minimum window.** $$ W_{\min} = 1 + 2a = 1 + 20 = 21 \text{ frames} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Insight:** If $a = 10$ and the question asks "what is the minimum window size to keep the link busy?", the answer is **$W = 1 + 2a = 21$**, *not* $W = 2a = 20$. The extra 1 accounts for the in-flight frame at the instant the sender finishes transmitting the window. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three ARQ Strategies | Property | Stop-and-Wait | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat | |---|---|---|---| | **Window size (sender)** | 1 | $W$ (typically $2^k - 1$) | $W$ (typically $2^{k-1}$) | | **Window size (receiver)** | 1 | 1 | $W$ | | **Out-of-order frames accepted?** | No | No | Yes | | **Receiver buffering** | None | None | Required (must hold out-of-order frames) | | **On frame loss** | Retransmit 1 frame | Retransmit from lost frame onward | Retransmit only the lost frame | | **Link utilization** | $\frac{1}{1+2a}$ | $\frac{W}{1+2a}$ for $W < 2a+1$, else 1 | $\frac{W}{1+2a}$ for $W < 2a+1$, else 1 | | **Complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | | **Best suited for** | Very low BER + low $a$ | Moderate BER | High BER, large $a$ | --- ## 3.5 Hamming Distance — Worked Example > **Problem:** A code has codewords $\{000000, 001111, 010110, 011001, 100011, 101100, 110101, 111010\}$. Find the minimum Hamming distance and the maximum error detection/correction capability. **Step 1 — Compute pairwise distances.** Pick representative codewords: * $d(000000, 001111) = 4$ * $d(000000, 010110) = 3$ * $d(000000, 011001) = 3$ * $d(000000, 100011) = 3$ * $d(001111, 010110) = 3$ * ... Continuing, $d_{\min} = 3$. **Step 2 — Apply the relations.** * Detect $s$ errors: $d_{\min} \ge s + 1 \Rightarrow s \le 2$. Can detect up to **2 errors**. * Correct $t$ errors: $d_{\min} \ge 2t + 1 \Rightarrow t \le 1$. Can correct up to **1 error**. > [!TIP] > **Code Reconstruction:** This is the classic (7,4) Hamming code, where $n = 7$, $k = 4$, $m = 3$. The single-error correction capability is the defining feature of the Hamming code family. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Data Link Layer Position in the Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB APPL["Application Layer
HTTP, DNS, FTP"] TRAN["Transport Layer
TCP, UDP"] NET["Network Layer
IP, ICMP, Routing"] DLL["Data Link Layer
Framing, Error Control,
Flow Control, MAC"] PHY["Physical Layer
Bits, Voltages, Modulation"] APPL --> TRAN --> NET --> DLL --> PHY PHY -. raw bits .-> DLL DLL -. reliable frames .-> NET ``` > The DLL is the *boundary* between the **software-driven reliability** (Layers 3+) and the **hardware-driven unreliability** (Layer 1). ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Four Design Issues ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DLLBlock["DATA LINK LAYER"] direction TB SVC["Service to Network Layer

Layer 3 PDU"] -->|upper SDU| SVC FLC -->|lower SDU| PH["Physical Layer

01111110"] ADR["Address
8 bits"] CTL["Control
8 bits"] INF["Information
Variable"] FCS["FCS
CRC-16/32"] FLG2["Flag
01111110"] ``` | Field | Purpose | |---|---| | **Flag** | Delimits start/end of frame; uses bit stuffing for transparency | | **Address** | Destination (and sometimes source) MAC address | | **Control** | Frame type (I-frame, S-frame, U-frame), sequence numbers | | **Information** | Upper-layer payload | | **FCS** | Frame Check Sequence — the CRC for error detection | ## 4.4 Bit Stuffing Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Sender reads next bit from payload"] --> B{"Count of consecutive 1s so far"} B -- "Less than 5" --> C["Transmit bit normally"] --> D["Increment 1-count if bit is 1, else reset"] --> A B -- "Exactly 5" --> E["Insert a stuffed 0 bit"] --> F["Reset 1-count to 0"] --> A G["Receiver side: after five 1s, examine next bit"] --> H{"Is the next bit a 0?"} H -- "Yes" --> I["Discard the stuffed 0; reset counter"] --> A H -- "No: next bit is 1" --> J["This is the end-of-frame flag 01111110"] ``` ## 4.5 Stop-and-Wait ARQ — Time Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Sender participant C as Channel participant R as Receiver S->>C: Frame 0 C->>R: Frame 0 R->>C: ACK 1 C->>S: ACK 1 S->>C: Frame 1 C->>R: Frame 1 (lost in channel) R-->>S: ACK 1 never sent S->>S: Timeout fires S->>C: Retransmit Frame 1 C->>R: Frame 1 (delivered this time) R->>C: ACK 2 C->>S: ACK 2 S->>C: Frame 2 ``` ## 4.6 Sliding Window — Go-Back-N vs. Selective Repeat ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GBN["Go-Back-N (GBN)"] G1["Sender transmits F0, F1, F2, F3, F4"] --> G2["F2 lost in transit"] G2 --> G3["Receiver discards F2, F3, F4
(out-of-order, not buffered)"] G3 --> G4["Sender timeout for F2"] G4 --> G5["Sender retransmits F2, F3, F4"] end subgraph SR["Selective Repeat (SR)"] S1["Sender transmits F0, F1, F2, F3, F4"] --> S2["F2 lost in transit"] S2 --> S3["Receiver buffers F3, F4
(in case F2 can be retransmitted)"] S3 --> S4["Sender timeout for F2 (only)"] S4 --> S5["Sender retransmits only F2"] S5 --> S6["Receiver delivers F2, then F3, F4 in order"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Visual Takeaway:** In GBN, **all frames after a lost one are discarded** by the receiver. In SR, **only the lost frame is retransmitted** — a clear efficiency gain at the cost of receiver buffering complexity. ## 4.7 CRC Engine — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR MSG["Message Bits
M(x)"] --> SHIFT["Shift Register
r stages
Initial: 0"] SHIFT --> DIV["Mod-2 Division
by G(x)"] DIV --> REM["Remainder R(x)"] REM --> APPEND["Append to M(x)"] APPEND --> TX["Transmitted Codeword
T(x) = x^r M(x) + R(x)"] TX --> CHAN["Channel
(may flip bits)"] CHAN --> RX["Receiver T prime x"] RX --> RDIV["Divide T prime x by G x"] RDIV --> RCHK{"Remainder = 0?"} RCHK -- "Yes" --> ACC["Accept Frame"] RCHK -- "No" --> REJ["Discard / NACK"] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** List and briefly explain the **four design issues** of the Data Link Layer. **Model Answer (Examiner-Verbatim, 3 marks):** The four design issues of the Data Link Layer are: 1. **Service to the Network Layer** — Defines the type of service (connectionless/connection-oriented, acknowledged/unacknowledged) offered to the upper layer. 2. **Framing** — The DLL must divide the bitstream from the Physical Layer into discrete, addressable units called *frames* and handle transparency through techniques like *bit stuffing* or *byte stuffing*. 3. **Error Control** — Detects and corrects bit errors introduced by the Physical Layer using error-detection codes (parity, CRC, checksum) and ARQ retransmission strategies. 4. **Flow Control** — Ensures that a fast sender does not overwhelm a slow receiver using protocols such as Stop-and-Wait or Sliding Window. *(Award 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for one-line definitions, 1 mark for examples — total 3 marks.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **bit stuffing** and **byte stuffing** in the context of Data Link Layer framing. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Bit Stuffing | Byte Stuffing | |---|---|---| | **Basic unit** | Individual bits | Entire bytes/characters | | **Trigger** | Five consecutive `1`s in payload | Flag byte appearing in payload | | **Insertion** | A `0` bit is automatically inserted | An ESC (escape) byte is inserted before the flag | | **Used in** | HDLC, PPP, USB | BISYNC, older ASCII protocols | | **Transparency mechanism** | Receiver deletes any `0` after five `1`s | Receiver deletes any ESC preceding a flag | | **Frame delimiter** | `01111110` | Special flag byte, e.g., `0x7E` | *(3 marks: 1 for correct definitions, 1 for the trigger rule, 1 for at least one example protocol.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — *(Module 2, 14 Marks)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **four services** that the Data Link Layer can provide to the Network Layer with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** for flow control. A channel has bandwidth 4 Mbps, one-way propagation delay 25 ms, and frame length 4000 bits. Compute the link utilization and the minimum sliding-window size needed for full utilization. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks The Data Link Layer provides three canonical services (sometimes categorized as four in KTU texts by separating acknowledged/unacknowledged): 1. **Unacknowledged Connectionless Service** (3 marks breakdown) * Frames are sent without establishing a connection and without waiting for an ACK. * Lost frames are not recovered; the Network Layer handles reliability. * **Use Case:** Ethernet LANs (IEEE 802.3), where the error rate is low and retransmission overhead is unacceptable for real-time traffic. * **Advantage:** Low latency, minimal protocol overhead. 2. **Acknowledged Connectionless Service** (2 marks) * Frames are still sent without a prior connection, but each frame is individually acknowledged. * **Use Case:** IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) — frames may be lost, so the receiver sends an ACK; unACKed frames are retransmitted by the sender. * **Advantage:** Reliable delivery for connectionless, bursty traffic. 3. **Acknowledged Connection-Oriented Service** (2 marks) * A virtual connection is established first; all frames are numbered, ACK'd, and delivered in order. * **Use Case:** HDLC over long noisy links, satellite links. * **Advantage:** Guarantees ordered, reliable delivery. *Examiner Note: Award 1 mark per correct service, 1 mark for use case / example. Total 7 marks.* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Stop-and-Wait ARQ description (3 marks):** Stop-and-Wait ARQ is a flow + error control mechanism where the sender transmits **one frame** and then waits for an acknowledgment (**ACK**) from the receiver before sending the next frame. The sender starts a **timer** upon transmission. If the ACK is received before timeout, the next frame is sent. If the timer expires, the sender **retransmits** the same frame. A **sequence number** (alternating 0 and 1) is used in the frame and the ACK to distinguish a fresh frame from a duplicate (caused by a delayed ACK arriving after a retransmission). Variants of Stop-and-Wait are widely used in half-duplex channels and simple embedded systems. **Step 2 — Numerical computation (4 marks):** Given: * $R = 4$ Mbps $= 4 \times 10^6$ bps * $T_p = 25$ ms $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ s * $L = 4000$ bits Compute transmission time: $$ T_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{4000}{4 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} $$ Compute parameter $a$: $$ a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{25 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 25 $$ Compute Stop-and-Wait utilization: $$ U_{\text{S\&W}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 50} = \frac{1}{51} \approx 0.0196 = 1.96\% $$ Compute the minimum sliding-window size for near-100% utilization: $$ W_{\min} = 1 + 2a = 1 + 50 = 51 \text{ frames} $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Stop-and-Wait utilizes only ~2% of the 4 Mbps link. To fully utilize the link, the sender must use a **Sliding Window protocol with at least 51 outstanding frames**. --- ### Question B — *(Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **Hamming code** error-correcting technique. Construct the Hamming code for the data word `1011` using even parity. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With neat diagrams, explain **Go-Back-N ARQ** and **Selective Repeat ARQ**. Compare their efficiency on a noisy channel of bandwidth 1 Mbps, propagation delay 20 ms, and frame size 1000 bits. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Hamming Code Theory (3 marks):** Hamming codes are a family of **linear error-correcting codes** that can detect up to 2-bit errors and correct up to 1-bit error. For a codeword of $n$ bits containing $k$ data bits, the number of redundant parity bits $m$ must satisfy: $$ 2^m \ge m + k + 1 $$ Each parity bit $p_i$ (at position $2^{i-1}$) covers all bit positions whose binary representation has a `1` in the $i$-th position. The **syndrome** (computed at the receiver by recomputing parity) gives the binary position of the single-bit error (syndrome = 0 implies no error). **Construction (4 marks):** For $k = 4$, find $m$: $2^3 = 8 \ge 3 + 4 + 1 = 8$, so $m = 3$, total $n = 7$. Place data bits $d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0 = 1\,0\,1\,1$ at positions 7, 6, 5, 3. Parity bits $p_2, p_1, p_0$ go at positions 4, 2, 1. | Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Role | $d_3$ | $d_2$ | $d_1$ | $p_2$ | $d_0$ | $p_1$ | $p_0$ | | Bit | 1 | 0 | 1 | $p_2$ | 1 | $p_1$ | $p_0$ | Compute parity bits (even parity): * $p_0$ (covers 1, 3, 5, 7) = make even ⇒ $0+1+1+1 = 3$ (odd) ⇒ $p_0 = 1$ * $p_1$ (covers 2, 3, 6, 7) = make even ⇒ $0+1+0+1 = 2$ (even) ⇒ $p_1 = 0$ * $p_2$ (covers 4, 5, 6, 7) = make even ⇒ $0+1+0+1 = 2$ (even) ⇒ $p_2 = 0$ **Codeword = 1010101.** (For full marks, include the decoding procedure: receiver computes syndrome, syndrome = 0 ⇒ no error; syndrome = position of the bit to flip; syndrome non-zero and not a power of 2 ⇒ uncorrectable double error.) --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Go-Back-N ARQ (3 marks):** In Go-Back-N, the sender maintains a **send window of size $W$** and can transmit up to $W$ unacknowledged frames. The receiver has a **receive window of size 1** — it only accepts frames in strict sequence order. If a frame is lost or arrives corrupted, the receiver discards it and all subsequent frames (since they are out of order). The sender, upon timeout for the lost frame, **retransmits that frame and all subsequent frames** (it "goes back N"). **Selective Repeat ARQ (2 marks):** In Selective Repeat, both sender and receiver maintain windows of size $W$ (typically $2^{k-1}$ where $k$ is the sequence-number field size). The receiver **buffers out-of-order frames** and sends a **selective ACK (SACK)** for each correctly received frame. The sender retransmits **only the lost frame**. SR is more bandwidth-efficient on noisy channels but requires more receiver memory. **Numerical Comparison (2 marks):** * $T_f = 1000/10^6 = 1$ ms * $a = T_p / T_f = 20/1 = 20$ * GBN max window = $2^k - 1$ (for $k = 8$, max $W = 255$). For full utilization, $W \ge 2a+1 = 41$. Achievable. Efficiency = $\min(W, 1+2a)/(1+2a)$. * SR max window = $2^{k-1} = 128$. Full utilization achievable with $W = 41$. Efficiency on a noisy channel is **higher than GBN** because SR does not retransmit the $W-1$ frames after the lost one. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Data Link Layer Design Issues":** > 1. **Confusing framing with encryption/authentication** — Framing is *structural* delimitation, not security. > 2. **Wrong CRC remainder length** — The CRC remainder MUST be padded to $r$ bits (the degree of $G(x)$). Writing `11` instead of `011` for a degree-3 generator loses 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting the +1 in $W = 1 + 2a$** — A common slip; the extra 1 accounts for the in-flight frame. > 4. **Mixing up Hamming detection and correction** — Detection requires $d_{\min} \ge s+1$; correction requires $d_{\min} \ge 2t+1$. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 5. **Not drawing the sliding window diagram** — In 14-mark questions, a clear labeled diagram of the send/receive windows with pointers (send_base, nextseqnum, rcv_base) carries 2–3 marks on its own. > 6. **Confusing bit stuffing and byte stuffing** — Many students answer with byte-stuffing rules when asked about bit stuffing. > 7. **Ignoring the sequence number in Stop-and-Wait** — The alternating 0/1 sequence number is what resolves the "duplicate frame" ambiguity, not an optional extra. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **DLL = Layer 2** — sits between Network (3) and Physical (1); provides **node-to-node** delivery. - **Four design issues (high-priority):** Service to Network Layer, Framing, Error Control, Flow Control. - **Three service models:** Unacknowledged connectionless (Ethernet), acknowledged connectionless (Wi-Fi), acknowledged connection-oriented (HDLC). - **Framing methods:** Character count (weak), flag bytes + byte stuffing, **bit stuffing (HDLC standard)**, physical layer coding violations (Token Ring). - **Bit stuffing rule:** Sender inserts a `0` after **five consecutive `1`s**; receiver deletes any `0` that follows five `1`s. - **Error detection codes:** Parity (single-bit), Checksum (TCP/UDP), **CRC (Ethernet, Wi-Fi — strongest)**. Generator $G(x)$ of degree $r$ detects all bursts of length $\le r$ and $\ge 99.99\%$ of longer bursts. - **CRC computation:** Append $r$ zeros, divide by $G(x)$ in GF(2), append the remainder. - **Hamming code:** Uses $m$ parity bits where $2^m \ge m+k+1$; corrects any **single-bit error**; syndrome gives the bit position. - **Hamming distance:** $d_{\min} \ge s+1$ to *detect* $s$ errors; $d_{\min} \ge 2t+1$ to *correct* $t$ errors. - **Stop-and-Wait ARQ:** $U = 1/(1+2a)$, $a = T_p/T_f$; one outstanding frame. - **Sliding Window:** Pipeline of $W$ unACKed frames; $W_{\min} = 1+2a$ for full utilization. - **Go-Back-N:** Receiver window = 1; retransmits *from* the lost frame. Max sender window $= 2^k - 1$. - **Selective Repeat:** Receiver window = $W$; buffers out-of-order frames. Max sender window $= 2^{k-1}$. - **Sub-layers:** **LLC (802.2)** — framing, error & flow control; **MAC (802.3/11)** — medium access. - **Real-world CRC polynomials:** CRC-32 = $x^{32}+x^{26}+x^{23}+x^{22}+x^{16}+x^{12}+x^{11}+x^{10}+x^8+x^7+x^5+x^4+x^2+x+1$ (Ethernet, ZIP, PNG). - **Key intuition:** Without DLL design, the **bit error rate** of the Physical Layer (e.g., $10^{-6}$ for copper, $10^{-3}$ for wireless) would propagate up to the application; DLL design brings it down to **practically zero** for the upper layers.

topics.title: Flow Control and ARQ Techniques; Data Link Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Control and ARQ Techniques; Data Link Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flow Control – The Foundation of Reliable Data Link Communication

### Formal Definition
**Flow Control** is a Data Link Layer function that coordinates the rate of data transmission between a sender and a receiver so that the sender does not transmit data faster than the receiver can process it. It ensures that the receiver's buffer does not overflow when a fast sender communicates with a slower receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Flow Control is explicitly listed under Module 2 of the KTU 2024 B.Tech (CSE) syllabus for PBCCT304. It is a direct CO1 (Apply the fundamental concepts of computer networks) and CO2 (Analyze various error control and flow control mechanisms) mapping topic.

The two principal categories of Flow Control in the KTU syllabus are:
- **Stop-and-Wait Flow Control** – Sender transmits one frame and waits for an acknowledgment (ACK).
- **Sliding Window Flow Control** – Sender can transmit multiple frames (up to a window size $W$) before requiring an ACK.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Bucket and the Tap Analogy**
> Imagine a water tap pouring water into a bucket. The tap (sender) can pour water (data) at a high pressure, but the bucket (receiver) can only hold a limited amount. If the tap pours too fast, the bucket overflows. **Flow control is the valve** that opens and closes to match the tap's pour rate with the bucket's drainage rate. In Stop-and-Wait, the tap pours one glass, waits for the bucket to drain, then pours again. In Sliding Window, the tap pours many glasses in advance (up to the bucket's capacity) and only waits when the bucket is full.

### Why Flow Control is Required
- **Speed Mismatch:** Sender (e.g., a server) and receiver (e.g., a mobile phone) operate at different processing speeds.
- **Buffer Limitation:** The receiver has a finite **receive buffer** of capacity $B$ bytes.
- **End-to-End Reliability:** Required over noisy or long-distance links where packets may be lost or delayed.
- **Resource Optimization:** Prevents wastage of bandwidth due to retransmissions triggered by buffer overflow.

### Performance Metric (Standard KTU Notation)
The dimensionless parameter **$a$** defines the relationship between propagation and transmission times:

$$a = \frac{\text{Propagation Time } (T_p)}{\text{Transmission Time } (T_t)}$$

This parameter is the **single most important ratio** in all flow control and ARQ efficiency derivations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Units to Remember:**
> - Bandwidth ($R$): **bits per second (bps)**
> - Frame Length ($L$): **bits**
> - Distance ($d$): **meters (m)**
> - Propagation Speed ($v$): **meters/second (m/s)** (typically $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber)
> - Round Trip Time (RTT): **seconds**

---

## 1.2 ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) Techniques

### Formal Definition
**ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** is an **error-control protocol** at the Data Link Layer where the receiver uses **acknowledgments (ACKs/NACKs)** to inform the sender about successfully or unsuccessfully received frames. Lost or corrupted frames are **retransmitted automatically** after a timeout, ensuring reliable, in-order delivery over an unreliable physical channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard):** ARQ is the cornerstone of the Data Link Layer's reliability mechanism. It combines error detection (using CRC/Parity), acknowledgments, sequence numbers, and timers to guarantee delivery.

### The Three KTU-Prescribed ARQ Protocols
The KTU 2024 Scheme prescribes the following three ARQ variants, each progressively more bandwidth-efficient:

| # | ARQ Protocol | Window Size | Receiver Behavior on Error | Complexity |
|---|--------------|-------------|----------------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** | $W = 1$ | Discards all subsequent frames until the erroneous one is corrected | Very Low |
| 2 | **Go-Back-N ARQ** | $W = N$ | Discards the erroneous frame **and all subsequent frames** | Moderate |
| 3 | **Selective Repeat ARQ** | $W = N$ | **Buffers (stores)** out-of-order frames; retransmits only the missing one | High |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Postman and the Receipt Book**
> Think of a postman delivering registered letters:
> - **Stop-and-Wait:** The postman delivers ONE letter, stands at the door, waits for a signed receipt, then comes back to the post office for the next letter. Slow but absolutely sure.
> - **Go-Back-N:** The postman delivers 5 letters in a row. If letter #3 is returned with a complaint, he goes **all the way back** and redelivers letters #3, #4, and #5 — even if #4 and #5 were received correctly.
> - **Selective Repeat:** The postman delivers 5 letters. If #3 is missing, the receiver (who has kept #4 and #5 in a tray) asks ONLY for #3. Efficient but the receiver needs a bigger "tray" (buffer).

### The Three ARQ Building Blocks
1. **Error Detection** – CRC, Checksum, or Parity bits.
2. **Acknowledgment (ACK)** – Positive feedback for a correctly received frame; **NAK (Negative ACK)** for errors.
3. **Sequence Numbers** – To identify frame order and detect duplicates. Range: $0$ to $2^k - 1$, where $k$ is the sequence number bit length.

---

## 1.3 Data Link Protocols

### Formal Definition
**Data Link Protocols** are standardized sets of rules that govern the exchange of frames between two adjacent nodes on a network. They define frame format, addressing, error handling, flow control, and the physical link management procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Mandate:** The student must be able to explain **HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control)** and **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** with their frame structures and operational modes. These are frequently asked in the University Exam for 7-mark sub-questions.

### HDLC – High-Level Data Link Control
- **Developed by:** ISO (International Organization for Standardization).
- **Bit-Oriented Protocol** – Uses a fixed bit pattern `01111110` as the **flag** delimiter.
- **Supports both** point-to-point and multipoint links.
- **Three Station Types:** Primary, Secondary, and Combined.
- **Three Frame Types:** Information (I-frames), Supervisory (S-frames), and Unnumbered (U-frames).

### PPP – Point-to-Point Protocol
- **Defined in:** RFC 1661 (IETF Standard).
- **Byte-Oriented Protocol** – Uses the byte `01111110` (0x7E) as the flag.
- **Used For:** Direct connections between two nodes (e.g., dial-up, DSL, serial links).
- **Three Components:** LCP (Link Control Protocol), NCP (Network Control Protocol), and the actual encapsulation method.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Postal Service Rules**
> A Data Link Protocol is like the **rulebook of a postal service**. It defines the size of the envelope (frame format), the language of the address (header), how to detect a torn envelope (error detection), and what to do if the address is unreadable (error recovery). HDLC and PPP are two different national postal standards — both deliver mail, but the envelope designs and procedures differ.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transmission Time, Propagation Time, and the Parameter '$a$'

These three quantities are the **mathematical foundation** of every flow control and ARQ problem in the KTU exam.

### Transmission Time ($T_t$)
The time required for the sender to push all bits of a frame onto the transmission medium.

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Frame Length (in **bits**)
- $R$ = Link Bandwidth (in **bits/second**)

### Propagation Time ($T_p$)
The time taken by one bit to physically travel from sender to receiver across the medium.

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v}$$

Where:
- $d$ = Link Length (in **meters**)
- $v$ = Signal Propagation Speed (typically $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable/fiber)

### The Parameter $a$ (a.k.a. Bandwidth-Delay Product Ratio)
The ratio of propagation time to transmission time:

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{d/v}{L/R} = \frac{R \cdot d}{v \cdot L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $a$ matters:** It tells you how many frames can "fit" in the wire at any given instant. A high $a$ means the link is **long and fat** (lots of bandwidth-delay product), and Stop-and-Wait becomes terribly inefficient. A low $a$ means Stop-and-Wait is acceptable.

### Bandwidth-Delay Product
The volume of data (in bits) that can be "in flight" on the link at any time:

$$\text{BDP} = R \times T_p = R \times \frac{d}{v} \quad \text{(bits)}$$

---

## 2.2 Stop-and-Wait ARQ – Detailed Operational Analysis

### Operational Steps
1. **Sender** transmits one frame and starts a **timer**.
2. **Receiver** receives the frame, checks for errors using CRC.
3. If correct, receiver sends **ACK**; if erroneous, receiver sends **NAK** (or simply discards).
4. **If ACK arrives before timer expiry** → sender transmits the next frame.
5. **If timer expires (timeout)** OR **NAK is received** → sender retransmits the same frame.
6. **If ACK is lost** → sender retransmits; receiver detects duplicate using **sequence number (0 or 1)**.

### Sequence Numbering
- Only **two sequence numbers** are needed: $0$ and $1$ (alternating).
- The receiver can distinguish a fresh frame from a retransmitted one.

### Efficiency Derivation Setup
For a **successfully transmitted frame** in Stop-and-Wait ARQ:

$$\text{Total Time} = T_t + T_p + T_p + T_t = T_t + 2T_p$$

(Sender transmits frame → waits $T_p$ for arrival → receiver sends ACK → ACK travels back in $T_p$ → sender receives ACK in $T_t$ transmission time of ACK, but if ACK is very small, $T_t^{ack} \to 0$).

**Useful Time** = $T_t$ (only one frame was transmitted)
**Total Cycle Time** = $T_t + 2T_p$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If the problem does NOT say to ignore the ACK transmission time, you must include it. The examiner will deduct marks if you silently assume $T_t^{ack} = 0$.

---

## 2.3 Go-Back-N ARQ – Deep Dive

### Concept
- Sender can transmit up to **$N$ frames** without waiting for an ACK (a sliding window of size $N$).
- $N$ is typically $2^k - 1$, where $k$ is the number of sequence number bits.
- The receiver accepts frames **only in order**. If frame $i$ is lost or corrupted, **all subsequent frames ($i+1, i+2, ..., i+N-1$) are discarded**, and a **NAK(i)** is sent.
- The sender, on receiving NAK, **goes back** to frame $i$ and retransmits $i, i+1, ..., i+N-1$.

### Why the Sequence Number Range is $2^k - 1$ for Go-Back-N
- If we had $2^k$ possible sequence numbers (e.g., 0, 1, 2, 3 for $k=2$), the sender could not distinguish between a new window starting at 0 and an old retransmission. With $N = 2^k - 1 = 3$, the ambiguity is resolved.

### Pipelining Effect
- Go-Back-N **pipelines** frames, meaning the sender never has to wait (under ideal conditions).
- Channel is kept busy continuously, achieving high utilization.

### Efficiency Formula (KTU High-Yield)

$$\eta_{GBN} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a} & \text{if } N < 1 + 2a \\[10pt] 1 \text{ (i.e., 100\%)} & \text{if } N \geq 1 + 2a \end{cases}$$

The boundary condition $N = 1 + 2a$ means: the window size is large enough to "fill the pipe" completely, so the sender is never idle.

---

## 2.4 Selective Repeat ARQ – Deep Dive

### Concept
- Sender and receiver both maintain a **window of size $N$**.
- The receiver **accepts out-of-order frames** and buffers them.
- Only the **missing/damaged frame** is retransmitted (not the entire window).
- This requires a **larger receiver buffer** than Go-Back-N.

### Sequence Number Range
- For Selective Repeat, the window size $N$ must be **$\leq 2^{k-1}$** (half the sequence number space).
- Example: If $k = 3$ bits, sequence numbers are $0$ to $7$, and the maximum window size is $4$ (i.e., $2^{3-1}$).

### Efficiency Formula (KTU High-Yield)

$$\eta_{SR} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a} & \text{if } N < 1 + 2a \\[10pt] 1 \text{ (i.e., 100\%)} & \text{if } N \geq 1 + 2a \end{cases}$$

### Comparison: Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat
| Feature | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| Receiver buffer | Small (1 frame) | Large ($N$ frames) |
| Wasted bandwidth on error | High (retransmits good frames too) | Low (only bad frame) |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Preferred for | Low error links | High error links |

---

## 2.5 HDLC – The High-Level Data Link Control Protocol

### HDLC Frame Structure
The HDLC frame has **six fields**:

| Flag (8 bits) | Address (8/16 bits) | Control (8/16 bits) | Information (variable) | FCS (16/32 bits) | Flag (8 bits) |
|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------|---------------|

- **Flag:** `01111110` – marks start and end of frame.
- **Address:** Identifies the secondary station.
- **Control:** Identifies the frame type (I, S, or U) and contains sequence/control bits.
- **Information:** The actual payload (only in I-frames and some U-frames).
- **FCS:** Frame Check Sequence (CRC-16 or CRC-32) for error detection.

### Bit Stuffing (Transparency)
To prevent the flag pattern `01111110` from appearing inside the data:
- Sender inserts an extra `0` after every sequence of **five consecutive 1s** in the data.
- Receiver removes the stuffed `0` after every five 1s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Bit stuffing is a 2-mark favorite. The rule is "5 ones in a row → stuff a 0." If a frame has 20 ones in its data field, 4 zeroes are stuffed. This is critical for HDLC.

### HDLC Frame Types
1. **I-Frames (Information):** Carry user data, contain send and receive sequence numbers.
2. **S-Frames (Supervisory):** For flow control and error control (ACK, NAK, Ready, Reject, Selective Reject). **No user data.**
3. **U-Frames (Unnumbered):** For link management (Set, Disconnect, Reset, etc.). Can carry data.

---

## 2.6 PPP – Point-to-Point Protocol

### PPP Frame Structure

| Flag (1 byte) | Address (1 byte) | Control (1 byte) | Protocol (1–2 bytes) | Information (variable) | FCS (2/4 bytes) | Flag (1 byte) |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------|---------------|

- **Flag:** `01111110` (0x7E).
- **Address:** Always `11111111` (0xFF) in PPP (broadcast).
- **Control:** Always `00000011` (0x03), indicating unnumbered information.
- **Protocol:** Indicates the network layer protocol encapsulated (e.g., 0x0021 for IP, 0xC021 for LCP).
- **FCS:** CRC for error detection.

### Byte Stuffing in PPP
- If the byte `0x7E` (flag) appears in the data, it is replaced by `0x7D 0x5E`.
- If `0x7D` appears, it is replaced by `0x7D 0x5D`.
- If ASCII control characters (< 0x20) appear, they are escaped with `0x7D`.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Transmission Time | $T_t = L / R$ | seconds |
| 2 | Propagation Time | $T_p = d / v$ | seconds |
| 3 | Parameter $a$ | $a = T_p / T_t = (R \cdot d) / (v \cdot L)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R \cdot T_p$ | bits |
| 5 | Stop-and-Wait Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ (or $\eta = (1 - 2a)$ if $a < 0.5$) | fraction (0 to 1) |
| 6 | Go-Back-N Window Size | $N = 2^k - 1$ | frames ($k$ = seq. bits) |
| 7 | Selective Repeat Window Size | $N = 2^{k-1}$ | frames |
| 8 | GBN / SR Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a}$ for $N < 1+2a$, else $\eta = 1$ | fraction |
| 9 | HDLC Bit Stuffing | One `0` inserted after every 5 consecutive `1`s | bits |
| 10 | HDLC Sequence Number Range | $0$ to $2^k - 1$ | integers |
| 11 | Round Trip Time | $\text{RTT} = 2 \cdot T_p$ | seconds |
| 12 | Utilization at Sender | $U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In the table above, I have deliberately written $\vert$ for any absolute-value-like notation and used `\dfrac{}{}` for fractions to prevent markdown table rendering errors in the KTU PDF exporter.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **HDLC** is the basis of many modern protocols, including **LAPB** (used in X.25 networks), **LAPD** (ISDN), and **LAPF** (Frame Relay). Even the **PPP** protocol used for direct internet dial-up is essentially a simplified version of HDLC.
- **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** is still used in **short-range, low-error links** such as infrared remotes, Bluetooth SPP, and RFID systems.
- **Selective Repeat ARQ** is used in modern **TCP** (a variant of it), satellite communications, and high-speed cellular links (4G/5G RLC layer).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stop-and-Wait ARQ Efficiency

### Given Data
- Frame length $L$ (in bits)
- Bandwidth $R$ (in bits per second)
- One-way propagation delay $T_p = d / v$ (in seconds)
- ACK frame length is negligible ($L_{ack} \to 0$)

### Step 1 – Compute Transmission Time
The time the sender takes to push $L$ bits onto the wire is the transmission time:

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R}$$

> **Logic:** If $L$ bits must be placed on a link of capacity $R$ bits per second, dividing the work by the rate gives the time. For example, 1000 bits at 1000 bps → 1 second.

### Step 2 – Compute One-Way Propagation Time
The time for a single bit to travel from sender to receiver:

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v}$$

> **Logic:** Distance divided by signal speed. For a 2000 km fiber link, $T_p = (2 \times 10^6) / (2 \times 10^8) = 0.01$ s = 10 ms.

### Step 3 – Define the Parameter $a$
The ratio of propagation to transmission time is a dimensionless quantity:

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{d/v}{L/R} = \frac{R \cdot d}{v \cdot L}$$

> **Logic:** $a$ is the "how many frames can be in flight" measure. If $a = 4$, it means 4 frames-worth of data could be on the wire before the first bit reaches the receiver.

### Step 4 – Total Cycle Time for One Frame (No Error Case)
The sender does the following in one successful cycle:
- Transmit one frame: $T_t$ seconds
- Wait for frame to reach receiver: $T_p$ seconds
- Receiver processes and sends ACK (instantaneous)
- ACK travels back to sender: $T_p$ seconds
- Sender is now ready for the next frame.

Total time for one frame's worth of useful work:

$$\text{Cycle Time} = T_t + 2T_p$$

### Step 5 – Useful Time
Only $T_t$ seconds of "useful" data transmission occurred (one frame):

$$\text{Useful Time} = T_t$$

### Step 6 – Efficiency Definition
Efficiency is the ratio of useful time to total time:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\eta = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$$

### Step 7 – Express in Terms of $a$
Divide numerator and denominator by $T_t$:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2(T_p/T_t)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$$

### Final Result – Stop-and-Wait ARQ Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}$$

### Step 8 – Physical Interpretation
- If $a \to 0$ (very short link or very long frame): $\eta \to 1$ (100% efficient).
- If $a \to \infty$ (very long link or very short frame): $\eta \to 0$ (terribly inefficient).
- **Critical Threshold:** When $a \geq 0.5$, efficiency drops below 50%. This is when Sliding Window protocols become mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Go-Back-N ARQ Efficiency (Error-Free Case)

### Given Data
- Window size $N$
- Parameter $a$ as derived above
- No errors occur (best case scenario)

### Step 1 – Total Time to Send One Window of $N$ Frames
In a single round trip, the sender pushes out $N$ frames back-to-back, then waits for an ACK for the first one.

The time to transmit $N$ frames is $N \cdot T_t$ (since each frame takes $T_t$ to send).
The time for the first bit of the window to travel to receiver and ACK to return is $2T_p$.

$$\text{Total Cycle Time} = N \cdot T_t + 2T_p$$

### Step 2 – Useful Time
The "useful" transmission is the $N$ frames:

$$\text{Useful Time} = N \cdot T_t$$

### Step 3 – Efficiency
By definition:

$$\eta = \frac{N \cdot T_t}{N \cdot T_t + 2T_p}$$

### Step 4 – Divide by $T_t$

$$\eta = \frac{N}{N + 2(T_p / T_t)} = \frac{N}{N + 2a}$$

### Step 5 – Identify Boundary Condition
The sender can keep the pipe full if the window is large enough to cover the bandwidth-delay product. The condition is:

$$N \geq 1 + 2a$$

If $N \geq 1 + 2a$, the sender finishes transmitting the entire window before the ACK for the first frame returns. Thus, no idle time → $\eta = 1$.

### Final Result – Go-Back-N ARQ Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{GBN} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a} & \text{if } N < 1 + 2a \\[10pt] 1 & \text{if } N \geq 1 + 2a \end{cases}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The boundary is $N < 1 + 2a$, not $N < 2a$. The "+1" comes from the fact that while the ACK is in transit, the sender is still transmitting the last frame of the window. Many students write $N < 2a$ and lose 1 mark.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Format)

**Problem:** A channel has a bandwidth of 1 Mbps and a one-way propagation delay of 20 ms. The frame size is 1000 bits. Find:
1. Transmission time $T_t$
2. Parameter $a$
3. Stop-and-Wait ARQ efficiency
4. Go-Back-N efficiency with $N = 7$
5. Minimum window size for 100% efficiency

### Solution

#### Part 1 – Transmission Time
$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

> **Logic:** 1000 bits pushed at 1 million bits per second takes 1/1000 of a second = 1 ms.

#### Part 2 – Parameter $a$
$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 20$$

> **Logic:** The propagation time is 20 times the transmission time. This means 20 frames-worth of bits could be in flight on the wire at any instant.

#### Part 3 – Stop-and-Wait ARQ Efficiency
$$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 40} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 \approx 2.44\%$$

> **Logic:** Stop-and-Wait is dreadful here! The sender is idle for 40 ms after every 1 ms of work. This is the classic "long fat pipe" problem.

#### Part 4 – Go-Back-N Efficiency with $N = 7$
First, check the boundary: $1 + 2a = 1 + 40 = 41$. Since $N = 7 < 41$:

$$\eta_{GBN} = \frac{N}{1 + 2a} = \frac{7}{41} \approx 0.1707 \approx 17.07\%$$

> **Logic:** Better than 2.44%, but still poor. The window is too small for this high-$a$ link.

#### Part 5 – Minimum Window for 100% Efficiency
The condition for $\eta = 1$ is $N \geq 1 + 2a$:

$$N_{\min} = 1 + 2a = 1 + 40 = 41$$

> **Logic:** The sender needs at least 41 frames outstanding to keep the link fully utilized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** This exact problem format (find $T_t$, $a$, $\eta_{S\&W}$, $\eta_{GBN}$, $N_{\min}$) appears in nearly every KTU University Exam. Practice it until it is muscle memory.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of HDLC Bit Stuffing Count

### Given
- Original data stream containing a certain number of 1s.

### Rule
After every sequence of **five consecutive 1s** in the data (between the opening and closing flags), a single `0` bit is automatically inserted by the sender.

### Derivation Logic
If the original data has $C$ clusters of five consecutive 1s, then $C$ extra `0` bits are stuffed. The transmitted bit count is:

$$L_{\text{transmitted}} = L_{\text{original}} + C$$

> **Example:** Original data = `01111110111011111010`
> - Position 1-6: `011111` → five 1s at positions 2-6 → stuff a 0 → `0111110`
> - Position 10-15: `11111` (positions 10-14) → stuff a 0
> - **Total stuffed bits:** 2
> - **Transmitted length:** Original + 2 bits

> **Reverse at Receiver:** After detecting five 1s, the receiver examines the next bit. If it is a `0`, it is removed (unstuffed). If it is a `1` (making six 1s), it is a flag.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (ARQ Techniques)

| Parameter | Stop-and-Wait | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| Window size $N$ | 1 | $2^k - 1$ | $2^{k-1}$ |
| Sequence bits $k$ | 1 | $\geq 2$ | $\geq 2$ |
| Throughput (low $a$) | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Throughput (high $a$) | Very Low | High (if $N$ large) | Highest |
| Receiver buffer | 1 frame | 1 frame | $N$ frames |
| Wasted BW on error | 1 frame | $N$ frames | 1 frame |
| Complexity | Trivial | Moderate | High |
| Use case | Simple links, low error | Moderate error rate | High error rate, large BDP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`, `frame1`) and contain NO markdown formatting (no `**`, no `*`, no nested tables) inside any quoted label string. All labels are in clean uppercase or proper case text. This ensures the diagrams render correctly in GitHub, Mermaid Live Editor, and the KTU PDF generator.

---

## 4.2 Stop-and-Wait ARQ – Sequence Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START])
    nodeB[Sender transmits frame N]
    nodeC[Start TIMER for frame N]
    nodeD[Frame travels across the medium]
    nodeE{Receiver: Is CRC correct?}
    nodeF[Receiver sends ACK for frame N]
    nodeG[Receiver discards frame and sends NAK]
    nodeH[ACK travels back to sender]
    nodeI{Timer expired OR NAK received?}
    nodeJ[Sender transmits next frame N plus 1]
    nodeK[Sender retransmits the SAME frame N]
    nodeL([END of one cycle])

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeJ
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeD
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The sender transmits a frame and starts a timer.
- The receiver checks the CRC. If correct, it returns an ACK; otherwise, a NAK.
- If the timer expires before an ACK arrives, the sender retransmits the same frame.
- Only after receiving a valid ACK does the sender move to the next frame.

---

## 4.3 Go-Back-N ARQ – Window Transmission and Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Sender Window contains frames 0 to N minus 1]
    nodeB[Sender transmits all N frames back to back]
    nodeC[Receiver receives frame 0 and 1 correctly]
    nodeD[Frame 2 is LOST in transit]
    nodeE[Receiver discards frame 3 4 5 because they are out of order]
    nodeF[Receiver sends NAK 2 back to sender]
    nodeG[Sender receives NAK 2]
    nodeH[Sender goes BACK to frame 2 and retransmits 2 3 4 5]
    nodeI[Receiver accepts the correctly ordered retransmitted frames]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Note the **waste**: even though frames 3, 4, 5 were received correctly, they are **discarded** because frame 2 was lost. They are retransmitted along with frame 2.
- This is why Go-Back-N is wasteful on noisy links.

---

## 4.4 Selective Repeat ARQ – Window Transmission and Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Sender transmits frames 0 to N minus 1]
    nodeB[Receiver buffers all out of order frames]
    nodeC[Frame 2 is LOST in transit]
    nodeD[Receiver sends NAK 2 for the missing frame]
    nodeE[Sender retransmits ONLY frame 2]
    nodeF[Receiver places frame 2 in the correct position]
    nodeG[Receiver delivers all frames 0 to N minus 1 in order to upper layer]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The receiver **buffers** all frames (unlike GBN where they are discarded).
- Only the missing frame is retransmitted, saving bandwidth.
- Requires a larger receiver buffer.

---

## 4.5 HDLC Frame Structure – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Flag 01111110] --> nodeB[Address 8 or 16 bits]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Control 8 or 16 bits]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Information Variable length payload]
    nodeD --> nodeE[FCS 16 or 32 bit CRC]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Flag 01111110]

    subgraph "HDLC Frame"
        nodeA
        nodeB
        nodeC
        nodeD
        nodeE
        nodeF
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The same flag `01111110` opens and closes the frame.
- The Information field is optional (absent in S-frames).
- FCS uses CRC-CCITT (16 bits) or CRC-32 (32 bits).

---

## 4.6 PPP Frame Structure – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Flag 0x7E] --> nodeB[Address 0xFF always]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Control 0x03 always]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Protocol 1 or 2 bytes]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Information variable length]
    nodeE --> nodeF[FCS 2 or 4 bytes]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Flag 0x7E]

    subgraph "PPP Frame"
        nodeA
        nodeB
        nodeC
        nodeD
        nodeE
        nodeF
        nodeG
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The Address and Control fields are constant in PPP.
- The Protocol field identifies the network layer protocol (e.g., 0x0021 for IPv4).
- Byte stuffing (not bit stuffing) is used for transparency.

---

## 4.7 ARQ Protocol Selection – Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Choose an ARQ protocol])
    nodeB{Is the link short and error free?}
    nodeC[Use Stop and Wait ARQ]
    nodeD{Is the error rate low?}
    nodeE[Use Go Back N ARQ]
    nodeF{Is the error rate high and bandwidth valuable?}
    nodeG[Use Selective Repeat ARQ]
    nodeH([Protocol Selected])

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- This is the practical selection logic used in real engineering design.
- Stop-and-Wait is chosen for simplicity on local links.
- Selective Repeat is chosen for satellite or noisy wireless.

---

## 4.8 HDLC Operational Modes – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[HDLC Operational Modes]
    nodeB[NRM Normal Response Mode]
    nodeC[ARM Asynchronous Response Mode]
    nodeD[ABM Asynchronous Balanced Mode]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD

    nodeE[Used in point to point with one primary and one secondary]
    nodeF[Secondary can initiate transmission without polling from primary]
    nodeG[Both stations are combined and can initiate independently]

    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** ABM is the most common in modern point-to-point HDLC links. The station is a "combined" station that can act as both primary and secondary.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"Differentiate between Stop-and-Wait ARQ and Go-Back-N ARQ."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Stop-and-Wait ARQ | Go-Back-N ARQ |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| Window size | 1 frame | $N$ frames (where $N = 2^k - 1$) |
| Efficiency | $\eta = 1 / (1 + 2a)$ (low) | $\eta = N / (1 + 2a)$ (high) |
| Receiver behavior on error | Waits for retransmission of the same frame | Discards the bad frame and all subsequent frames |
| Wasted bandwidth | 1 frame | Up to $N$ frames |
| Sequence numbers | 2 (0 and 1) | $2^k$ |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "efficiency" with "throughput." Efficiency is a dimensionless ratio; throughput is in bits/second. Do not mix them. Also, do not say "Go-Back-N does not have sequence numbers" — it does, but it uses $2^k - 1$ instead of $2^k$.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"What is the function of the Flag field in HDLC? Explain bit stuffing with an example."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Function of the Flag Field:**
The Flag field in HDLC has the bit pattern `01111110` and serves two purposes:
1. **Frame Delimiter:** It marks the beginning and end of every HDLC frame.
2. **Frame Synchronization:** It allows the receiver to identify the start of a new frame.

**Bit Stuffing (Transparency):**
To prevent the flag pattern from appearing inside the user data, the sender inserts a `0` bit after every sequence of **five consecutive 1s**. The receiver removes this stuffed `0` to recover the original data.

**Example:** Suppose the original data is `0110111111100111111010`.
- The sender detects `01111111` (seven 1s) → it becomes `011111011` (six 1s followed by a stuffed 0).
- Similarly, the next `11111` gets a `0` stuffed after it.
- **Stuffed data:** `01101111101100111111010010`

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "after six 1s, stuff a 0." The correct rule is **after FIVE 1s**, stuff a 0. The sixth 1 would be a flag bit, not part of the data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module 2 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into **two sub-parts** of **7 marks each**. Either sub-part can be the compulsory entry question. The student is given an internal choice between two full questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]

**Question:** (a) Explain the **Sliding Window Protocol** with a suitable diagram. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of Stop-and-Wait Protocol. (7 Marks)

**(b) A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kbps and a one-way propagation delay of 20 ms. Calculate:**
- (i) The frame size if the parameter $a = 4$.
- (ii) The efficiency of Stop-and-Wait ARQ.
- (iii) The minimum window size required for 100% efficiency using Go-Back-N. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

The **Sliding Window Protocol** is a flow control technique where both the sender and the receiver maintain a "window" of acceptable frame sequence numbers. The sender can transmit multiple frames (up to the window size $W$) before requiring an acknowledgment. As ACKs are received, the window "slides" forward, permitting new frames to be sent.

**Key Features:**
1. **Sender Window:** Maintains a list of sequence numbers it is permitted to send.
2. **Receiver Window:** Maintains a list of sequence numbers it is prepared to receive.
3. **Pipelining:** The sender continuously transmits frames without waiting, achieving high throughput.
4. **Two Variants:** Go-Back-N (receiver discards out-of-order) and Selective Repeat (receiver buffers out-of-order).

**Diagram (Sender Side):**
```
| 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 |
        ^         ^
        |         |
   Send Base   Next Seq
   (after ACK) (next to send)
```

**How it overcomes Stop-and-Wait limitations:**
- Stop-and-Wait has very low efficiency on long, high-bandwidth links (because $a$ is large).
- Sliding Window allows the sender to keep the pipe full by sending multiple frames in transit, increasing utilization to nearly 100% (when $N \geq 1 + 2a$).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $R = 4 \text{ kbps} = 4 \times 10^3$ bps
- $T_p = 20 \text{ ms} = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ s = $0.02$ s
- $a = 4$

**(i) Frame Size when $a = 4$:**

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} \implies T_t = \frac{T_p}{a} = \frac{0.02}{4} = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

$$L = T_t \times R = 0.005 \times 4 \times 10^3 = 20 \text{ bits}$$

> **Logic:** Since $T_t = L / R$, the frame size is $L = T_t \times R = 5$ ms $\times$ 4 kbps = 20 bits.

**(ii) Stop-and-Wait ARQ Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 8} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111 \approx 11.11\%$$

**(iii) Minimum Window Size for 100% Go-Back-N Efficiency:**

$$N_{\min} = 1 + 2a = 1 + 8 = 9 \text{ frames}$$

> **Logic:** The window must be at least 9 frames to ensure the sender finishes transmitting before the first ACK returns, eliminating idle time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In part (b)(i), students often forget to convert kbps to bps and write 4 instead of 4000. Also, the unit "ms" must be converted to "s" before dividing. Losing 1 mark for unit errors is very common.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]

**Question:** (a) Describe the **HDLC Protocol** with its frame format. Explain the three types of HDLC frames. (7 Marks)

**(b) Explain the **Selective Repeat ARQ** protocol. How does it differ from Go-Back-N ARQ in terms of sequence number requirements? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

**HDLC Protocol – Overview:**
**High-Level Data Link Control (HDLC)** is a bit-oriented data link protocol standardized by ISO. It supports both point-to-point and multipoint configurations and provides reliable, in-order frame delivery.

**HDLC Frame Format:**

| Flag (8 bits) | Address (8 bits) | Control (8 bits) | Information (Variable) | FCS (16/32 bits) | Flag (8 bits) |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|------------------------|------------------|---------------|

- **Flag:** `01111110`, marks frame boundaries.
- **Address:** Identifies the secondary station (1 byte in basic mode, 2 bytes extended).
- **Control:** Identifies frame type and contains sequence numbers/control bits.
- **Information:** The actual user data (absent in S-frames).
- **FCS:** Frame Check Sequence using CRC for error detection.

**Three Types of HDLC Frames:**

1. **I-Frames (Information Frames):** Carry user data. The control field has two sequence numbers — `N(S)` (send sequence number) and `N(R)` (receive sequence number, used for piggybacked ACK). Used for data transfer.

2. **S-Frames (Supervisory Frames):** Used for flow and error control. **Do not carry user data.** Four subtypes:
   - **RR (Receive Ready):** Positive ACK.
   - **RNR (Receive Not Ready):** Flow control, receiver is busy.
   - **REJ (Reject):** NAK for Go-Back-N style error recovery.
   - **SREJ (Selective Reject):** NAK for Selective Repeat style recovery.

3. **U-Frames (Unnumbered Frames):** Used for link establishment, disconnection, and mode setting. May carry data in some cases. Examples: SABM, DISC, UA, FRMR.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Selective Repeat ARQ:**
In **Selective Repeat ARQ**, the sender can transmit up to $N$ frames within its window. The receiver **accepts frames in any order** (within the window) and **buffers** out-of-order frames. When the sender is notified (via a NAK) of a missing or erroneous frame, it retransmits **only that specific frame**, not the entire window.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Sender transmits frames 0 through $N-1$ in a window.
2. Receiver accepts and buffers all correctly received frames, even if out of order.
3. If a frame is missing, the receiver sends a **NAK** for that specific frame.
4. Sender retransmits only the NAKed frame.
5. The window slides forward as cumulative or selective ACKs arrive.

**Sequence Number Requirement:**
- For Selective Repeat, the **maximum window size is $N = 2^{k-1}$**, where $k$ is the number of sequence number bits.
- For example, with $k = 3$ bits (sequence numbers 0 to 7), the maximum window size is $N = 2^{3-1} = 4$ frames.
- This is **half** the sequence number space to avoid ambiguity between new frames and old retransmissions.

**Comparison with Go-Back-N:**

| Parameter | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Sequence number range | $2^k$ | $2^k$ |
| Maximum window size | $2^k - 1$ | $2^{k-1}$ |
| Receiver buffer | 1 frame | $N$ frames |
| Wasted bandwidth on error | Up to $N$ frames | 1 frame |
| Implementation complexity | Lower | Higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is to write that Selective Repeat uses $2^k - 1$ window size. The correct formula is $2^{k-1}$. The reason is that the receiver must distinguish between an old retransmitted frame and a new frame in the next cycle, and with a full $2^k$ window, this distinction becomes impossible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam – Module 2**

### Flow Control
- Stop-and-Wait: One frame at a time, simple but inefficient for high $a$.
- Sliding Window: Multiple frames, high efficiency.

### Parameter $a$ and Times
- $T_t = L / R$, $T_p = d / v$, $a = T_p / T_t = (R \cdot d) / (v \cdot L)$.
- $\text{BDP} = R \cdot T_p$ (in bits).

### Stop-and-Wait ARQ
- Sequence bits: 1 (sequence numbers 0 and 1).
- Efficiency: $\eta = 1 / (1 + 2a)$.
- Threshold: If $a \geq 0.5$, efficiency falls below 50%.

### Go-Back-N ARQ
- Window size: $N = 2^k - 1$.
- Sequence bits: $k$ (e.g., $k=3$ → $N=7$).
- Efficiency: $\eta = N / (1 + 2a)$ if $N < 1 + 2a$, else $\eta = 1$.
- Receiver discards out-of-order frames.

### Selective Repeat ARQ
- Window size: $N = 2^{k-1}$.
- Sequence bits: $k$ (e.g., $k=3$ → $N=4$).
- Receiver buffers out-of-order frames.
- Most bandwidth-efficient but complex.

### HDLC
- Bit-oriented protocol.
- Frame: Flag, Address, Control, Information, FCS, Flag.
- Bit stuffing: After 5 ones, insert a 0.
- Three frame types: I, S, U.

### PPP
- Byte-oriented protocol.
- Frame: Flag, Address (0xFF), Control (0x03), Protocol, Information, FCS, Flag.
- Byte stuffing using escape byte 0x7D.

### Common Mistake Zones
- Confusing $T_t$ with $T_p$.
- Forgetting to convert ms to s or kbps to bps.
- Writing $N = 2^k$ for GBN (correct is $2^k - 1$).
- Writing $N = 2^{k-1}$ for GBN (this is for Selective Repeat).
- Using $\vert$ inside markdown tables (use $\mid$ or $\vert$).
- Confusing bit stuffing (HDLC) with byte stuffing (PPP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
