```mermaid
    L6[Routing Engine OSPF BGP RIP Daemon]
    L5[Forwarding Engine Line Cards Hardware ASIC]
    L4[Driver Layer Device Drivers]
    L3[Kernel IOS JunOS or OpenWRT]
    L2[Bootloader and Configuration]
    L1[Physical Hardware CPU Memory Flash NVRAM]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L3 --> L6 --> L7
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
*Define a router. Differentiate between forwarding and routing.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A router is a Layer-3 device that connects different IP networks and forwards packets based on destination IP address using a routing table. **(1 mark)**
**Forwarding** is the local per-packet process of moving a packet from input port to output port using the routing table; it occurs in hardware at high speed. **(1 mark)**
**Routing** is the network-wide process of building and maintaining the routing table using routing protocols (OSPF, BGP); it occurs in software on the routing processor. **(1 mark)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the longest prefix match (LPM) in IP routing? Why is it essential in CIDR networks?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
LPM is the algorithm used by routers to select the routing table entry with the **longest matching subnet mask** (most specific prefix) when multiple entries match a destination IP. **(2 marks)**
In **CIDR networks**, addresses are aggregated using variable-length prefixes. LPM ensures that the most specific route is always chosen, enabling efficient use of the IPv4 address space and hierarchical routing. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **internal architecture of a router** with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of each component in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **packet switching** in a router. Discuss the three switching fabric techniques with their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Router Architecture (7 marks):**

A modern router consists of **four major components**:

**1. Input Ports (2 marks):**
- Terminate the physical link and perform bit-level reception.
- Perform data-link layer decapsulation.
- Execute the **lookup and forwarding** function using the forwarding table (FIB).
- Implement **Longest Prefix Match** using high-speed TCAM memory.
- Store the packet in the input port's queue awaiting switching.

**2. Switching Fabric (2 marks):**
- The central component connecting input and output ports.
- Three implementations: memory-based, bus-based, and crossbar-based.
- Determines the **aggregate throughput** of the router.

**3. Output Ports (2 marks):**
- Receive packets from the switching fabric.
- Buffer packets in the **output queue** (transmit buffer).
- Perform **scheduling** (FIFO, Priority, Weighted Fair Queuing) to decide transmission order.
- Perform data-link encapsulation and physical-layer transmission.

**4. Routing Processor (1 mark):**
- Runs routing protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP).
- Maintains the **routing table** and installs forwarding entries in the FIB.
- Handles management functions (CLI, SNMP, logging).

*(Include the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Packet Switching in Routers (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Packet switching is the technique where data is broken into independent packets, each carrying a destination address, and forwarded hop-by-hop through routers.

**Three Switching Fabric Techniques:**

**1. Switching via Memory (1.5 marks):**
- Original approach used in first-generation routers.
- Input port copies the packet into processor memory; routing processor extracts destination address, performs lookup, and copies packet to output port buffer.
- **Drawback:** Single shared memory bus → limited throughput (bus contention).
- Used in low-end software routers.

**2. Switching via Bus (1.5 marks):**
- Input port transmits the packet directly to the output port via a **shared bus**.
- Output port recognizes its own label and accepts the packet.
- **Drawback:** Bus bandwidth is the bottleneck; only one packet can traverse the bus at a time.
- **Advantage:** Simpler than crossbar; suitable for moderate-speed routers.

**3. Switching via Interconnection Network (Crossbar) (2.5 marks):**
- Uses a **$N \times N$ crossbar switch** connecting $N$ input ports to $N$ output ports.
- Multiple packets can be switched **simultaneously** as long as they target different output ports.
- Non-blocking architecture; scales to terabits per second.
- Used in **modern high-performance core routers** (Cisco CRS, Juniper PTX).

**Performance Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Technique | Throughput | Latency | Cost |
|-----------|------------|---------|------|
| Memory | Low | High | Low |
| Bus | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Crossbar | High | Low | High |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]**

**(a)** Explain the **IPv4 datagram header format** with a neat diagram. List the key fields and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Network Address Translation (NAT)**. Explain its working with a suitable diagram. What are its advantages and limitations? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IPv4 Header Format (7 marks):**

A standard IPv4 header is **20 bytes (160 bits)** minimum, expandable to 60 bytes with options.

**Field-wise Description:**

| Bit Position | Field | Size (bits) | Function |
|--------------|-------|-------------|----------|
| 0 | Version (VER) | 4 | Always **4** for IPv4 |
| 4 | Internet Header Length (IHL) | 4 | Header length in 32-bit words (min 5, max 15) |
| 8 | Type of Service (TOS / DSCP+ECN) | 8 | QoS priority and congestion signaling |
| 16 | Total Length | 16 | Entire datagram size in bytes (max 65,535) |
| 32 | Identification | 16 | Used for **fragmentation** and reassembly |
| 48 | Flags (DF, MF) | 3 | Don't Fragment, More Fragments bits |
| 51 | Fragment Offset | 13 | Position of fragment in original datagram |
| 64 | Time To Live (TTL) | 8 | Decremented at each hop; packet dropped at 0 |
| 72 | Protocol | 8 | Next-layer protocol (6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP) |
| 80 | Header Checksum | 16 | 1's complement sum of header words |
| 96 | Source IP Address | 32 | 4-byte sender address |
| 128 | Destination IP Address | 32 | 4-byte final destination |
| 160 | Options (optional) | 0–320 | Rarely used (Loose/Strict Source Routing, Record Route, Timestamp) |
| Variable | Padding | Variable | Ensures 32-bit alignment |
| — | Data Payload | Variable | Upper-layer PDU (TCP segment, UDP datagram, etc.) |

**Total maximum datagram size = 65,535 bytes.** Fragmentation is performed by routers when the outgoing link's MTU is smaller than the datagram size.

---

**(b) Network Address Translation (NAT) (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** NAT is a mechanism used by routers (typically edge/access routers) to map **multiple private IP addresses** to **one or more public IP addresses**, conserving the IPv4 address space and providing basic network isolation.

**Working (4 marks):**

1. A host inside the private network (e.g., 192.168.1.10) sends a packet to an external server (e.g., 8.8.8.8).
2. The **NAT-enabled router** receives the packet and modifies:
   - **Source IP** → public IP (e.g., 203.0.113.5) of the WAN interface.
   - **Source Port** → a unique port number from the **NAT translation table**.
3. The router stores the mapping: `(192.168.1.10:5000 ↔ 203.0.113.5:6000)`.
4. When the reply arrives, the router consults the table, reverses the translation, and forwards the packet to the original internal host.

**NAT Translation Table (1 mark):**

| Private IP:Port | Public IP:Port |
|-----------------|----------------|
| 192.168.1.10:5000 | 203.0.113.5:6001 |
| 192.168.1.11:5000 | 203.0.113.5:6002 |
| 192.168.1.12:5000 | 203.0.113.5:6003 |

**Advantages (0.5 marks):**
- Conserves public IPv4 addresses.
- Provides basic security by hiding internal topology.

**Limitations (0.5 marks):**
- Breaks end-to-end principle; **P2P applications** suffer.
- Complicates protocols carrying IP info in payload (IPsec, FTP).
- Single point of failure for outbound connectivity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Router Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "router" with "switch":** A switch is Layer-2 (MAC-based), a router is Layer-3 (IP-based). Examiners **strictly deduct 1–2 marks** if this is mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting to decrement TTL:** When tracing packet flow across routers, students often forget TTL is decremented by 1 at each hop. This costs **1 mark** in numerical/tracing questions.
> 3. **Not mentioning "Longest Prefix Match":** For any routing table question, simply saying "the router looks up the IP" is incomplete. Always state **LPM** explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing transmission delay with propagation delay:** $d_{trans} = L/R$ depends on packet size and bandwidth; $d_{prop} = d/s$ depends on distance and medium. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** Even a hand-drawn block diagram of router architecture is worth **2 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Routers**

- **Router = Layer-3 device** connecting distinct IP networks; uses **IP addresses** for forwarding decisions.
- **Two-plane architecture:** Control Plane (routing, software) vs Data Plane (forwarding, hardware).
- **Four components:** Input Ports → Switching Fabric → Output Ports → Routing Processor.
- **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** is the core forwarding algorithm in CIDR networks.
- **TTL (Time To Live):** Maximum 255; decremented at every router; packet dropped at TTL=0 (prevents infinite loops).
- **IPv4 Header:** 20 bytes minimum, 60 bytes maximum. Key fields: Version, IHL, TTL, Protocol, Source/Dest IP, Checksum.
- **Nodal Delay Formula:** $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$.
- **Transmission Delay:** $d_{trans} = L/R$ (function of packet size and bandwidth).
- **Propagation Delay:** $d_{prop} = d/s$ (function of distance and medium speed).
- **Traffic Intensity $I = La/R$:** If $I > 1$, queue grows unbounded → packet loss.
- **Three switching fabric types:** Memory (slow, cheap), Bus (moderate), Crossbar (fast, expensive — used in core routers).
- **Router types:** Core, Edge, Distribution, Access, Wireless.
- **NAT** maps private → public IPs; breaks end-to-end principle; conserves IPv4.
- **Output port scheduling algorithms:** FIFO, Priority Queuing, Round Robin, Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ).
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $B = R \times d_{prop}$: determines minimum buffer size.
- **CIDR notation:** $a.b.c.d/n$ where $n$ = number of network bits; $32-n$ = host bits.
- **Classful addressing** is obsolete; KTU 2024 exam focuses on **CIDR + VLSM + Subnetting**.
- **FIB** (Forwarding Information Base) is consulted by input ports; **RIB** (Routing Information Base) is maintained by routing processor.
- **Routing vs Forwarding:** Routing = building the map; Forwarding = following the map per packet.
- **Common KTU exam topics:** Architecture diagram, IPv4 header, LPM examples, delay calculations, NAT working, switch-fabric comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gateways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gateways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Gateway (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *gateway* is a network interconnection device that operates at **all seven layers of the OSI Reference Model** and is used to connect two networks that employ **dissimilar network protocols**. It performs complete **protocol translation/conversion** so that data originating on one network can be understood by a host on a structurally different network.

A gateway is essentially a **"translator"** between heterogeneous networks. Unlike a bridge (Layer 2) or a router (Layer 3), which forward frames or packets with minimal modifications, a gateway must **reconstruct the entire protocol stack** when bridging the gap between, for example, a TCP/IP-based Ethernet LAN and an IBM SNA mainframe network, or between an IPv4 network and an IPv6 network.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Identifier for Exams:** Whenever a question mentions *"communication between two networks with completely different architectures or protocol suites"*, the answer is **Gateway**, not Router or Bridge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The International Airport Customs Counter

Imagine you are flying from **Kerala (India)** to **London (UK)**:

- The **airport immigration/customs counter** is your **Gateway**.
- You cannot simply walk out of one country and into another — a **translator (immigration officer)** verifies your **passport** (header information), checks your **visa category** (protocol type), and validates your **luggage against customs rules** (payload handling).
- After the gateway processes you, you are *re-issued* a valid entry pass for the destination country.
- Similarly, a **gateway in a network** receives a packet from Network A, **strips the original headers, translates addresses and protocol fields, encapsulates the payload in a new protocol stack, and forwards it** to Network B.

> [!TIP]  
> **Memory Trick:** *Gateway = Going-Through-A-Way* → the only way to *go through* between incompatible networks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

| Property | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **OSI Layer Operation** | **All 7 Layers (Application to Physical)** |
| **Primary Function** | **Protocol Translation / Conversion** |
| **Speed Classification** | **Slowest among interconnecting devices** (due to deep processing) |
| **Intelligence Level** | **Most intelligent interconnecting device** |
| **Typical Deployment** | Enterprise–ISP boundary, Enterprise–Mainframe boundary, IoT–Cloud boundary |

---

## 1.4 The "Default Gateway" — A Special Case

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Default Gateway** is the IP address (usually a router interface) to which a host sends all packets destined for **outside its local subnet**. In small/home networks, the default gateway is the LAN-side address of the Wi-Fi router (e.g., `192.168.1.1`).

Although the term "default gateway" historically refers to a *router*, the exam-friendly term in KTU is to clearly distinguish:
- **Gateway (true)** → Protocol translator.
- **Default Gateway (term)** → A *router's IP* acting as exit point for a host.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Gateway Works

A gateway performs **seven-layer protocol conversion** by executing the following structured logic:

1. **Receive Frame (Layer 1–2):** A bitstream arrives on the physical interface and is assembled into a frame (e.g., Ethernet II frame from Network A).
2. **De-encapsulation up to Application Layer (Layer 3–7):** The gateway parses IP header, TCP/UDP header, and finally the application-layer payload (e.g., HTTP, SMTP, FTP).
3. **Protocol Mapping / Translation:** The gateway maps the fields of Protocol Stack A to Protocol Stack B.  
   - Example: An IPv4 packet from an Ethernet LAN is translated into an **IPX/SPX** packet for a legacy Novell NetWare network.
   - Example: An SMTP email arriving from the Internet is translated to **X.400** format for delivery to a government mail server.
4. **Re-encapsulation (Layer 7 → Layer 1):** A new header for Protocol Stack B is prepended at each layer, and the frame is transmitted on Network B's physical medium.
5. **Acknowledgment / Session Handling:** For stateful application gateways (e.g., e-mail gateways), a session may be maintained between the two end-hosts, with the gateway acting as an *application proxy*.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why is a gateway slow?** Because it must process every byte up to Layer 7. It cannot simply forward packets like a router does at Layer 3.

## 2.2 Classification of Gateways (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Type of Gateway | OSI Layer of Operation | Real-World Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Network Gateway** | Layer 3 (Network) | Connects networks with different Network-layer protocols (e.g., IPv4 ↔ IPv6 transition). |
| **Application-Level Gateway (ALG) / Proxy Gateway** | Layer 7 (Application) | Email gateway (SMTP ↔ X.400), HTTP proxy, FTP gateway. Acts as a proxy server. |
| **Transport Gateway** | Layer 4 (Transport) | Translates between TCP and SCTP, or between TCP and a proprietary transport protocol. |
| **Protocol Gateway** | All 7 layers | Full-stack conversion, e.g., Ethernet/TCP/IP ↔ Token Ring/SNA. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]  
> Gateways are mostly **conceptual** in KTU exams. There are no heavy numerical formulas, but these **logical formulas** appear frequently:

| Concept | Logical Expression / Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Default Gateway Selection Rule** | A host sends a packet to its **default gateway** if `dest_IP AND mask` $\neq$ `host_IP AND mask` (i.e., destination is outside the local subnet). |
| **Subnet Match Equation** | `(Destination IP) AND (Subnet Mask) = (Source IP) AND (Subnet Mask)` → *Local delivery*; otherwise → *Send to default gateway*. |
| **Gateway Throughput Approximation** | $T_{gw} \approx \dfrac{P_{size}}{t_{L1} + t_{L2} + t_{L3} + t_{L4} + t_{L5} + t_{L6} + t_{L7} + t_{L1}^{'}}$ where $t_{Li}$ is processing delay at layer $i$ and $t_{L1}^{'}$ is retransmission delay on outgoing link. |
| **Packet Translation Identity** | $P_{out} = f_{translate}(P_{in})$ where $f_{translate}$ is a deterministic, bijective mapping for headers, and identity for payload (most cases). |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Networking:** Connect legacy SNA/IBM mainframes to modern TCP/IP LANs.
- **IoT & Cloud Integration:** Translate between lightweight IoT protocols (MQTT, CoAP) and cloud REST/HTTP APIs.
- **Telecommunications:** SS7 ↔ SIP gateways between traditional PSTN and VoIP networks.
- **Cybersecurity:** Application-level gateways double as **stateful firewalls** (e.g., WAF — Web Application Firewall).
- **Network Migration:** IPv4-to-IPv6 dual-stack gateways ease the transition period.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How a Packet Travels Host A → Gateway → Host B

Let us derive the complete **layer-by-layer protocol translation** that a gateway performs. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

### Given Scenario
- **Host A** is on **Network 1 (Ethernet + IPv4 + TCP + HTTP)**.
- **Host B** is on **Network 2 (Token Ring + IPX + SPX + FTP)**.
- A **Gateway G** sits between the two networks.

### Step 1: Host A Constructs the Outgoing Frame (Network 1 Stack)

$$
\text{Frame}_{A \to G} = \underbrace{H_{HTTP}}_{\text{App}} \mid \underbrace{H_{TCP}}_{\text{Trans}} \mid \underbrace{H_{IPv4}}_{\text{Net}} \mid \underbrace{H_{Eth}}_{\text{DataLink}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Payload}}_{\text{User Data}}
$$

**Logic:** Host A creates a fully encapsulated frame. The IPv4 destination IP is Host B's IP (which is routable via the gateway's Network 1 interface).

### Step 2: Gateway G Receives and De-encapsulates up to Layer 7

$$
\text{Decoded at G} = \{H_{HTTP}, H_{TCP}, H_{IPv4}, H_{Eth}, \text{Payload}\}
$$

The gateway uses its **Data-Link Layer (Eth)** to read the frame, then strips $H_{Eth}$, then strips $H_{IPv4}$, then strips $H_{TCP}$, and finally exposes the HTTP header and payload. This is the **full de-encapsulation** that routers and bridges do *not* perform.

### Step 3: Protocol Translation (The Core Operation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{HTTP}^{A} &\longmapsto H_{FTP}^{B} \quad &\text{(Application-layer translation)} \\
H_{TCP}^{A} &\longmapsto H_{SPX}^{B} \quad &\text{(Transport-layer translation)} \\
H_{IPv4}^{A} &\longmapsto H_{IPX}^{B} \quad &\text{(Network-layer translation)} \\
H_{Eth}^{A} &\longmapsto H_{TokenRing}^{B} \quad &\text{(Data-Link-layer translation)} \\
\text{Payload} &\longmapsto \text{Payload} \quad &\text{(Payload preserved, optionally converted)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each header field is mapped one-to-one. For instance, IPv4's `TTL` field is mapped to IPX's `hop count`; TCP's `port 80` is mapped to FTP's `port 21` if HTTP-to-FTP conversion is required; the Ethernet MAC destination is mapped to the Token Ring MAC destination.

### Step 4: Re-encapsulation on Network 2

$$
\text{Frame}_{G \to B} = \underbrace{H_{FTP}}_{\text{App}} \mid \underbrace{H_{SPX}}_{\text{Trans}} \mid \underbrace{H_{IPX}}_{\text{Net}} \mid \underbrace{H_{TokenRing}}_{\text{DataLink}} \mid \text{Payload}
$$

**Logic:** The gateway now produces a brand-new frame conforming to Network 2's protocol suite and transmits it to Host B.

### Step 5: Verification — Symmetry

If Host B replies, the gateway performs the **reverse translation** $f_{translate}^{-1}$ so that Host A correctly interprets the response.

$$
P_{out} = f_{translate}(P_{in}) \quad \text{and} \quad P_{in}^{reply} = f_{translate}^{-1}(P_{out}^{reply})
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Gateway vs Other Interconnecting Devices

> [!NOTE]  
> This is the **most-asked comparison table** in KTU exams. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Parameter | Repeater | Bridge | Router | **Gateway** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) | **All 7 Layers** |
| **Function** | Regenerates signal | Filters/forwards frames | Routes packets | **Translates protocols** |
| **Networks Connected** | Same | Same (e.g., two Ethernet segments) | Similar (e.g., two IP networks) | **Dissimilar (different protocol stacks)** |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Moderate | **Slowest** |
| **Intelligence** | Lowest | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Collision Domain** | Same | Separate per port | Separate per port | **Separate, plus full session control** |
| **Typical Use** | Cable extension | Segmenting LANs | Inter-networking | **Heterogeneous network interconnects** |

## 3.3 Subnetting Logic: When Does a Host Use Its Default Gateway?

Given a host with IP `192.168.10.25/26`, derive whether the destination `10.0.0.5` requires the default gateway.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Subnet Mask }(/26) &= 255.255.255.192 \\
\text{Host Network} &= (192.168.10.25) \text{ AND } (255.255.255.192) \\
&= 192.168.10.0 \\
\text{Dest Network} &= (10.0.0.5) \text{ AND } (255.255.255.192) \\
&= 10.0.0.0 \\
\text{Since } 192.168.10.0 &\neq 10.0.0.0, \text{ destination is OUTSIDE local subnet.} \\
\therefore \text{ Host uses Default Gateway to forward packet.}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A: Gateway Placement in a Heterogeneous Network

> [!NOTE]  
> The diagram below shows two structurally different networks interconnected by a single Gateway. This is a classic KTU 14-mark illustration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NET1[Network 1 TCP IP Ethernet]
        HA[Host A] -->|HTTP over TCP| SW1[Switch 1]
        HB[Host B] -->|HTTP over TCP| SW1
        SW1 -->|Ethernet Frame| GW[Gateway G]
    end
    subgraph NET2[Network 2 IPX SNA Token Ring]
        GW -->|Token Ring Frame| MA[Mainframe Host]
        HC[Host C] -->|FTP over SNA| MA
    end
    classDef gateway fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef host fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef mainframe fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class GW gateway
    class HA,HB,HC host
    class SW1 switch
    class MA mainframe
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- Hosts A and B (on Network 1) speak **HTTP/TCP/IP/Ethernet**.
- Host C and the Mainframe (on Network 2) speak **FTP/SNA/IPX/Token Ring**.
- The **Gateway G** (highlighted in yellow) is the only device that can translate between these two completely different protocol stacks.

## 4.2 Diagram B: Internal Operational Flow of a Gateway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Physical Signal Layer 1] --> B[Assemble Frame Layer 2]
    B --> C[Parse Network Header Layer 3]
    C --> D[Parse Transport Header Layer 4]
    D --> E[Open Session Layer 5]
    E --> F[Decode Presentation Layer 6]
    F --> G[Decode Application Layer 7]
    G --> H{Protocol Translation Engine}
    H --> I[Map App Header to Target Protocol]
    I --> J[Map Transport Header to Target Protocol]
    J --> K[Map Network Header to Target Protocol]
    K --> L[Map Data Link Header to Target Protocol]
    L --> M[Reassemble Frame in Target Stack]
    M --> N[Transmit Frame on Destination Network]
    N --> O[Update Translation Cache and Logs]
    classDef decision fill:#ffe066,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class H decision
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**
- The gateway climbs up the entire 7-layer stack on the incoming side.
- At the **Protocol Translation Engine**, it performs the one-to-one mapping.
- It then descends back down the stack, generating a fully translated frame.
- Finally, it updates its internal translation cache (to optimize future packets in the same flow).

## 4.3 Diagram C: Default Gateway Decision Process in a Host

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Host has packet to send]) --> Extract[Extract Destination IP and Source IP]
    Extract --> Apply[Apply Subnet Mask AND Operation]
    Apply --> Compare{Dest Network equals Source Network?}
    Compare -->|Yes| Local[ARP and Deliver Locally on LAN]
    Compare -->|No| Default[Send to Default Gateway IP]
    Default --> Resolve[ARP for Default Gateway MAC]
    Resolve --> Forward[Forward Frame to Gateway MAC on Local Subnet]
    Forward --> End([Gateway takes over routing])
    Local --> End
    classDef decision fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class Compare decision
```

**Explanation:** A host uses a simple **AND operation with the subnet mask** to decide whether the destination is local or remote. Remote packets are always handed to the **default gateway**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Define a gateway. How is it different from a router?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **gateway** is a network interconnecting device that operates at **all seven layers of the OSI model** and is used to connect two networks that use **entirely different protocols**. Its primary function is **protocol translation** — converting the entire protocol stack (from application to data-link) of one network to that of another.

**Difference from a router:**

| Aspect | Router | Gateway |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 only | All 7 layers |
| Function | Routes packets between similar IP networks | Translates between dissimilar protocols |
| Speed | Moderate | Slower (full-stack processing) |

A router, for example, forwards an IPv4 packet between two Ethernet networks without changing the payload structure. A gateway, however, would translate an IPv4 packet into an IPX packet when connecting an Ethernet/IP LAN to a Token Ring/IPX network.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark tabular difference + 1 mark example = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**What is a default gateway? Why is it required in every host?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **default gateway** is the **IP address of a router interface** on the local subnet to which a host forwards all packets destined for **networks outside its own subnet**. The default gateway is the **exit point** of the local network.

**Why is it required?**
1. A host has limited routing knowledge — it only knows its own subnet.
2. To reach any IP address outside its subnet, the host must hand over the packet to a device (the default gateway) that has wider routing information.
3. Without a default gateway, communication with external networks (e.g., the Internet) is **impossible**.

**Example:** A laptop with IP `192.168.1.10/24` and default gateway `192.168.1.1` (the Wi-Fi router) uses the gateway to reach `8.8.8.8` (Google DNS).

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark reason + 1 mark example = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> Students frequently write "gateway = router" or "gateway = bridge" in the first sentence and lose **2–3 marks instantly**. Always start your answer with: *"A gateway is a device that operates at all 7 OSI layers and performs protocol translation between heterogeneous networks."*  
> Also, do not confuse **"Default Gateway"** (which is a router's IP) with **"Gateway"** (which is a true protocol translator) — examiners deduct marks for this mix-up.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **types of gateways** with suitable examples. Draw a labeled diagram showing the placement of a gateway between two heterogeneous networks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A host has IP address `172.16.45.12` with subnet mask `255.255.240.0`. Determine whether the host will use its default gateway to send a packet to IP `172.16.50.25`. Justify your answer with step-by-step calculation.

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Types of Gateways [7 Marks]

The various types of gateways are:

**1. Network Gateway (Layer 3) [1 mark]**
- Operates at the Network Layer.
- Connects networks using different network-layer protocols.
- **Example:** IPv4 ↔ IPv6 transition gateway, IP ↔ IPX gateway.

**2. Application-Level Gateway (ALG) / Proxy [2 marks]**
- Operates at the Application Layer (Layer 7).
- Acts as a **proxy server**: it terminates the client's connection, then opens a new connection to the actual server.
- Performs application-specific translation.
- **Examples:**
  - **E-mail Gateway:** Converts between SMTP (Internet) and X.400 (Government/Military mail systems).
  - **HTTP Proxy Gateway:** Mediates between an internal corporate browser and external web servers, often with caching.
  - **FTP Gateway:** Translates FTP commands between IPv4 and IPv6 stacks.

**3. Transport Gateway [1 mark]**
- Operates at Layer 4.
- Translates between transport protocols, e.g., TCP ↔ SCTP.

**4. Protocol Gateway (Full-Stack) [1.5 marks]**
- Operates across all 7 layers.
- Provides complete protocol suite translation.
- **Example:** A gateway between an Ethernet/TCP/IP LAN and a Token Ring/SNA mainframe network.

**5. Default Gateway [1.5 marks]**
- Special case: the IP address of a router that serves as the exit point for a host's traffic destined for external networks.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above) [1 mark]**

#### Part (b) — Subnet Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Host IP: `172.16.45.12`
- Subnet Mask: `255.255.240.0` (i.e., `/20`)
- Destination IP: `172.16.50.25`

**Step 1: Convert to binary and apply AND operation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Host IP} &= 172.16.45.12 \\
\text{In binary} &= 10101100.00010000.00101101.00001100 \\
\text{Subnet Mask} &= 255.255.240.0 \\
\text{In binary} &= 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000 \\
\text{Host AND Mask} &= 10101100.00010000.00100000.00000000 \\
&= 172.16.32.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the same AND operation to the destination IP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dest IP} &= 172.16.50.25 \\
\text{In binary} &= 10101100.00010000.00110010.00011001 \\
\text{AND Mask} &= 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000 \\
\text{Dest AND Mask} &= 10101100.00010000.00110000.00000000 \\
&= 172.16.48.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare the two network addresses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Host Network} &= 172.16.32.0 \\
\text{Dest Network} &= 172.16.48.0 \\
\text{Since } 172.16.32.0 &\neq 172.16.48.0,
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion [1 mark]:** The destination is **outside the host's local subnet**. Therefore, the host **WILL use its default gateway** to forward the packet.

*(1 mark step 1 + 1 mark step 2 + 1 mark step 3 + 2 marks conclusion + 2 marks for clear logic and presentation = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Main Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **step-by-step operation of a gateway** when a packet travels from a TCP/IP Ethernet network to an IPX/SPX Token Ring network.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **gateway, router, bridge, and repeater** in a tabular form with at least **six distinguishing parameters**.

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Gateway Operation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Packet Origination [1 mark]**
Host A on Network 1 creates a packet. The encapsulation is:
`Payload + HTTP header + TCP header + IPv4 header + Ethernet header`

**Step 2: Frame Reception at Gateway [1 mark]**
The gateway receives the Ethernet frame on its Network 1 interface.

**Step 3: De-encapsulation up to Application Layer [2 marks]**
The gateway climbs the entire 7-layer stack:
- Strips Ethernet header.
- Strips IPv4 header.
- Strips TCP header.
- Exposes the HTTP application data.

**Step 4: Protocol Translation [2 marks]**
The translation engine performs the following mappings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{HTTP} &\mapsto H_{FTP} \\
H_{TCP} &\mapsto H_{SPX} \\
H_{IPv4} &\mapsto H_{IPX} \\
H_{Ethernet} &\mapsto H_{TokenRing}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Re-encapsulation and Forwarding [1 mark]**
The gateway produces a new frame:
`Payload + FTP header + SPX header + IPX header + Token Ring header` and transmits it on Network 2 to Host B.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above) [extra clarity]**

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table [7 Marks]

| Parameter | Repeater | Bridge | Router | **Gateway** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 | Layer 2 | Layer 3 | **All 7 Layers** |
| **Function** | Signal regeneration | Frame filtering/forwarding | Packet routing | **Protocol translation** |
| **Network Type** | Same network | Same protocol family | Similar protocols (e.g., IP-IP) | **Dissimilar protocols** |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Moderate | **Slowest** |
| **Intelligence** | None | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Typical Device Example** | Network hub's built-in repeater | Ethernet bridge | Cisco 2900 series router | **SMTP-X.400 mail gateway, IPv4-IPv6 gateway** |

*(1 mark per correct row of comparison = 6 marks + 1 mark for any valid extra distinguishing parameter or conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gateways (Module 3, KTU 2024)**

- **Definition:** A gateway is a network device that operates at **all 7 OSI layers** and **translates protocols** between heterogeneous networks.
- **Core Function:** Protocol translation (re-encapsulation from Layer 7 down to Layer 1).
- **Default Gateway:** The IP address of a **router's interface** used by a host to forward packets destined outside its local subnet. **It is a special use of the term, not a true protocol-translating gateway.**
- **Subnet Decision Rule:** A host uses the default gateway if `(Dest IP) AND (Subnet Mask) ≠ (Source IP) AND (Subnet Mask)`.
- **Types of Gateways:** Network (L3), Transport (L4), Application/Proxy (L7), Protocol (all 7), and Default Gateway.
- **Gateway vs Router:** Router works at Layer 3 between similar networks; gateway works at all 7 layers between **dissimilar** networks.
- **Gateway vs Bridge:** Bridge works at Layer 2; gateway works at all 7 layers.
- **Gateway vs Repeater:** Repeater works at Layer 1 (signal boost only); gateway is the **most intelligent** interconnecting device.
- **Real-world Examples:** SMTP↔X.400 mail gateway, IPv4↔IPv6 NAT64 gateway, SS7↔SIP telecom gateway, HTTP proxy gateway, WAF (Web Application Firewall).
- **Performance Characteristic:** Gateways are the **slowest** interconnecting devices because they process every byte up to the application layer.
- **Examiner's Golden Sentence:** *"A gateway connects two networks with different protocol suites by performing seven-layer protocol conversion."* Memorize this verbatim.

> [!TIP]  
> **Last-Minute Tip:** In KTU ESE answers, always **draw the network diagram first** (1 mark reserved), then **tabulate your comparison** (1 mark reserved). Examiners reward visualization skills.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Layer Protocols - Virtual Circuits and Datagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Layer Protocols - Virtual Circuits and Datagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Network Layer in the OSI/TCP-IP Stack

The **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI reference model is responsible for the **end-to-end delivery of packets** (also called *datagrams* in the Internet Protocol suite) from a source host to a destination host across one or more intermediate packet-switched networks. To accomplish this universal delivery, the layer must define a universal addressing scheme (the logical IP address), determine a viable path through a maze of interconnected routers, and orchestrate the forwarding of packets from input port to output port at every intermediate node.

At KTU 2024 level, three high-level design philosophies dominate Layer 3 forwarding:

- **Virtual Circuit (VC) Switching** — A connection-oriented paradigm where a logical path is first established before data flows.
- **Datagram Packet Switching** — A connectionless paradigm where each packet is routed independently.
- **Source Routing** — A less common hybrid where the sender pre-specifies the entire route.

The first two (VC and Datagram) form the **core comparison point** for the KTU Module 3 syllabus and frequently appear in Part B (14-mark) questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The KTU Module 3 explicitly demands a comparative study of Virtual Circuit and Datagram subnets, their internal mechanics, advantages, disadvantages, and the design trade-offs they impose on the underlying physical and data-link infrastructure.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Virtual Circuit (VC)

A **Virtual Circuit** is a logical, end-to-end communication path that mimics the behaviour of a dedicated physical circuit (such as a traditional telephone line) over a shared, statistically-multiplexed packet-switched network. The phrase *virtual* emphasises that the path is software-defined and uses shared physical links, while *circuit* emphasises that all packets of a session traverse the **same pre-established sequence of routers and links**, mimicking a private wire.

Formally, a VC implementation involves three distinct phases:

1. **Connection Setup Phase** — Source and destination exchange control packets to establish virtual circuit identifiers (**VCIs**) at every hop.
2. **Data Transfer Phase** — Data is transmitted as a stream of packets, each carrying the VCI as a short tag rather than full destination address.
3. **Teardown Phase** — Either side sends a control packet to release the resources held at each switch along the path.

## 1.3 Formal Definition of a Datagram Network

In a **Datagram Network**, each packet (called a *datagram*) is treated as an entirely independent entity. The network imposes **no notion of a connection** between sender and receiver. Every packet carries the **full destination address** in its header, and every intermediate router independently decides the next-hop forwarding for that packet using a local routing table lookup.

> [!NOTE]
> The Internet Protocol (IP) is the canonical example of a datagram service. Each IPv4 packet has a 20-byte minimum header with full 32-bit source and destination addresses — there is no connection state held in routers for ongoing flows.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

### Analogy 1: Train Journey (Virtual Circuit)

Imagine you book a **reserved train ticket** from Thiruvananthapuram Central to New Delhi. Before you board, the booking system has already allocated a specific seat, a specific train, and a specific route (with predetermined stops). All your co-travellers on the same train follow the same path, in the same carriages, in the same order. The ticket (VCI) is a small token that the ticket-checker (router) reads to direct you along the pre-decided route. Even if the train is delayed, the route does not change.

### Analogy 2: Independent Car Drivers (Datagram)

Now imagine instead that each of your family members **drives a separate car** from Thiruvananthapuram to New Delhi. They have no convoy arrangement. Each driver uses Google Maps independently, makes their own decisions at every intersection, and could even take different highways, different rest stops, and arrive in a different order. The only common element is the **destination address** typed into each car's GPS (the destination IP in each datagram header). Some might arrive at noon, others at midnight.

### One-Line Memory Hook

> **VC = Train (single route, all packets travel together).**
> **Datagram = Independent cars (each packet self-routed, no shared path).**

## 1.5 Why This Distinction Matters in Engineering

The choice between VC and Datagram architectures is **not a minor implementation detail** — it dictates:

- The size and complexity of forwarding tables.
- The resilience of the network to link failures.
- The latency characteristics of the first packet versus subsequent packets.
- Whether Quality of Service (QoS) guarantees can be enforced.
- The level of state management required inside router hardware (critical for ASIC design and TCAM sizing).

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 Board Exam, examiners expect students to clearly state that **ATM, Frame Relay, and X.25** use Virtual Circuits, whereas the **Internet (IPv4/IPv6) and Ethernet IP-routed networks** use Datagrams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Mechanics of a Virtual Circuit Network

A Virtual Circuit switch maintains a **VC Table (VCI Table)**. Each entry maps a tuple `(Incoming Interface, Incoming VCI)` to an `(Outgoing Interface, Outgoing VCI)`. The VCI is a small integer (typically 8 to 16 bits), which is far more efficient to look up than a 32-bit IPv4 address.

### Operational Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Connection Request (Setup)**
The source sends a `SETUP` (or `CALL_REQUEST`) control packet to the destination, addressed with the full destination address. Each intermediate router:
- Receives the request on an input port.
- Chooses a free outgoing VCI on a chosen next hop.
- Creates a new entry in its VC table mapping `(in_port, in_vci) → (out_port, out_vci)`.
- Forwards the request.

**Step 2 — Connection Confirm (Accept)**
The destination returns an `ACCEPT` packet, and each router again installs reverse-direction VCI mappings.

**Step 3 — Data Transfer**
Data packets now carry only the small VCI, not the full address. At every hop, the switch reads the VCI, looks up the VC table, and forwards in hardware — extremely fast, often a single clock-cycle lookup in a TCAM.

**Step 4 — Teardown**
A `CLEAR` or `RELEASE` packet propagates back, and each switch deletes the relevant VC table entries.

### Engineering Insight: Why Use Small VCIs?

A 32-bit IPv4 lookup requires content-addressable memory with millions of entries. A 12-bit VCI lookup needs only 4096 possible tag values and can be done in a single SRAM read. This is the historical reason ATM and Frame Relay were so popular in high-speed core networks of the 1990s before optical backbones shifted the economics.

## 2.2 Internal Mechanics of a Datagram Network

A Datagram router maintains a **Routing Table (Forwarding Table)**. Each entry maps a **destination IP prefix** to a **next-hop IP and outgoing interface**. Unlike VC, **no per-flow state** is stored — only destination-based state.

### Operational Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Packet Arrival**
A datagram arrives at an input port with a full 32-bit (or 128-bit for IPv6) destination address in the header.

**Step 2 — Longest Prefix Match (LPM)**
The router scans its forwarding table for the **longest matching prefix** (e.g., 128.10.0.0/16 matches more specifically than 128.0.0.0/8) and selects the corresponding next-hop.

**Step 3 — Decrement TTL, Recompute Checksum**
The **Time-To-Live (TTL)** field (8 bits) is decremented by 1. If TTL reaches 0, the packet is dropped and an ICMP "Time Exceeded" message is sent back to the source. The header checksum is recomputed because TTL changed.

**Step 4 — Forwarding**
The packet is placed in the output queue of the chosen outgoing interface, potentially after queuing/scheduling decisions.

**Step 5 — Independent Routing Per Packet**
There is no "next packet" memory. Two consecutive packets from the same flow may take different paths if the network topology or routing tables change between them.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises the essential quantitative relationships a student must memorise for KTU 2024 examination questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IPv4 Header Size | $H_{IPv4} = 20 \text{ bytes (min)} + \text{Options}$ | Min **20 bytes**, max **60 bytes** (4-bit IHL × 4) |
| 2 | Total IPv4 Datagram Size | $\text{Total Length} = \text{Header} + \text{Data}$ | Max **65,535 bytes** (16-bit field) |
| 3 | TTL Decrement | $TTL_{out} = TTL_{in} - 1$ | Min **0**, packet dropped, ICMP sent |
| 4 | Hop Limit (IPv6) | $HL_{out} = HL_{in} - 1$ | Min **0** |
| 5 | Fragment Offset | $Offset_{i} = i \times \text{MTU}_{payload}$ | $i$ = fragment index, 13-bit field |
| 6 | VCI Lookup Cost | $C_{VCI} = O(1)$ TCAM read | Constant time, **1 clock cycle** typically |
| 7 | Datagram Forwarding Cost | $C_{LPM} = O(\log N)$ to $O(N)$ | $N$ = number of routing table prefixes |
| 8 | Header Overhead Ratio (VC) | $\eta_{VC} = \dfrac{L_{VCI}}{L_{VCI} + L_{data}}$ | $L_{VCI}$ typically 2–4 bytes |
| 9 | Header Overhead Ratio (Datagram) | $\eta_{DG} = \dfrac{L_{header}}{L_{header} + L_{data}}$ | $L_{header}$ typically 20 bytes |
| 10 | End-to-End Delay (VC) | $T_{VC} = T_{setup} + (N_{hops} \times t_{per\_hop}) + T_{teardown}$ | $T_{setup}$ = RTT typically |
| 11 | End-to-End Delay (Datagram first pkt) | $T_{DG,1} = N_{hops} \times t_{per\_hop} + T_{lookup}$ | No setup delay |
| 12 | Connectionless Forwarding State | $S_{DG} = O(P)$ | $P$ = number of prefixes |
| 13 | Connection-Oriented Forwarding State | $S_{VC} = O(F \times H)$ | $F$ = active flows, $H$ = avg hops |
| 14 | Maximum IPv4 Address Space | $A_{v4} = 2^{32}$ | $\approx 4.29 \times 10^9$ addresses |
| 15 | Maximum IPv6 Address Space | $A_{v6} = 2^{128}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for the KTU 2024 Examiner's Perspective:** Of the 15 rows above, rows 3, 4, 7, 10, 11, 12, and 13 are the most frequently tested in numerical Part A (3-mark) sub-questions. Always show the substitution of numerical values explicitly in the answer sheet.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The VC/Datagram dichotomy is not just academic — it has shaped the **entire history of carrier-grade networking**:

- **X.25 (1970s)** — Pure VC, used over unreliable analog leased lines. Heavy error recovery at every hop.
- **Frame Relay (1990s)** — Simplified VC with no hop-by-hop error recovery, trusting fibre to be reliable.
- **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode, 1990s)** — Cell-based VC with 53-byte cells. Dominated WAN backbones before IP.
- **MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching, 2000s–present)** — A modern revival of the VC concept using short 20-bit labels. MPLS is widely used today for traffic engineering and VPN services, often described as "connection-oriented IP".
- **Internet (IPv4/IPv6, 1980s–present)** — Pure datagram. The global Internet is the most successful example of connectionless design.

> [!NOTE]
> **MPLS Note for KTU 2024:** Even though the global Internet uses datagrams at Layer 3, the underlying carrier networks often tunnel datagrams through MPLS virtual circuits. This is the most relevant industrial example of the VC concept in modern networking — definitely worth mentioning in any 14-mark answer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Context and Numerical Demonstration

To anchor the conceptual discussion, this section works through three numerical scenarios that are typical of KTU 2024 Module 3 questions. Each scenario demonstrates a different quantitative aspect of the VC-vs-Datagram comparison.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example 1: TTL Decrement and Maximum Hop Count

### Problem Statement
A KTU lab generates an IPv4 datagram with an initial **TTL = 64**. The packet traverses 10 routers, encounters a transient routing loop that causes it to loop through 3 additional routers, and is then successfully delivered.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the starting TTL value from the problem statement.**

$$
TTL_{initial} = 64 \text{ hops}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the TTL decrement rule at every router hop. Each router decreases TTL by 1.**

$$
TTL_{decrement} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{routers}} 1 = N_{routers}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the actual number of routers traversed in this scenario.**

$$
N_{routers} = 10 + 3 = 13
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the remaining TTL at the destination.**

$$
TTL_{final} = TTL_{initial} - TTL_{decrement} = 64 - 13
$$

$$
TTL_{final} = 51 \text{ hops}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check against the boundary condition that TTL must remain non-negative for delivery.**

$$
TTL_{final} = 51 \geq 0 \quad \text{(Valid, packet delivered)}
$$

If the loop had added more than 64 extra hops, the TTL would have reached 0 and the packet would have been dropped with an **ICMP Type 11 — Time Exceeded** message returned to the source.

### Result Interpretation

This demonstrates the **self-stabilising property** of IP datagrams. Even if routing tables misconfigure a temporary loop, the TTL field guarantees that no packet can circulate forever — a property Virtual Circuit networks also rely on as a backstop, but it is essential in datagrams because there is no setup-phase admission control.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 2: Header Overhead Comparison (VC vs Datagram)

### Problem Statement
A KTU simulation sends a file of $L_{file} = 10{,}240 \text{ bytes}$ across a network.

- **Scenario A** uses a Datagram network (IPv4) with a 20-byte IP header.
- **Scenario B** uses a Virtual Circuit network with a 4-byte VCI header per cell.
- The MTU in Scenario B is 48 bytes of payload per cell.

Compute the header overhead ratio $\eta$ and the total bytes on the wire for both scenarios.

### Step-by-Step Solution — Scenario A (Datagram)

**Step 1 — Write the header overhead ratio formula for the datagram case.**

$$
\eta_{DG} = \dfrac{L_{header}}{L_{header} + L_{payload}}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the datagram fits in a single packet (no fragmentation). Then $L_{payload} = L_{file} = 10{,}240$ bytes.**

$$
\eta_{DG} = \dfrac{20}{20 + 10{,}240} = \dfrac{20}{10{,}260}
$$

$$
\eta_{DG} \approx 0.001949 = 1.95\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total bytes on the wire.**

$$
B_{DG} = L_{header} + L_{payload} = 20 + 10{,}240 = 10{,}260 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step-by-Step Solution — Scenario B (Virtual Circuit)

**Step 1 — Compute the number of cells required to carry the 10,240-byte payload.**

$$
N_{cells} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{file}}{MTU_{payload}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{10{,}240}{48} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the ceiling division. Compute the raw division first.**

$$
\dfrac{10{,}240}{48} = 213.333\ldots
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ceiling to get the integer number of cells.**

$$
N_{cells} = 214 \text{ cells}
$$

**Step 4 — The last cell carries only partial data. Calculate the actual data bytes in the last cell.**

$$
L_{last} = 10{,}240 - (213 \times 48) = 10{,}240 - 10{,}224 = 16 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total bytes on the wire (VCI header is added to every cell).**

$$
B_{VC} = N_{cells} \times (L_{VCI} + MTU_{payload}) - (MTU_{payload} - L_{last})
$$

The term $(MTU_{payload} - L_{last})$ corrects for the unused padding bytes in the final cell that the link layer still must transmit.

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
B_{VC} = 214 \times (4 + 48) - (48 - 16) = 214 \times 52 - 32
$$

$$
B_{VC} = 11{,}128 - 32 = 11{,}096 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the VC header overhead ratio for analytical comparison.**

$$
\eta_{VC} = \dfrac{N_{cells} \times L_{VCI}}{B_{VC}} = \dfrac{214 \times 4}{11{,}096} = \dfrac{856}{11{,}096}
$$

$$
\eta_{VC} \approx 0.0771 = 7.71\%
$$

### Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Datagram (Scenario A) | Virtual Circuit (Scenario B) | Better Performer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total bytes on wire | 10,260 B | 11,096 B | **Datagram** |
| Header overhead ratio | **1.95%** | 7.71% | **Datagram** |
| Number of forwarding units | 1 packet | 214 cells | **Datagram** |
| Per-hop lookup cost | 32-bit LPM | 12-bit VCI | **VC** |
| Per-flow state in switches | 0 entries | 1 entry per flow | **Datagram** |
| QoS enforcement ease | Difficult | **Easy** (per-VCI queueing) | **VC** |
| Resilience to link failure | **High** (reroute dynamically) | Low (re-establish VC) | **Datagram** |

### Engineering Insight

The matrix above makes the central trade-off of the topic **explicit**: datagrams trade higher per-packet processing cost for **no per-flow state** and **superior resilience**, while virtual circuits trade scalability for **superior speed and QoS**. This is the answer students must articulate clearly in any 14-mark comparison question.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example 3: End-to-End Delay for First Packet (VC vs Datagram)

### Problem Statement
A KTU 2024 Board Exam style numerical problem: A 5-hop path is to be established between source and destination.

- Per-hop processing delay $t_{proc} = 1 \text{ ms}$.
- Per-hop transmission delay $t_{tx} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$.
- One-way propagation delay per hop $t_{prop} = 2 \text{ ms}$.
- VC setup message experiences the same per-hop delay, and the `SETUP` must travel 5 hops one way and the `ACCEPT` must travel 5 hops back.
- Assume datagram lookup cost is included in $t_{proc}$.

Compute the **time to deliver the first data byte** in both architectures.

### Step-by-Step Solution — Virtual Circuit

**Step 1 — Compute the one-way per-hop total delay.**

$$
t_{hop} = t_{proc} + t_{tx} + t_{prop} = 1 + 0.5 + 2 = 3.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time for the SETUP control packet to reach the destination (5 hops one way).**

$$
T_{setup\_forward} = N_{hops} \times t_{hop} = 5 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time for the ACCEPT control packet to return to the source (5 hops one way).**

$$
T_{setup\_return} = N_{hops} \times t_{hop} = 5 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total setup delay for the VC.**

$$
T_{setup} = T_{setup\_forward} + T_{setup\_return} = 17.5 + 17.5 = 35 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the time for the first data packet to traverse 5 hops (no teardown yet).**

$$
T_{first\_data} = N_{hops} \times t_{hop} = 5 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Total time to first data byte in VC.**

$$
T_{VC,first} = T_{setup} + T_{first\_data} = 35 + 17.5 = 52.5 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step-by-Step Solution — Datagram

**Step 1 — In a datagram network, there is no setup. The first packet can be sent immediately.**

$$
T_{setup, DG} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — The first data packet traverses 5 hops.**

$$
T_{DG,first} = N_{hops} \times t_{hop} = 5 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \text{ ms}
$$

### Comparative Result

| Architecture | Time to First Byte |
|---|---|
| **Virtual Circuit** | $52.5 \text{ ms}$ |
| **Datagram** | $17.5 \text{ ms}$ |
| **Difference (VC penalty)** | $35 \text{ ms}$ (the full setup RTT) |

### Engineering Insight

The 35 ms penalty is precisely why short-lived transactions (a single DNS query, a single HTTP request) favour datagrams. The penalty is amortised away only for long-lived sessions (e.g., a video stream or a 1-hour VoIP call) where hundreds of packets amortise the setup cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** A common error in KTU answer sheets is forgetting to count the **return path** of the `ACCEPT` packet. Both the forward `SETUP` and the reverse `ACCEPT` traverse 5 hops each. The examiner specifically watches for the phrase *"RTT of 5 hops"* to ensure students have accounted for both directions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary of Trade-offs

| Dimension | Virtual Circuit Wins When... | Datagram Wins When... |
|---|---|---|
| Setup delay | Tolerable (long sessions) | Intolerable (short bursts) |
| Router state | Few flows, many packets/flow | Many flows, few packets/flow |
| Header efficiency | $L_{VCI} \ll L_{IP}$ | $L_{IP} \ll L_{VCI}$ per cell |
| Path resilience | Topology is stable | Topology changes (mobile/ad-hoc) |
| QoS / traffic engineering | Required (per-VCI queue) | Best-effort only |
| Hardware speed | ASIC-based label switching | General-purpose CPU forwarding |
| Failure recovery | Slow (re-establish VC) | Fast (reroute per packet) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The following diagrams follow the strict node-identifier alpha rule: every node ID begins with a letter and is purely alphanumeric. No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names. All node labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables.

---

## 4.2 Diagram A: Virtual Circuit Setup, Data Transfer, and Teardown Phases

This block-level functional architecture flow shows the three phases of a virtual circuit session as discrete state transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> nodeA_IDLE
    nodeA_IDLE : NODE A IDLE NO VCI ALLOCATED
    nodeB_R1 : ROUTER R1 RECEIVES SETUP ALLOCATES VCI 17
    nodeC_R2 : ROUTER R2 RECEIVES SETUP ALLOCATES VCI 42
    nodeD_R3 : ROUTER R3 RECEIVES SETUP ALLOCATES VCI 99
    nodeE_DEST : DESTINATION RECEIVES SETUP REPLIES ACCEPT
    nodeF_ACTIVE : VCI ACTIVE BIDIRECTIONAL DATA FLOW
    nodeG_CLEAR : CLEAR PACKET PROPAGATES BACKWARDS
    nodeH_RELEASED : VCI ENTRIES DELETED AT ALL HOPS
    nodeA_IDLE --> nodeB_R1 : SOURCE SENDS SETUP PACKET
    nodeB_R1 --> nodeC_R2 : FORWARD WITH VCI 17
    nodeC_R2 --> nodeD_R3 : FORWARD WITH VCI 42
    nodeD_R3 --> nodeE_DEST : FORWARD WITH VCI 99
    nodeE_DEST --> nodeF_ACTIVE : ACCEPT BACK INSTALLS REVERSE VCIS
    nodeF_ACTIVE --> nodeG_CLEAR : SESSION ENDS CLEAR SENT
    nodeG_CLEAR --> nodeH_RELEASED : ALL SWITCHES FREE VCI SLOTS
    nodeH_RELEASED --> nodeA_IDLE : READY FOR NEW SESSION
```

### Architectural Interpretation

- The state machine clearly separates the three canonical phases: **IDLE → SETUP → ACTIVE → TEARDOWN → IDLE**.
- The intermediate nodes `nodeB_R1`, `nodeC_R2`, `nodeD_R3` each represent a **per-switch local action** (VCI allocation and table update).
- This diagram directly supports a 14-mark answer that requires the student to *describe the operation of a virtual circuit network*.

---

## 4.3 Diagram B: Datagram Packet Switching — Independent Routing

This diagram shows the same source and destination but with three independent packets choosing different paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[SOURCE HOST A] --> r1A[ROUTER R1]
    srcA --> r1A
    r1A --> r2A[ROUTER R2]
    r1A --> r3A[ROUTER R3]
    r2A --> r4A[ROUTER R4]
    r3A --> r4A
    r3A --> r5A[ROUTER R5]
    r4A --> dstA[DESTINATION HOST B]
    r5A --> dstA
    p1A["PACKET 1 PATH R1 R2 R4"] -.-> r1A
    p2A["PACKET 2 PATH R1 R3 R4"] -.-> r1A
    p3A["PACKET 3 PATH R1 R3 R5"] -.-> r1A
```

### Architectural Interpretation

- The **dotted arrows** labelled `PACKET 1 PATH`, `PACKET 2 PATH`, `PACKET 3 PATH` emphasise that each packet's path is **decided independently** at each router using only the destination IP.
- Notice that `ROUTER R3` is part of two different paths, demonstrating that **datagrams do not reserve capacity** — multiple flows multiplex into the same router dynamically.
- The three solid arrows exiting `ROUTER R1` (to `R2` and `R3`) represent the **forwarding decision space** at R1 — the longest-prefix match picks one of them per packet.

---

## 4.4 Diagram C: Side-by-Side Architectural Comparison Block Diagram

This block-level diagram maps the contrasting decision points and state locations in both architectures.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph vcA["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT BLOCK"]
        vcInA["INPUT PACKET WITH VCI"] --> vcLookupA["VCI TABLE LOOKUP EXACT MATCH"]
        vcLookupA --> vcForwardA["FORWARD ON INTERFACE FROM TABLE"]
        vcStateA["PER FLOW VCI STATE IN SWITCH"] -.-> vcLookupA
    end
    subgraph dgA["DATAGRAM BLOCK"]
        dgInA["INPUT PACKET WITH FULL IP ADDRESS"] --> dgLookupA["ROUTING TABLE LPM LOOKUP"]
        dgLookupA --> dgForwardA["FORWARD ON NEXTHOP INTERFACE"]
        dgStateA["PER PREFIX ROUTING STATE IN ROUTER"] -.-> dgLookupA
    end
```

### Architectural Interpretation

- The dotted arrows (`vcStateA`, `dgStateA`) point into the lookup blocks to visually represent that **state is consulted during forwarding**, not created per packet.
- The **VC block** uses an exact-match VCI lookup (fast, hardware-friendly).
- The **Datagram block** uses a longest-prefix-match (LPM) lookup (more complex, supports CIDR aggregation).
- This block diagram is the ideal visual to include in a 14-mark comparison answer to **earn the full 7 marks allocated to the architecture description**.

---

## 4.5 Diagram D: VC Table vs Routing Table — Functional Mapping

This diagram shows the structure of the two table types that drive forwarding in each architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vcTable["VC TABLE AT ROUTER R2"]
        vcT1["ROW 1 IN PORT 0 VCI 12 OUT PORT 1 VCI 47"]
        vcT2["ROW 2 IN PORT 0 VCI 18 OUT PORT 2 VCI 33"]
        vcT3["ROW 3 IN PORT 1 VCI 05 OUT PORT 0 VCI 91"]
    end
    subgraph rtTable["ROUTING TABLE AT ROUTER R2"]
        rtT1["PREFIX 10.0.0.0 SLASH 8 NEXTHOP 192.168.1.1 OUT ETH0"]
        rtT2["PREFIX 172.16.0.0 SLASH 12 NEXTHOP 192.168.1.2 OUT ETH1"]
        rtT3["DEFAULT 0.0.0.0 SLASH 0 NEXTHOP 10.0.0.1 OUT ETH0"]
    end
```

### Architectural Interpretation

- The VC table maps **(interface, VCI) pairs** — a 2-tuple local to one switch. VCIs have no global meaning.
- The routing table maps **destination prefixes** — entries that must be consistent across all routers in the network (computed by routing protocols like OSPF, BGP).
- For the KTU 2024 exam, a student drawing both tables side by side demonstrates clear understanding and is likely to gain **at least 2 bonus marks** under the examiner's discretion.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Virtual Circuit. Mention two real-world protocols that use it. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Circuit (VC)** is a logical connection-oriented path established between a source and destination across a packet-switched network. It involves three phases: **connection setup, data transfer, and teardown**. Each packet carries a small **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** rather than a full destination address, and all packets of a session follow the same sequence of routers.

**Two real-world protocols that use virtual circuits:**

1. **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)** — Used in 1990s WAN backbones; uses 53-byte cells with VPI/VCI labels.
2. **X.25** — One of the earliest packet-switched protocols; uses virtual circuits over unreliable analog lines.

> [!TIP]
> For a 3-mark answer, two real-world examples are mandatory. ATM is the strongest choice because it is most directly associated with VC in the KTU syllabus. Adding **MPLS** as a modern example earns an extra appreciation mark.

---

### Question 2: Why does a datagram network not maintain per-flow state in routers? State two consequences. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A datagram network does **not** maintain per-flow state because each packet is routed **independently** using only the destination address and a destination-prefix routing table. There is no setup phase, and routers do not allocate resources per flow.

**Two consequences:**

1. **Scalability** — The router's state grows only with the number of destination prefixes (typically $O(10^5)$), not with the number of active flows (which can be $O(10^6)$ to $O(10^7)$). This is why the global Internet can scale to billions of endpoints using only datagram IP.
2. **Robustness against failures** — If a link or router fails, datagrams are automatically rerouted via alternative paths in the routing table. There is no need to re-establish a connection. This is in contrast to VC networks, where a single link failure tears down many virtual circuits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Virtual Circuit and Datagram Subnets — Comparative Study `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Explain with neat diagrams the working of a virtual circuit network. How are VCI tables maintained at each switch?**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Compare the virtual circuit and datagram approaches of packet switching. List four advantages and four disadvantages of each.**

---

### Model Answer for Question A, Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define a Virtual Circuit (1 mark).**
A Virtual Circuit is a logical, connection-oriented path established between a source and a destination across a packet-switched network, identified at every hop by a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)**.

**Step 2 — Describe the three phases (2 marks).**

- **Setup phase:** Source sends a `CALL_REQUEST` packet with the full destination address. Each intermediate switch allocates a free VCI on its outgoing interface and creates an entry in its VCI table.
- **Data transfer phase:** Data packets carry only the VCI, not the full address. Each switch performs an exact-match lookup on `(input port, VCI)` and forwards the packet on the prescribed output port with the prescribed outgoing VCI.
- **Teardown phase:** Either side sends a `CLEAR` packet. Each switch deletes its VCI table entry, freeing the slot for future VCs.

**Step 3 — Describe the VCI table maintenance (2 marks).**

A VCI table at switch $S_i$ has rows of the form:
- **Incoming interface**
- **Incoming VCI**
- **Outgoing interface**
- **Outgoing VCI**

When the `CALL_REQUEST` is received, the switch selects a free VCI value on the chosen outgoing interface. The pair `(incoming interface, incoming VCI)` is the lookup key, and `(outgoing interface, outgoing VCI)` is the forwarding action. VCIs are **locally significant** — the VCI 17 at switch R1 has no relation to the VCI 17 at switch R2.

**Step 4 — Provide a neat diagram (2 marks).**

Draw a source-destination path with three intermediate switches (R1, R2, R3). Show the `SETUP` arrow moving forward with VCI allocations at each switch, and the `ACCEPT` arrow moving back with reverse VCIs allocated. Label one VCI table with sample values such as:

- R1: `(Port 0, VCI 12) → (Port 1, VCI 47)`
- R2: `(Port 0, VCI 47) → (Port 2, VCI 33)`
- R3: `(Port 0, VCI 33) → (Port 1, VCI 91)`

---

### Model Answer for Question A, Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Tabular comparison (3 marks).**

| Parameter | Virtual Circuit | Datagram |
|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | Required | Not required |
| Addressing in data packet | Small VCI (2–4 bytes) | Full IP address (4 or 16 bytes) |
| Per-flow router state | Yes (VCI entries) | No |
| Routing decision per packet | No (decided at setup) | Yes (per packet) |
| Path followed by packets | Same path | Can differ per packet |
| Resilience to failure | Low — VC breaks | High — dynamic reroute |
| QoS support | Easy (per-VCI queue) | Difficult |
| Typical examples | ATM, X.25, Frame Relay, MPLS | IP (IPv4, IPv6) |
| Header processing cost | Low (exact match) | High (longest prefix match) |
| End-to-end delay (first packet) | High (setup RTT) | Low (no setup) |
| Suitable traffic pattern | Long-lived sessions | Short bursts, many flows |

**Step 2 — Advantages of VC (2 marks).**

1. Packets arrive in order because all follow the same path — useful for voice/video streaming.
2. QoS and bandwidth reservation are possible because resources are reserved at setup.
3. Router forwarding is a single-clock-cycle VCI lookup — extremely fast in hardware.
4. Network operator can engineer traffic explicitly per VC (the basis of MPLS traffic engineering).

**Step 3 — Advantages of Datagram (2 marks).**

1. No setup delay — first packet arrives as quickly as the network can route.
2. Robust to failures — packets automatically find alternate paths via dynamic routing.
3. Stateless routers scale better — only prefix-based state, not flow-based state.
4. Simpler end-system logic — no need to manage connection state in the application.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering a Part B comparison question, students frequently lose 2–3 marks by **listing only advantages of one approach** and forgetting to list disadvantages of the other. The examiner allocates marks **symmetrically**. Always write four advantages AND four disadvantages of BOTH architectures. A 7-mark sub-part with a one-sided answer can never earn more than 3 marks.

---

### Question B: Datagram Network Operation, IPv4 Header Fields, and Fragmentation `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a datagram network. Discuss the role of the routing table and the Longest Prefix Match (LPM) algorithm.**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Draw the IPv4 header format and explain the function of the following fields: Version, Header Length, Total Length, Time-To-Live, Protocol, and Header Checksum.**

---

### Model Answer for Question B, Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define a datagram network (1 mark).**
A datagram network is a connectionless packet-switched network where each packet (datagram) is routed independently through the network using the destination address in its header and a local forwarding-table lookup at every router.

**Step 2 — Block diagram of datagram operation (2 marks).**

```
[Source Host]
     |
     v
[Router R1] ---> [Routing Table Lookup LPM] ---> [Forward to Next Hop]
     |
     v
[Router R2] ---> [Routing Table Lookup LPM] ---> [Forward to Next Hop]
     |
     v
[Router R3] ---> [Routing Table Lookup LPM] ---> [Forward to Next Hop]
     |
     v
[Destination Host]
```

**Step 3 — Role of the routing table (2 marks).**

The routing table at each router contains entries of the form `(destination prefix, prefix length, next-hop IP, outgoing interface)`. Routes are computed by routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP) and pushed into the forwarding table. When a datagram arrives:

- The router extracts the destination IP from the header.
- It searches the table for the **longest matching prefix** (LPM).
- It forwards the datagram on the corresponding outgoing interface to the corresponding next-hop.

**Step 4 — Longest Prefix Match algorithm (2 marks).**

LPM works as follows:

1. Scan all routing table entries.
2. For each entry, check whether the destination IP begins with the entry's prefix bits.
3. Among all matching entries, select the one with the **longest prefix** (most specific).
4. If no entry matches, use the **default route** (0.0.0.0/0).

**Example:** Destination IP = `192.168.5.42`. Two table entries match: `192.168.0.0/16` and `192.168.5.0/24`. The LPM algorithm picks the `/24` because it is the most specific match. This is why CIDR aggregation works in the global Internet.

---

### Model Answer for Question B, Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Draw the IPv4 header (3 marks).**

A 32-bit wide header with the following field layout (each row = 32 bits):

```
|Version| IHL |Type of Service|        Total Length         |
|        Identification         |Flags| Fragment Offset     |
|  Time to Live   |   Protocol   |     Header Checksum       |
|                  Source IP Address (32 bits)               |
|               Destination IP Address (32 bits)             |
|      Options (0 to 40 bytes, IHL-dependent)                |
```

**Step 2 — Explain each field (1 mark each, total 6 fields = 6 marks; balance marks come from the diagram).**

- **Version (4 bits):** Defines the IP protocol version. For IPv4, this field is set to `0100` (i.e., 4). Allows routers to interpret the rest of the header correctly.
- **Header Length / IHL (4 bits):** Length of the IP header in **32-bit words**. The minimum value is 5 (corresponding to 20 bytes, no options), and the maximum is 15 (60 bytes, with 40 bytes of options).
- **Total Length (16 bits):** Total length of the **entire datagram** in bytes, including header and data. Maximum value is 65,535 bytes.
- **Time-To-Live (8 bits):** Counter that is decremented by 1 at every router hop. When TTL reaches 0, the datagram is dropped and an ICMP "Time Exceeded" message is sent back to the source. Prevents indefinite circulation in case of routing loops.
- **Protocol (8 bits):** Identifies the next-level (transport-layer) protocol encapsulated in the data portion. Common values: 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 1 = ICMP. Tells the destination host which transport-layer entity to hand the data to.
- **Header Checksum (16 bits):** A 1's complement checksum computed over the IP header only (not the data). Because the TTL field changes at every hop, the checksum is **recomputed at every router**. Provides protection against header corruption in transit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:** A frequent error in KTU answer sheets is stating that the IP header checksum covers the **entire datagram including data**. The correct answer is that it covers the **header only**. This single-word error (data vs header) costs 1 full mark, and the examiner explicitly looks for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this section as a **last-night revision checklist** before the KTU 2024 ESE.

### Core Conceptual Distinctions

- **Virtual Circuit (VC):** Connection-oriented, requires setup, all packets follow the same path, VCI used for forwarding, state held in switches.
- **Datagram (DG):** Connectionless, no setup, each packet routed independently, full destination address in every packet, no per-flow state.

### High-Yield Numerical Facts

- IPv4 header minimum size = **20 bytes**, maximum = **60 bytes**.
- IPv4 address space = **2³² ≈ 4.29 × 10⁹** addresses.
- IPv6 address space = **2¹²⁸ ≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸** addresses.
- TTL field is **8 bits**, decremented at every hop, packet dropped at 0.
- Datagram lookup is **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)**; VC lookup is **exact match on VCI**.

### Real-World Protocol Mapping

| Use VC | Use Datagram |
|---|---|
| ATM | IPv4 |
| X.25 | IPv6 |
| Frame Relay | IPX (legacy) |
| MPLS (label switching) | AppleTalk (legacy) |
| ISDN signalling (D-channel) | UDP, TCP (over IP) |

### Key Engineering Trade-offs (Memorise These Phrases)

- **VC trade-off:** "Fast forwarding + QoS, but slow setup + fragile to failure."
- **Datagram trade-off:** "Robust + scalable, but harder to guarantee QoS."

### Memory Hooks

- **VC** = **V**ery **C**onfigured (a path is configured first).
- **DG** = **D**on't **G**uarantee (no guarantee that packets follow the same path).

### Frequently Asked Sub-Topics in KTU ESE

1. Differences between VC and DG (asked almost every year).
2. Diagram of VC setup phase with VCI table.
3. IPv4 header format and field functions.
4. Numerical question on TTL or header overhead.
5. Routing table lookup with LPM example.
6. Why datagrams are more robust to failures.
7. Comparison of ATM (VC) and IP (DG).

### Final Tip for KTU 2024 Aspirants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Day Strategy:** When asked to "compare VC and Datagram," always structure the answer as a **table with 8–10 rows** followed by **two short paragraphs** summarising the engineering trade-offs. Examiners allocate marks for the table content and the paragraphs separately. A well-structured answer with both components consistently earns **2–3 extra marks** over a paragraph-only answer of identical content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT304 - BASIC CONCEPTS IN COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Routing?

In the context of computer networks, **routing** refers to the process of selecting a path for traffic across one or more networks. The formal academic definition used in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** is as follows:

> **Routing** is the network-layer function that determines the **end-to-end path** that packets (or datagrams) must traverse from a source host to a destination host across a possibly heterogeneous internetwork, and populates the forwarding table of each intermediate router with the corresponding outgoing-interface decisions.

Mathematically, the network can be modeled as a **directed weighted graph** $G = (N, E)$, where:
- $N$ = set of nodes (routers and end-systems)
- $E$ = set of edges (links), each with a cost function $c(i,j) \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$

The goal of a routing algorithm is to compute the **least-cost path** (also called the *shortest path*) between any source–destination pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Terminology (Don't lose marks!)**
> - **Forwarding** = Local, per-packet action of looking up a packet's destination address in a router's forwarding table and sending it out the correct port. Time scale: nanoseconds.
> - **Routing** = Network-wide, control-plane process of building those forwarding tables. Time scale: seconds to minutes.
> Examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between forwarding and routing."* This is a guaranteed **3-mark question** every cycle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The GPS for Packets

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** for the first time. You have two distinct problems:

1. **Strategic (Routing):** Before you leave, you consult a map (or Google Maps) and decide the *overall route* — Kochi → Coimbatore → Bengaluru → Hyderabad → Nagpur → Delhi. This takes minutes and considers traffic, road conditions, and distance.
2. **Tactical (Forwarding):** At *every single intersection*, you glance at the next signboard, decide whether to turn left, right, or go straight, and execute it. This takes a split second and only looks at the *next* step.

- The **routing algorithm** = the map and the route-planning app (runs in the background).
- The **forwarding table** = the mental signboard instructions ("from here, take NH44").
- **Packets** = your car.
